I don’t answer. My instincts are doing their own triage: exit options, distance to the door, how fast I can clear the stairs, whether these new shoes will hold if I sprint.

She tilts her head like she’s listening to something I can’t hear. Then she says, softly, “He’ll be happy.”

“He who?” My voice comes out rough.

She starts up the last steps. I move without deciding to—back, away from the door at the landing, deeper into the stairwell corner. Stupid. Don’t corner yourself.

“Don’t,” she says, and it’s not a warning. It’s almost… bored. “I’m not here for them. Not my lane.”

“What lane is this?” I snap before I can stop myself. “Because I’m just trying to work.”

That gets her attention. Her eyes flicker up to my face, and something in them sharpens, like the moment a camera focuses.

“Work,” she repeats, tasting it. “Yes. You do a lot of that.”

The air shifts. It’s subtle. The stairwell smells suddenly like rain on hot pavement. My shoes feel warmer.

She steps close enough that I can see her laces clearly—flat, waxed, threaded through eyelets that look like they were punched by hand. Green like new leaves. Green like traffic lights.

“Don’t wear them for money,” she says, nodding at my feet, like she’s quoting a rule posted somewhere.

“I didn’t—” I start. “I mean. I did. I had to. My shoe—”

She holds up a hand. “You’ll tell yourself whatever you need. I’m not here to argue with your excuses.”

“Then why are you here?”

She smiles again, quick, like a flash of teeth. “To make sure you see me. That’s all.”

Before I can ask what that means, she turns and walks down the stairs. Not hurried. Not sneaking. Just leaving, like the stairwell belongs to her.

My body leans to follow, like a dog tugged by a leash. The shoes want motion. They want the next step. I can feel it—an itch in the soles, an impatience in the laces.

I dig my nails into my palm and force myself to stay.

When her footsteps fade, the stairwell becomes ordinary again: bleach, dust, distant HVAC hum.

I exhale, shaky.

Then my phone buzzes.

DISPATCH: NEW ORDER. PICKUP 6:57. PENALTY IF DECLINED.

Of course.

I go back down, out into the lobby, out into the wet morning. The marble squeak is gone. My feet are silent now, even when I want them loud. People glance at me and then away like I’m a trick of light.

Outside, the rain has turned to a steady sheet. The kind that soaks you through your jacket seams. I pedal hard, and my legs—my legs feel like they belong to someone who hasn’t been hurting for months.

The relief is so immediate it makes me angry.

Because I know what relief like this costs. Nothing comes free in this city. If something is free, you’re the product.

I do three deliveries back-to-back. Each one is too easy. Each time I check my time, I’m ahead by minutes I didn’t earn. The city’s usual friction—crowds, stuck doors, elevators that take forever—just… slides. A light stays green long enough. A security guard looks away at the right moment. A gate that’s always padlocked is open, swinging slightly like someone just passed through.

By 10:20, I should be limping. I’m not.

By noon, I’m giddy in the worst way, like pain has been replaced with something brighter and more dangerous.

At 12:07, I stop at a deli on 8th because my stomach is eating itself. I lean my bike against the window, order a bacon egg and cheese because I’m not a saint, and check my bank app while the guy behind the counter moves like he hates everyone equally. My balance is a joke. The numbers look unreal in a way that makes me want to laugh.

When the sandwich is ready, I take it to the little shelf by the napkins and hot sauce.

And that’s when I see him.

He’s standing at the end of the condiment station like he’s been there the whole time. Mid-thirties, maybe. Clean hoodie, dark jeans, no visible umbrella, but he’s not wet. He’s holding a coffee he didn’t pay for—no lid, no sleeve—like he doesn’t fear spills.

His sneakers are scuffed, the kind of beat-up pair you keep because they’ve molded to your foot perfectly. Gray, with a rip at the side that should have killed them months ago. Somehow they look better for it.

He smiles at me like we’re friends.

“Nice pair,” he says, nodding at my feet.

My stomach drops. I wrap my fingers tighter around my sandwich like it’s a weapon.

“Do I know you?”

He makes a face like I’ve disappointed him. “Ow. Harsh.”

I glance toward the counter. The guy is watching TV mounted above the grill. Two construction workers are arguing about sports. Nobody is paying attention to us. Nobody ever pays attention to the courier unless she’s in the way.

“You were in the stairwell,” I say, keeping my voice low.

He lifts his hands. “Me? No. That was—” He pauses, as if searching for a polite word. “A colleague. She likes drama.”

“You know my name,” I say.

He takes a slow sip of coffee. “Everybody knows your name, Nim. It’s in your profile.”

“It’s not,” I snap. I’ve never put my real name on any app. I use “N. Carter.” I don’t need customers Googling me.

He chuckles. “Okay. Sure.”

The sound makes my skin crawl. Not because it’s creepy. Because it’s too normal. Like this is just how the world works and I’ve been the only one refusing to read the terms.

“What do you want?” I ask.

He leans closer, and I smell something on him—citrus and cold air and, under that, a faint metallic tang like coins.

“I want you to take them off,” he says.

I stare at him. “Why?”

His eyes flick down to my ankles, as if he can see through the fabric to the bruised skin beneath.

“Because you’re bleeding,” he says gently, and that’s the worst part: the gentleness. “Not yet. But soon.”

I shift my weight. The shoes feel snug, supportive, alive. The laces press across the top of my foot like fingers. I realize I haven’t taken them off since the stairwell. Twelve hours. My feet should be itching. Instead, I feel… held.

“I’m fine,” I lie automatically.

He grins wider. “Everybody says that. Everybody means: please don’t look closer.”

I hate how accurate that is.

“Who are you?” I ask.

He considers. “Depends who’s asking.”

“Me,” I say.

His grin softens into something almost real. “Hermes.”

I bark a laugh. It comes out wrong, sharp. “What, like—”

“Like the god,” he says, as if I asked whether the bacon is pork. “Yes.”

I wait for the punchline. For the camera. For Jae jumping out from behind the chips with their little handheld camera and yelling gotcha. But the deli is still just a deli. The guy behind the counter flips a sandwich. The TV shouts a weather report.

“Okay,” I say, because my brain needs something to grab. “If you’re Hermes. The messenger. Whatever. What do you have to do with my shoes?”

Hermes taps his coffee cup against the metal counter. “Those aren’t your shoes.”

“I didn’t say they were.”

“But you’re wearing them,” he says lightly. “And wearing is… possession-adjacent.”

My jaw tightens. “Someone sent them to me.”

“No,” Hermes says, and the word lands with a strange weight. “Someone sent you to them.”

I feel heat under my soles. The shoes don’t like him talking about them like that. Or maybe they love it.

“I’m not doing this,” I say, and reach for the edge of my own certainty. “I’m going back to work.”

Hermes shifts, blocking my path without moving much. It’s like he’s just suddenly there, in the exact space I need.

“Listen,” he says, and the friendliness drops a notch. “You have options. That’s the good news.”

I glare at him. “And the bad news?”

His eyes flick toward the window, the rain-washed street, the people hurrying with heads down.

“The bad news is you’ve already been seen,” he says. “Not just by her.” A tilt of his head, the green-lace woman. “By other people who don’t knock twice. They break doors.”

I swallow. “So what. I give them to you?”

Hermes spreads his hands. “You bring them to me. Tonight. Midnight. South end of the Broadway Bridge. Under the third lamp.” His smile returns, thin. “Classic. Romantic.”

I snort. “That’s not romantic.”

“It is for me,” he says, and there’s a flicker of something behind his eyes—loneliness maybe, or hunger. “Do that, and I make the looking stop. I’ll redirect attention. I’ll—” He snaps his fingers. “Lose the thread.”

“You can do that?” I ask, hating how hopeful I sound.

“I can do a lot,” Hermes says. “But not for free.”

I stare at him. “There it is.”

He nods, pleased. “There it is.”

“What do you want?” I ask again, softer now, because I can feel my heart starting to race and I don’t want the deli to notice.

Hermes glances down at my shoes, and his voice drops like we’re sharing gossip.

“I want the city moving,” he says. “I want the flows uninterrupted. Packages delivered. Messages sent. Bodies in motion. Because when things stop…” He shrugs, casual, but the shrug doesn’t reach his eyes. “We fade. We become stories people used to tell.”

“We,” I repeat.

Hermes smiles. “You’re quick.”

I take a half-step back. The shoes make no sound. It feels like disappearing.

“You’re not answering me,” I say. “Why me?”

Hermes looks at me for a long second. The deli noise blurs. The rain on the window looks like it’s sliding in slow motion.

“Because you run,” he says simply. “And because you don’t know how to stop.”

Anger flares, hot and immediate. “I stop all the time.”

Hermes lifts his brows. “Do you?”

The words land, and my chest tightens. I think of the stairwell. The green laces. The way my body leaned forward.

Hermes steps aside, like he’s done blocking me. “Midnight,” he says again. “Bridge. Third lamp. Wear them or don’t. But choose.”

He turns away, and for a second, I swear he blurs at the edges, like a heat shimmer. Then he’s just a man walking out into the rain without an umbrella, coffee in hand, blending into the stream of people.

I stand there gripping my sandwich until the paper tears a little.

My phone buzzes.

DISPATCH: HIGH PRIORITY. PICKUP NOW. DOUBLE BONUS.

My foot shifts, ready to move.

And I realize, with a cold clarity that cuts through the leftover giddiness: the shoes aren’t just making me faster.

They’re teaching the city to open for me.

Outside, across the street, someone in a dark raincoat pauses under the deli awning and looks directly at my feet.

Not my face. My feet.

Their gaze lingers like a hand.

Then, as if they’ve confirmed something, they raise their phone and take a picture.

I turn to go, heartbeat loud in my ears, and the shoes carry me out before I can decide whether I’m running toward the bridge at midnight—

or away from it.
step_outputs.8:
### Chapter 3: Deadstock

By the time my shift ends, my legs are vibrating like they’re full of bees.

Not with fatigue. With unused energy. Like I’ve been holding my breath all day and only just realized it.

I should go home. Take the shoes off. Check my feet, check the bruises, check for the tread marks that keep ghosting my skin. Call Jae back—I’ve ignored three texts and one missed call. Lie. Tell the truth. Either one will hurt.

Instead I do what I always do when something’s wrong: I move. I make another errand. I make another loop. If I keep moving, nothing can catch me.

That’s the logic that got me into a life where my body is paid by the minute.

At 6:40 p.m., I lock my bike outside a storefront on 14th that looks like any other resale place if you don’t know the difference: clean windows, curated pairs perched on acrylic risers, a ring light glowing in the back for product photos. A sign in minimalist font: R E Y E S   A U T H E N T I C.

I know it because I used to come here when I was nineteen and stupid and convinced a pair of shoes could change the shape of a life. Dorian gave me my first real courier hookup. He also taught me how to spot fakes, which is to say: how to be suspicious of everything, even beauty.

The bell above the door rings when I enter. It’s bright inside, warm, smelling faintly of leather conditioner and citrus cleaner. Everything is too neat. Even the air feels arranged.

Dorian looks up from behind the counter like he’s been expecting me.

He’s early thirties, Dominican, hair cropped close, beard sharp enough to draw a line with. He wears a black tee and dark jeans and sneakers so clean they look untouched by weather. He looks like success without any joy.

“Nim,” he says, and his voice is smooth. Familiar. “Long time.”

“Yeah,” I say, and try to keep my eyes off the wall of shoes because I don’t want my feet to start craving. “You got a minute?”

Dorian’s gaze drops, immediately, to my shoes.

The room changes around that look. Not magically. Socially. Like in a high school cafeteria when the popular kid focuses on your table.

His expression doesn’t shift much, but I see something in his eyes—recognition and fear, almost the same thing.

“Where,” he says quietly, “did you get those?”

My stomach tightens. “They were a delivery.”

Dorian’s hands move automatically, pulling a microfiber cloth from under the counter and wiping an invisible speck from the glass. He always does something with his hands when he’s thinking. Organizing as coping.

“Nobody delivers those,” he says. “Those deliver you.”

I hear Hermes in that sentence, and my skin prickles.

“I’m not here for riddles,” I say. “I’m here because—” Because I’m scared. Because someone took a picture of my feet. Because a god told me to meet him under a bridge like we’re in a fairy tale. Because I can’t feel my pain and that’s somehow worse. “Because people are looking.”

Dorian’s mouth tightens. “Who talked to you?”

“Does it matter?”

“It matters,” he says, and now there’s something like anger in his voice. Not at me. At the universe. “Did Hermes talk to you?”

My throat closes. I don’t want to give him that. Answers are leverage. But Dorian already knows too much; it shows in how he’s standing—feet planted, shoulders open, like he’s ready for a negotiation.

“Maybe,” I say.

Dorian exhales through his nose. “Of course.”

“You know him,” I say, and it comes out accusatory.

Dorian’s smile appears for a second and vanishes. “I know of him. I’ve… done business.” He taps the counter twice with his fingernail, a small tic. “This city runs on messengers, Nim. Some of them have more… reach.”

“And the girl with the green laces?” I ask before I can stop myself.

His eyes flick up hard. “You saw her?”

I feel my own face go still. “Who is she?”

Dorian shakes his head once, sharp, like he’s throwing the thought away. “Doesn’t matter. Not tonight.”

It does matter. It matters in the way that warning mattered. But Dorian is already moving.

“Take them off,” he says.

I laugh, because it’s the same line Hermes used, and somehow that makes it worse. “No.”

Dorian leans forward over the counter, voice low. “Nim. I’m not trying to control you. I’m trying to keep you alive.”

“Alive?” I repeat. “I’m not dead.”

Dorian’s gaze drifts, involuntarily, to my ankles again. “Not yet.”

I swallow. “What do you want?”

His face softens a fraction. “I want you to let me help. Give them to me. Just for now. I can store them. I have—” He hesitates, like the word tastes bad. “Facilities.”

“Like a vault,” I say, and something in me rises, old resentment. Dorian always did like to own the solution.

“Like somewhere safe,” he corrects. “And then we figure out who’s coming and what they want.”

“And how much it’ll sell for,” I say.

His eyes flash. “This isn’t about money.”

“Everything is about money,” I shoot back. “Even when it’s about gods.”

Dorian flinches like I slapped him, and I regret it immediately because it’s not fair. Dorian isn’t a cartoon villain twirling a mustache over a pile of sneakers. He used to split his lunch with me when I was broke. He used to let kids hang out in here just to be warm.

But he’s also the kind of person who keeps favors alive until they’re debt.

He steps back, visibly choosing calm. “Okay,” he says, too controlled. “Let me show you something.”

He gestures toward a door at the back of the store. It says EMPLOYEES ONLY in plain black letters.

“I’m not going—”

“Nim,” he says, and my name in his mouth is a hook. “Please.”

I follow him because part of me still believes in the version of Dorian who once fixed my bike chain for free.

The back room isn’t huge, but it’s precise: shelves of boxed shoes labeled with neat handwriting, a workbench with an authentication station—UV light, magnifying glass, a scale. A wall-mounted safe with a keypad. The air is cooler. Smells like cardboard and glue and something sour underneath, like old sweat.

Dorian crosses to a shelf and pulls down a shoebox, older, worn at the corners. He sets it on the bench like it’s fragile.

“Look,” he says.

I lift the lid.

Inside is a pair of sneakers that were once white, now the color of bone. The soles are scuffed. The laces are missing. The shoes look… lived in. Loved hard. On the tongue, stamped faintly, is a symbol that makes my vision swim for a second—not a brand, not a logo. A shape that feels like it belongs in a museum and a nightmare at the same time.

I blink fast. The symbol resolves into nothing recognizable.

“What are these?” I ask, and my voice is smaller than I want.

Dorian’s face is careful. “Deadstock,” he says.

“That’s not what deadstock means,” I snap automatically. Deadstock means unworn. New old stock. Grails that never touched pavement.

Dorian nods once. “I know what it means.”

He opens a drawer and takes out a small plastic bag. Inside is a toe tag like from a morgue, white paper with black print. A barcode. A name.

My stomach turns. I read it before I can stop myself. MARTINEZ, CELIA. DATE—

I jerk my eyes away.

Dorian watches me, and there’s something raw in his expression. “They were found on a body,” he says quietly. “No ID. No phone. Just those shoes. Clean as if they’d never been worn. But the feet…” He swallows. “The feet were gone.”

Cold spreads through my chest. I glance down at my own shoes. The matte black fabric seems to drink the room’s light. My toes feel suddenly too present.

“You’re lying,” I say, because I need him to be.

Dorian shakes his head. “I wish.”

I step back from the bench. “So you—what, you keep them?”

“I keep things that are dangerous,” he says, and something in his voice edges toward pleading. “Because someone has to. Because the gods don’t care who gets crushed under their stories.”

My phone buzzes in my pocket, and I flinch like it’s a sting. I pull it out.

JAE: where are you
JAE: call me
JAE: nim. seriously.

I don’t answer. My thumb hovers.

Dorian sees the name and softens. “You haven’t told them.”

“It’s not their business,” I say.

“It becomes their business when you get hurt,” he replies.

“Stop,” I say, and my voice cracks. “Stop acting like you’re the only one who cares.”

Dorian’s jaw clenches. “I care. I cared when you were sleeping on your aunt’s couch. I cared when you were riding on bald tires. I cared when you came in here with blood on your sock and told me it was nothing.”

I go still. I hate that he remembers. I hate that he watched.

“I’m fine,” I say automatically.

Dorian’s eyes drop to my shoes again. His gaze lingers too long, like he’s listening through them. “Take them off,” he says, softer now. “Please.”

The laces on my sneakers tighten.

Not visibly, not like a cartoon. Just a subtle pressure across the top of my foot, as if my shoes have a pulse and it’s getting faster.

I swallow hard. “I can’t.”

Dorian’s face shifts. “You mean you won’t.”

“I mean I can’t,” I repeat, and I don’t know if it’s true until I try.

I reach down, fingers fumbling for the knot. It’s a simple bow. It should come apart. I pinch the lace and pull.

The knot doesn’t move.

I pull harder. The lace bites into my fingers like it has texture now, like it’s rougher than fabric. My heart starts pounding. I yank again.

Nothing.

Dorian’s eyes widen a fraction. For the first time, his control cracks.

“They’re on you,” he whispers.

A chill crawls up my spine.

I straighten, breathing fast. “What does that mean?”

Dorian looks at me like I’m standing on the edge of a roof. “It means,” he says, voice low and urgent, “you need to decide who gets to claim you before someone decides for you.”

From the front of the store, the bell above the door rings again.

Dorian’s head snaps toward the sound. He doesn’t move, but his whole body tightens.

“Stay here,” he says.

I don’t listen. I move toward the doorway, because I’ve never been good at staying.

Through the crack, I see a figure in a dark raincoat at the counter. Hood up. Face obscured. They’re holding a phone, angled down.

Not filming the sneakers on the wall.

Filming the floor.

Filming my feet.

My shoes warm, impatient.

And somewhere in the back of my mind, like a notification I can’t swipe away, Hermes’s voice: Midnight. Bridge. Third lamp.

I back away, silently, and for the first time since the stairwell, I feel something heavier than fear.

I feel owned.

### Chapter 4: The Platform Below the Platform

I don’t let Dorian walk me out.

I don’t let him “handle it.” I don’t let him add me to a shelf with labels and locks.

While he’s in the front doing his polite-knife voice—“Can I help you?”—I slip out the back the way I used to when I was nineteen and broke and ashamed and didn’t want to be seen leaving with nothing.

The alley behind Reyes Authentic is narrow and wet, dumpsters sweating in the rain. A cat watches me from a fire escape like I’m the entertainment. I move fast, but it’s not panic-speed. It’s the shoes. They like urgency. They turn it into traction.

My phone buzzes in my pocket, and I ignore it until I’m three blocks away, breath steady, heart loud.

When I finally look, it’s not Dispatch.

Jae.

JAE: I’m coming to find you.
JAE: answer me.

I stop under an awning and stare at the words until my eyes blur. The rain ticks against the metal above my head like impatient fingers.

My thumb hovers over Call.

The shoes warm at the soles, urging me forward. Stop-and-call is friction. Friction is what they’ve been smoothing all day.

I hate them for that, irrationally, like they’re a person who keeps interrupting.

I type: Working late. Sorry. Call you soon.

The lie is small and quick and sits in my stomach like a stone.

Then I move.

I don’t go home. Home has mirrors and silence and the question of taking the shoes off again, of feeling my pain return or realizing it never left and I just stopped hearing it.

I ride until the day tilts into evening. The rain keeps going in that steady, punishing way that makes the city smell like wet concrete and hot brakes. I do two more deliveries without thinking, because muscle memory is a trap. Each one feels like a loop tightening.

By 9:40 p.m. Dispatch sends me a “high priority” pickup at a place I’ve never seen on the map. The pin flickers, then settles near a subway stop I use all the time.

The note reads: STOP RUNNING IN CIRCLES.

I stare at it for a long time, thumb hovering over Decline.

My rating is already shaky from a canceled order earlier. Rent is due in eight days. I tell myself it’s just another weird app glitch, but the words feel personal, like someone is leaning close to my ear.

I accept.

The subway entrance yawns like a mouth. I lock my bike to a pole, slip my bag over my shoulder, and descend into the station.

Down here the air is warmer, thick with bodies and electricity. Everything smells like metal and sweat and damp newspaper. The platform is crowded with commuters and tourists and kids with headphones, all of us pretending we’re alone.

A train roars through, not stopping, its windows filled with flickering ads: luxury watches, streaming shows, political slogans. My reflection stutters in the glass, and for a second I think I see the green laces behind me.

I spin.

Nobody.

My phone vibrates. The pickup pin has moved. It’s now under the platform.

Impossible. The map shows a dot beneath the tracks, pulsing patiently.

I step away from the crowd, toward the far end where the lights are dimmer and the tiled wall is stained with something that might be rust or old water. There’s a service door there, painted the same color as the wall, like it’s trying to be invisible.

The shoes pull.

I stop in front of the door and listen. On the other side: nothing. Not silence exactly. More like… distance. Like the sound has been turned down.

A security camera mounted above the platform should be watching me. When I glance up, the little red light on it blinks once and goes dark.

My mouth goes dry.

I put my hand on the door handle.

It turns easily.

Beyond is a stairwell going down. Concrete steps. No signage. No smell of piss like you’d expect. The air is clean, almost cold.

I hesitate, and in that hesitation, I feel my body try to argue with itself. Every sensible part of me says: don’t go down random stairs underground at night. Every part shaped by gig work says: follow the pin, get paid, don’t ask questions.

The shoes don’t say anything. They just press, warm and steady, like a hand at my lower back.

I go down.

The station noise fades with each step. By the time I reach the bottom, it’s like the city has been shut off behind a door.

I arrive at another platform.

It looks like the subway platform I just left, but wrong. The tiles are the same, but older, cracked. The fluorescent lights flicker with a slower rhythm. The ads along the wall are not ads. They’re blank panels that shimmer as if something is trying to form in them.

There are people standing here too—waiting, spaced out, patient in the dead-eyed way of commuters—but the air around them feels thin. Their clothes are outdated or too formal or mismatched. A woman in a church hat. A man in a suit with wide lapels. A kid in a hoodie that says CLASS OF 2009.

Nobody is talking.

Nobody is looking at their phones.

My heart hammers. I take a step forward and my sneakers make no sound.

On the track, far down the tunnel, a light appears. Not the harsh white of an approaching train. A dim, bluish glow like foxfire.

The blank ad panels along the wall flare briefly, and I see images in them—so fast I’m not sure I’m seeing at all.

A hospital corridor. My mother’s hands, chapped, folding laundry. Jae’s face lit by their laptop at 2 a.m., eyes tired.

Then another image, sharper, crueler: my own feet, bare, standing on cold tile, toes bruised, skin marked with tread patterns like fingerprints.

I flinch and the panel goes blank again.

“Don’t stare,” a voice says beside me.

I jerk sideways, almost tripping.

She stands there like she’s always been at my shoulder: a woman in a dark coat, hair pulled back. Not beautiful in a glossy way. Beautiful like a knife is beautiful: purposeful. She holds a ring of keys on a chain at her waist, and the chain makes a soft bell sound when she shifts.

Her eyes are calm. Deep water.

“Who are you?” I whisper, because my voice feels too loud here.

She studies me. Her gaze goes to my shoes, then to my face, and it feels like being weighed.

“Persephone,” she says.

I laugh, a short desperate sound. “No. No, you’re—”

“I am,” she says, not unkindly. “And you’re trespassing.”

My fingers tighten on my bag strap. “The pin—my job—”

“You’re not working,” Persephone says. “You’re being moved.”

Anger flashes. “I can move myself.”

She looks at me for a long moment. “Can you?”

The question hits too close, and I hate that she knows exactly where to press.

The train-light in the tunnel grows brighter, closer. The people on the platform shift subtly, like grass bending toward wind. No one steps over the yellow line because the yellow line here looks like it’s made of ash.

“What is this place?” I ask.

“A platform,” Persephone says, as if that’s obvious. “Below the platform. You have many names for it. None of them are correct.”

“The underworld,” I say, and my mouth tastes like metal.

Persephone nods once. “A door. Not a destination. Most of you don’t understand the difference.”

The glow reaches the station, and what arrives is not a subway train so much as the idea of one: a long dark shape with windows that show nothing but a moving blur. It exhales cold air as it stops.

The doors open without sound.

The waiting people begin to board in orderly silence. No pushing. No impatience. They move like they’ve had all the time in the world and it didn’t help.

My stomach twists. “Are they—”

“Dead,” Persephone says. She says it plainly, not dramatic. “Or close enough.”

I stare at them and feel a sudden, sharp ache of recognition. Not of individuals—of posture. Of the way grief sits in shoulders. Of that look of quiet surrender you see on late-night trains when everyone’s tired of being alive in public.

“What do you want from me?” I ask, voice shaking.

Persephone’s gaze drops to my shoes again. “Not what I want,” she corrects. “What the shoes want. What they allow.”

I look down. The matte black fabric is darker here, like it’s absorbing the blue glow. The laces feel snug, almost comforting. My feet are warm, even though the air is cold.

“You’re wearing passage,” Persephone says. “Do you know what passage costs?”

“I paid,” I say automatically. “I—” I think of my old shoes splitting, of the relief flooding my legs. “I didn’t steal them.”

Persephone’s expression softens, a fraction. “You did what you had to. So do they.”

“That’s not an answer,” I snap, and my anger is ugly now, raw. “What do they take?”

Persephone shifts her weight, keys chiming softly. “Small things,” she says. “At first. So you won’t notice. A smell. A detail. The exact shade of someone’s eyes. The way a voice breaks on certain words.”

My throat tightens. I think of my mother laughing—how it sounds, how it used to sound. I try to summon it precisely, and a blank spot opens in my mind, like a file that won’t load.

Panic rises, hot and immediate. “No,” I whisper. “No, I remember—”

But when I chase it, it slips away like water.

My eyes burn. I blink fast, furious at myself for almost crying on a dead platform.

Persephone watches me with something like sympathy. “Every crossing leaves a mark,” she says. “You can’t run through thresholds without paying the boundary.”

“I didn’t ask to cross,” I say.

“You put them on,” Persephone replies, and there’s no judgment, only fact.

I look at the train doors, still open. The blue light spills out, painting the platform in cold color. I feel something in my chest tug, hard, toward it. A pull like gravity.

For one horrible second, I want to step in. I want to see if the underworld has my missing memories lined up like lost-and-found. I want to see if it has someone I miss so badly my ribs ache.

Persephone’s hand touches my wrist. Light contact. Heavy meaning.

“You can visit,” she says quietly. “You cannot stay.”

I swallow. “I don’t want to stay.”

Her eyes hold mine. “Not today,” she says, and it’s not a threat. It’s a knowledge.

The train begins to close its doors. The blue light narrows. The last of the dead step inside without looking back.

As the doors seal, the blank panels along the wall shimmer again, and for a heartbeat I see Jae’s face—worried, furious, alive.

“Go,” Persephone says, releasing my wrist.

“How?” I ask, voice thin.

She nods toward a service stairwell at the far end of the platform, where a door stands open onto darkness.

“You already know how,” she says. “You do it every day. You take the stairs.”

I hesitate. “Are you… helping me?”

Persephone looks away, toward the sealed train now dissolving into tunnel dark. “I’m keeping the boundary,” she says. “If you break it, you won’t be the only one who pays.”

I want to ask about Hermes, about Nike, about Dorian’s deadstock and the girl with the green laces. But the platform is thinning, like a dream that doesn’t want to be remembered.

I turn and run up the stairs.

Halfway up, my phone buzzes with a new notification.

DELIVERY COMPLETE. PAYMENT PROCESSED.

No amount listed.

At the top, I push through the service door and stumble back onto the living platform, into noise and warmth and the smell of cheap cologne and fries. Commuters jostle past me, annoyed at my existence.

My heart is racing. My legs feel too light.

I check my bank app with shaking fingers.

My balance hasn’t changed.

I stare until my eyes blur.

So that was the payment.

Not money.

Me.

I climb the station stairs to street level, and rain hits my face like a slap. The city is still here, loud and indifferent. Cars hiss through puddles. A siren wails somewhere distant.

I look down at my shoes.

For the first time since I put them on, I don’t feel gratitude. I feel a kind of quiet fury, the slow heat of a person realizing they’ve signed something they didn’t read.

Across the street, in the reflection of a dark storefront window, I see a figure standing under an awning, watching.

Not filming.

Just watching.

Hermes, hands in his pockets, smiling like this is all on schedule.

He raises two fingers in a casual salute and mouths a word I can’t hear.

Midnight.

I turn away from him and start walking, because if I start running now, I’m not sure I’ll ever stop.

### Chapter 5: Nike Unbranded

I don’t go to the bridge.

Not right away.

I go home long enough to change my socks and stare at myself in the bathroom mirror like I’m waiting for my face to confess something. The tread marks are there when I peel the sneakers off—faint, like bruises shaped into patterns. They don’t hurt. That’s almost worse. Proof without punishment.

When I try to loosen the laces, they give a little, just enough to let my feet breathe, but the knot still won’t fully come undone. Like the shoes will tolerate comfort but not escape.

My roommate is asleep. The apartment smells like reheated noodles and damp towels. I step over a pile of Jae’s old zines they left here last week and didn’t pick up. My phone is face-down on the table. I don’t flip it over. I already know what I’ll see: missed calls. Worry that curdles into anger.

I sit on the edge of my bed and flex my toes. Pain doesn’t come back the way it should. It hovers at the edges, muffled. My ankle feels like a mouth held shut.

I remember Persephone’s calm voice: small things, at first.

I close my eyes and try to summon my mother’s laugh again, just to prove I can. I can get the outline, the general shape, but not the exact sound. It’s like trying to remember a song from childhood and realizing you only know the chorus.

A tightness builds behind my eyes. I swallow it down.

At 11:14, my phone buzzes. One new message, unknown number.

STADIUM’S CLOSED. THAT’S WHY IT’S QUIET.

I stare at it until my skin prickles. I don’t know any stadium. I haven’t been to one in years. I haven’t run on a real track since high school—since my coach told me I had talent and my body believed him, before the tendonitis and the grocery-store job and the way adulthood teaches you to settle for less pain instead of more joy.

Another text arrives.

YOU WANT ANSWERS? GO WHERE YOU USED TO WIN.

A memory surfaces: a rooftop track behind a rec center on the west side, chained up most of the time. When I was seventeen I used to climb the fence after hours with my teammate and run laps under the city lights until our lungs hurt and we felt holy.

I should ignore it. I should go to the bridge and hand the shoes over to Hermes like he asked, because that would be simple. It would be transactional. It would be the kind of problem I know how to solve.

But something in me—pride, maybe, or the stubborn little piece of me that resents being managed—won’t.

At 11:32, I’m back on my bike, hoodie pulled up against the rain, sneakers on my feet like a decision I’m too tired to argue with. The city at night is all glare and puddles. Headlights stretch into lines on the asphalt. My breath fogs in the cold air.

The rec center is locked, the front gates chained. The basketball court is empty, glossy with rain. I circle around back, find the old fence with the bent section where people used to hop. My hands slip on wet metal as I climb.

The rooftop track is there, hidden above the world like a secret. A loop of red rubber, faded now, edges cracked, weeds poking through where nobody’s bothered to patch. The city sprawls around it, windows glowing, cars moving like blood through veins.

And standing in the middle of the track, barefoot despite the cold rain, is a woman.

She’s not dressed like a goddess. She’s dressed like someone who showed up to run: dark athletic shorts, a thin jacket, hair pulled back. Her skin is beaded with rain. Her posture is coiled, precise, like a spring.

She turns as I step onto the rubber and her eyes land on my feet immediately.

“Finally,” she says.

Her voice is sharp, clear, carrying easily over the wind.

I stop on the edge of the track, heart thudding. “Who are you?”

She looks up at my face like it’s the less important part of me. “You know.”

“I don’t,” I say, and my throat feels dry despite the rain.

She smiles, and it’s not friendly. It’s delighted in a way that makes my stomach flip. “Say it,” she orders. “You’ve been saying everyone else’s names like they’re passwords. Say mine.”

I swallow. I don’t want to. Names have weight. Persephone felt like a boundary. Hermes felt like a hook. This woman feels like… pressure. Like the moment before the starting pistol.

“Nike,” I say.

The word lands, and the air around us changes. Not a lightning-bolt change. A physical one. My lungs feel fuller. The rain seems to thin. My pulse steadies, like my body has fallen into someone else’s rhythm.

Nike nods once, satisfied. “Good.”

I don’t move closer. I keep space between us like it’s safety. “Why did you text me?”

Nike laughs softly. “Hermes would have had you under his lamp by now if I didn’t interfere.”

“You know Hermes,” I say.

“I know a thief,” she replies. “He stole my name and sold it by the yard.”

Anger rises hot in me, not mine and also mine. I think of storefronts and swooshes and the way people worship scarcity like it’s religion. I think of my own teenage self saving for months to buy shoes because I thought they’d make me faster, better, worth more.

“Those shoes,” I say, nodding at my feet. “Are they yours?”

Nike’s gaze flicks down, and for the first time, I see something like grief on her face. It’s gone in a blink, replaced by hard focus.

“They were,” she says. “Before they became a product. Before they became a prize in someone else’s game.”

“Dorian said—” I start, then stop. Dorian’s name feels dangerous in my mouth.

Nike’s eyes narrow. “The one who stacks souls in boxes.”

My stomach turns. “He thinks he’s protecting people.”

Nike steps closer. Her bare feet make no sound on the wet rubber. She stops just outside my arm’s reach, and I can feel her presence like heat.

“Protection,” she says, tasting the word like something bitter. “Ownership with a nicer face.”

“You’re not exactly—” I gesture vaguely, frustrated. “You’re not exactly gentle either.”

Nike’s smile cuts. “No.”

She circles me slowly, and my body reacts the way it did when I used to line up at races—nerves sharpening, attention narrowing, everything else dropping away. I hate it. I miss it.

“What do you want?” I ask.

Nike stops behind me. I feel her gaze on the back of my neck. “I want you to stop pretending you don’t want to win,” she says.

I spin to face her. “I don’t—”

Nike’s eyes blaze. “You do,” she says, and the words hit me like a shove. “You want to win against pain. Against your landlord. Against the city. Against the part of you that thinks you’ll always be running for someone else’s schedule.”

My throat tightens. The rain stings my eyes. I blink hard.

“I want my leg to work,” I say, because I can’t hold the rest of it. “I want to stop hurting.”

Nike nods, and her expression softens into something like understanding. “Yes,” she says. “That’s honest.”

Silence stretches between us, filled with rain and distant sirens. The city below keeps moving, indifferent.

Nike lifts a hand and points at my shoes. “Those don’t heal,” she says. “They negotiate. They make deals with your body and call it mercy.”

Persephone’s warning echoes: small things, at first.

“I’m already paying,” I whisper.

Nike’s gaze flicks to my face. “What did they take?”

I try to answer quickly, to stay tough, but my voice catches. “I can’t remember my mother’s laugh.”

Nike’s jaw tightens. For a second, she looks furious in a way that’s almost protective. “That,” she says quietly, “is not victory. That is theft.”

“You’re a goddess of victory,” I say, and there’s bitterness in it. “What do you know about theft?”

Nike laughs once, sharp. “Everything.”

She steps onto the inside lane of the track, bare feet slick with rain, and starts walking the curve. Not fast. Not slow. Measured.

“Victory has always been hungry,” she says. “It eats time, bodies, countries. It has always asked: what will you sacrifice? People blamed me for what they chose to do in my name.”

She stops at the far curve and looks back at me. Rain runs down her face like tears she refuses to acknowledge.

“Then they turned me into encouragement,” she continues, voice flat with contempt. “As if victory is a slogan. As if you can buy it and hang it in your closet.”

My shoes warm, as if reacting to her voice. The laces tighten a fraction, defensive.

Nike notices. Her eyes narrow. “They’re listening,” she says.

I swallow. “What are they?”

Nike comes back toward me, the rain parting around her like she’s slightly out of phase with the weather. She stops close enough that I can see the fine lines at the corners of her eyes, the intensity there, the tiredness.

“They’re passage rights,” she says, using the same phrase Hermes did, but her tone makes it sound like an indictment. “They were made for crossing thresholds. Battlefields. Finish lines. Life and death. They were not made for resale. They were not made for delivery bonuses.”

“I didn’t want them,” I say, though part of me did the moment my pain shut up.

Nike’s gaze is steady. “But you wore them,” she says.

I look down at my feet. The shoes are matte black, unbranded, perfect. They look like nothing special. That’s what makes them terrifying. They could be anyone’s.

“What do you want me to do?” I ask.

Nike’s expression shifts into something colder, more formal. A judge stepping into her role.

“You think this is about choosing a side,” she says. “Hermes or the collector or the underworld. It isn’t.”

She reaches out and, with two fingers, taps the toe of my sneaker. The touch is light, but it sends a shock up my leg, not painful—clarifying. My breath catches.

“This is about how you win,” Nike says. “In this city. In this body.”

The wind shifts, and for a moment the rooftop smells like the old days: rubber and sweat and adrenaline, not rain and fear.

Nike points toward the track, toward the loop.

“Run,” she says.

I stare at her. “Now?”

“Not for speed,” she says, and her voice is almost gentle. “For truth.”

I hesitate. My stomach twists. Running is how I escape. Running is how I avoid deciding.

Nike’s eyes lock on mine. “If you want to keep wearing passage,” she says, “you will be tested. Not by prophecy.” Her mouth curls with contempt at the word. “By thresholds. By the things you step over.”

I swallow. “And if I don’t?”

Nike’s smile is thin. “Then you’ll do what you’ve always done,” she says. “You’ll give the shoes to someone who will tell you what victory means. Hermes will sell you safety. The collector will store you like inventory. Zeus will notice you eventually and remind you what old victory looks like.”

The name Zeus makes the air feel heavier, like thunder behind clouds. I remember the storm-light in chapter-four rain, the way the security camera blinked out. A pressure, distant but inevitable.

Nike takes a step back, giving me space.

“Choose,” she says simply.

My phone buzzes in my pocket like a heartbeat.

UNKNOWN: under the third lamp. don’t be late.

Hermes.

I look from my phone to Nike’s bare feet on the track.

The rain keeps falling. The city keeps moving.

I feel the shoes on my feet like hands.

And for the first time, I wonder if the only way to win is to stop letting anyone else lace me up.
step_outputs.11:
### Chapter 6: Laces Like Contracts

Jae is sitting on my front steps when I get home, hood up, knees pulled to their chest like they’ve been waiting long enough to get angry properly.

Streetlight turns the rain into silver threads. The building’s buzzer panel is busted, so anyone can get in if they want. The fact that Jae is outside anyway feels like a statement: I’m not sneaking into your life. You’re sneaking out of mine.

They look up as I approach, and their face is tight with worry that’s already started curdling into something sharper.

“Where were you?” Jae asks.

I stop a step below them. My shoes are wet. The stairwell light behind me flickers. For a second I think of Persephone’s platform and feel cold.

“Working,” I say.

Jae’s laugh is short and humorless. “No you weren’t.”

I hate that they know. I also hate that I want to be known.

“I don’t have time for this,” I say, and step past them toward the door.

Jae stands fast, blocking me with their body. Not threatening, just stubborn. “You don’t have time for me but you have time to disappear for twelve hours and not answer your phone?”

I clench my jaw. “Jae—”

“Don’t,” they cut in. “Don’t say my name like it’s a warning sign.”

Rain slides off their hood. Their cheeks are flushed from cold or anger. Their hands are empty, which means they didn’t come to help. They came to confront. That’s worse.

I glance down the street. A car passes slow. Headlights sweep over us. For a heartbeat, I’m sure I’ll see a phone lifted, a camera pointed at my feet.

Nothing.

My stomach twists anyway.

“Come upstairs,” I say finally.

Jae follows, silent, the silence loud between us. Inside the apartment, the air is warmer, but the damp clings to my clothes. I kick the door shut with my heel. The sneakers don’t make a sound.

Jae’s gaze drops immediately to my feet.

“What are those?” they ask.

“Shoes,” I say, too quick.

“Nim,” Jae says, voice low. “What is going on?”

I rub my face with my hands. My fingertips smell like wet metal. “It’s… complicated.”

Jae’s eyes narrow. “That’s what people say right before they get scammed.”

A laugh almost escapes me—because yes. Exactly. I’ve been scammed by gods.

I pull my phone out, scroll, show them the unknown texts. Stadium. Bridge. Don’t be late. I don’t show them the underworld pin because I don’t know how to say platform below the platform without sounding like I’m having a breakdown.

Jae reads, expression shifting from anger to concentration. They are at their most alive when there’s something to decode.

“Who is sending these?” they ask.

I hesitate. I think of Hermes’s smile. Persephone’s calm. Nike’s hard honesty.

“I don’t know,” I lie, and the lie tastes like pennies.

The laces tighten.

Not painfully. Just enough that I notice.

I freeze, eyes flicking down.

Jae notices the flicker in my attention. “What?”

“Nothing,” I say automatically.

The pressure increases. A warning. A punishment.

Jae follows my gaze, crouches in front of me before I can stop them, and reaches for my shoe.

“Don’t touch—” I start.

Their fingers brush the lace.

Jae jerks back as if burned. “Jesus—” They stare at their fingertips. “It’s warm.”

My skin prickles. “I told you.”

Jae stands, suddenly wary. “Okay,” they say, voice tight. “Okay. That’s not normal.”

“No,” I agree, and my voice cracks with relief at finally saying something true. “It’s not.”

Jae looks at me like they’re seeing me again, not just my evasions. “Are you in danger?”

I open my mouth. Close it. The honest answer is yes, and I don’t know how big the danger is. The honest answer is I don’t know what’s hunting me and I don’t know what I’ve already paid.

“I need your help,” I say, and the words feel like swallowing glass.

Jae’s face softens for a second—then hardens again. “With what?”

“With getting out,” I say. “With figuring out how to take them off. With—” I gesture helplessly at my feet. “With not being owned.”

Jae exhales slowly. “Okay,” they say. “Okay. Start from the beginning.”

So I tell them enough. The stairwell. The green laces. Hermes in the deli saying his name like it was a punchline. Dorian’s back room. The toe tag. The platform below the platform, but I describe it as “a place under the subway” because I can’t make myself say underworld yet. I tell them about Persephone without using the name. I tell them about the memory slipping.

When I finish, my mouth is dry. Jae is quiet, eyes distant.

Finally they say, “This sounds like a cult.”

“It’s not,” I snap, then immediately regret it. Because it does. And it might be.

Jae picks up their phone, starts scrolling. “Dorian,” they say. “Reyes Authentic. He’s got a warehouse location listed on his business registry. I can pull—”

“No,” I say sharply. “We’re not going to him.”

Jae looks up. “Why?”

Because he wants to put me in a box. Because he’s already got deadstock with toe tags. Because he looked at my shoes like inventory.

“He’s part of it,” I say.

Jae’s mouth twists. “And Hermes isn’t?”

I flinch at the name. Jae catches it. “So you do know who’s texting.”

I scrub a hand through my hair, frustrated. “It doesn’t matter.”

“It matters,” Jae says, echoing Dorian. They lean forward. “If this is real—if there are gods walking around in sneakers—then they’re not texting you because they’re bored. They want something.”

“I know,” I say. “That’s why I need help.”

Jae stares at my shoes again. “Can you take them off now?”

I swallow. “I tried.”

“Try again,” Jae says, firm.

I bend down, fingers fumbling. My hands are clumsy when I’m tired. I grip the lace knot and pull.

For a second, it loosens—just a fraction—and hope flares in my chest like a match.

Then it tightens again, abruptly, snapping back into place. The lace bites into my skin. The knot is perfect, unmovable.

Jae swears under their breath. “Okay,” they say, voice shaking now. Not fear exactly. Anger. “Okay. So we treat it like a lock.”

“And you know locks?” I ask.

Jae points at themselves. “I know how people hide things,” they say. “I know how systems work. I know where rich guys like Dorian put their ‘insurance.’”

My stomach drops. “Jae—”

“No,” Jae cuts in. “You don’t get to vanish and then decide you’re too scared to act. Either you trust me, or you don’t.”

The words hit, and I realize how close we are to breaking for good. I’ve stretched the thread between us thin.

“I trust you,” I say, because I do, even when I don’t deserve to.

Jae nods once, decisive. “Then we go to his warehouse,” they say. “We don’t talk. We don’t negotiate. We get whatever he has that can undo this.”

“And if he catches us?”

Jae’s smile is grim. “Then we’ll know for sure whose side he’s on.”

Two hours later, we’re in a rideshare heading to an industrial strip by the river. I didn’t bring my bike. Too recognizable. Too tied to my normal life. The car smells like air freshener and exhaustion. The driver doesn’t talk.

Outside, the warehouse district is a grid of corrugated metal and chain-link and puddles. Floodlights make the rain look like static. The river is a dark smear beyond.

Jae leads, phone in hand, moving with purpose. They’re wearing boots and a jacket with too many pockets. They look like someone who planned. I look like someone who ran out the door with a god’s shoes on and no plan at all.

We find the warehouse: no sign, just a keypad by a steel door and a security camera above it.

Jae whispers, “Camera.”

I glance up. The red light is on.

My shoes warm.

The camera’s red light blinks once and goes dark.

Jae stares at it, then at my feet. “That’s you,” they whisper.

“It’s not me,” I whisper back. “It’s them.”

We move fast. Jae produces a slim piece of metal from their pocket—something they definitely shouldn’t have. They slide it into the keypad casing, pop a panel loose like they’ve done it before.

“How do you—” I start.

Jae doesn’t look up. “I did a story on eviction defense,” they mutter. “You learn things.”

The door clicks.

Inside, the air is cold and dry, smelling like cardboard and plastic wrap. The lights are off, but there’s a faint glow from exit signs. Rows of shelving stretch into darkness.

We step in, and the steel door shuts behind us with a soft, terrible finality.

Jae’s breath catches. “Okay,” they whisper. “Okay. This is—”

The shoes press against my soles like they’re eager, like they recognize this place. Like the warehouse is a temple.

We move between shelves. Each box is labeled. Each label is neat. Inventory numbers. Sizes. Dates.

Deadstock.

Jae raises their phone flashlight. The beam catches a row of clear plastic bins stacked like coffins. Inside: shoes, wrapped, pristine.

On one bin, a white tag dangles.

Not a barcode label.

A toe tag.

Jae swallows. “Nim…”

My stomach churns. “Don’t read it,” I say.

Too late. Jae’s eyes flick, take it in, and something in their face changes. The moral anger becomes fear.

“We have to get out,” Jae whispers.

“We need the other shoe,” I say, and I don’t know where the certainty comes from. Nike’s voice, maybe. Dorian’s warning. A sense that the shoes are a pair and pairs are binding.

Jae looks at me, incredulous. “The other shoe?”

“The matching one,” I insist. “Or something that binds them. A certificate. A—”

My voice trails off because, at the end of the aisle, a light clicks on.

Footsteps.

Slow, deliberate, approaching.

And a voice—smooth, controlled, familiar—floats toward us through the dark.

“You really don’t know how to stay out of other people’s rooms, do you?” Dorian says.

Jae’s hand grips my sleeve hard.

My shoes warm, impatient, and the laces tighten like they’re preparing to run—whether I want to or not.

### Chapter 7: Victory Costs

Dorian doesn’t come at us fast.

He comes like someone who knows he doesn’t have to.

He steps into the aisle with a flashlight in one hand, the beam sweeping over plastic-wrapped boxes and our faces like a searchlight. Behind him, two men in dark jackets move soundlessly, not quite guards, not quite friends. Their shoes are clean. Too clean for a warehouse.

Dorian stops a few feet away, expression calm enough to be insulting.

“Nim,” he says, like he’s greeting me at the store. “And Jae.” His eyes flick to them. “I didn’t think you were the type.”

Jae’s jaw clenches. “What the hell is this place?”

Dorian’s smile is small. “Inventory.”

“That’s not inventory,” Jae snaps, pointing at the toe tags.

Dorian doesn’t look. He doesn’t have to. “It’s containment,” he says instead, and his voice tightens on the word. “It’s keeping dangerous things from walking back into the world.”

“By putting names on them like corpses?” Jae’s voice cracks. Anger and fear at once.

Dorian’s gaze returns to me, steady. “You shouldn’t have brought them,” he says softly, and there’s something in it like disappointment. “Now they’re exposed.”

“I didn’t,” I say, and my own voice sounds thin in the cavern of shelves. “They came because of me.”

Dorian nods once. “Yes.”

The laces on my sneakers tighten, not painfully, but like a hand closing. My feet feel planted. Ready. Like the shoes have decided this is a starting line.

Jae whispers, “Nim, we need to go.”

“I know,” I whisper back. But my eyes are on Dorian because I need him to answer something.

“Why the toe tags?” I ask.

Dorian’s expression flickers—annoyance, then something like shame. “Names matter,” he says. “If you don’t name a loss, it turns into a rumor. Into nothing.”

“Or into a product,” Nike’s voice says behind us.

My blood turns to ice.

Dorian’s face changes too—his control cracks, just a fraction, like a mask slipping. The two men behind him shift, uneasy.

I turn.

Nike stands at the end of the aisle, barefoot on the concrete, rain still clinging to her hair as if she walked in from the storm without bothering with doors. The fluorescent hum seems to bow around her. Even Dorian’s flashlight beam looks dimmer near her.

“Don’t,” Dorian says, and it’s not to Nike. It’s to me. “Don’t let her—”

Nike laughs, sharp as a cut. “Let me what?” she asks. “Tell her the truth?”

Jae stares, wide-eyed, and I can feel their disbelief fighting with the evidence of their own senses. They grip my sleeve like they’re anchoring themselves to reality through my body.

“Nim,” Jae whispers, voice shaking. “Who is that?”

I swallow. My mouth tastes like metal. “Nike.”

Saying it here, in this warehouse of deadstock and toe tags, makes the air tighten.

Nike’s gaze lands on my feet. The laces twitch.

“You brought her to your shelves,” Nike says to Dorian. “You really thought you could store victory like a shoe size.”

Dorian’s jaw clenches. “I thought I could keep people alive.”

Nike’s eyes blaze. “Alive isn’t the same as owned.”

The tension snaps sideways. One of Dorian’s men steps forward, hand moving toward his jacket. My shoes warm, urgent. The aisle narrows in my vision. Everything becomes route and obstacle and exit.

A crack of thunder rolls outside, low and heavy, like the sky clearing its throat.

Dorian glances up, as if he can see through the ceiling to the storm. His face tightens with something like fear.

“He’s watching,” Dorian mutters.

Nike’s smile is cold. “Yes,” she says. “He noticed.”

The fluorescent lights flicker. For a heartbeat, the warehouse is lit by something else—white-blue, like lightning but not from the fixtures. I smell ozone.

Hermes appears in that flicker like a thought becoming a person. One moment the aisle is empty behind Nike, the next he’s there leaning against a shelf, hands in his pockets, looking infuriatingly relaxed.

“Hi,” Hermes says brightly, as if we’re at a party. His sneakers are dry despite the rain. “Everybody’s here. Great.”

Jae makes a sound in their throat that might be a laugh or a sob.

Dorian’s eyes narrow. “You brought her,” he accuses Hermes.

Hermes puts a hand to his chest. “Me? No,” he says, offended. “I did not. You did, by making a mess. I simply followed the interesting part.”

Nike’s head turns slightly, like she’s listening to something beyond us. The storm outside deepens. The river wind slams rain against the warehouse walls.

“Enough,” Nike says, voice low. “This isn’t about your games. It’s about her feet.”

All three of them—gods and reseller—look at me like I’m an object on a table. Like my body is a site of negotiation.

Something in me snaps. Not loud. Just decisive.

“Stop,” I say.

They all pause.

Jae’s grip on my sleeve tightens. “Nim—”

I turn to them. Their face is pale. Their eyes are furious and scared and so, so alive. They came here for me. They broke their own rules for me. And I dragged them into this.

“I’m sorry,” I say, and the words come out raw. “I shouldn’t have—”

“Later,” Jae whispers, voice shaking. “We need to—”

A sound cuts through us: the warehouse door at the far end slams open.

Wind and rain rush in, cold and violent. The storm has found a way inside like it has a key. Somewhere in the building, metal groans.

The air pressure shifts. My ears pop. The hairs on my arms stand up.

Zeus is not a person walking in. Zeus is the storm deciding the room is his.

Dorian’s men back up instinctively. Hermes’s grin falters for the first time, the charm slipping.

Nike lifts her chin, defiant. Persephone’s calm voice echoes in my memory: if you break it, you won’t be the only one who pays.

The ceiling lights flare and die. Darkness falls, punctuated by the strobing white of lightning through high windows.

In that lightning flash, I see the warehouse shelves differently: not just boxes and bins, but lines—routes, corridors, a labyrinth of stacked desires. I can see the exits like they’re glowing. I can see the thin places, the weak points.

The shoes are humming against my feet like they’re excited. Like they’ve been waiting for a storm.

“Nim,” Hermes says, and his voice is sharper now. “Midnight at the bridge. Third lamp. I told you. You could have made this easy.”

“Easy for who?” I spit back.

Hermes’s eyes flash. “For you,” he says, and there’s real frustration there, almost pleading. “You think you can out-run gods? You can’t. You can only choose which ones you owe.”

Nike steps toward me, gaze steady. “Or you can choose what you step on,” she says.

Dorian’s voice cuts in, urgent. “You give me the shoes, Nim, I can shelter you. I can shelter your friend. I can close this down.”

“You mean lock me up,” I say.

Dorian flinches. “I mean protect you.”

“You mean own me,” I correct, and the words feel like pulling something sharp out of my throat.

Another lightning flash. The air smells like burning dust.

Jae’s hand slips into mine. Their palm is cold and sweaty. “Nim,” they whisper, voice breaking. “I don’t care about the shoes. I care about you. Please.”

The simplicity of it—human, messy, not a bargain—hits me harder than any god.

My shoes tighten, demanding motion. Run. Escape. Save yourself.

And in the tightness, I feel the old habit—the courier reflex. If it’s dangerous, run. If it’s complicated, run. If you don’t want to decide, run.

Victory, Nike had said, is the part where you decide what you’ll step on.

I look at the aisle ahead, at Dorian’s shelves lined with deadstock and toe tags. At Hermes, ready to slip away. At Nike, watching like a judge. At Jae’s face, pleading.

A choice forms in me like a bruise.

“Jae,” I say, voice steadying. “On my count, you go.”

“What?” Jae whispers.

“Run,” I say. “But not with me.”

Jae’s eyes widen. “No—”

“Yes,” I snap. “You get out.”

I turn toward Dorian. “You want to protect people?” I shout over the wind. “Then open the damn door.”

Dorian’s face twists. He looks toward the storm-ripped entrance, then back at me. Lightning flashes. In that light, I see something human in him—fear, yes, but also calculation, and a flicker of care.

“You don’t understand what you’re doing,” Dorian says.

“I do,” I say, and I realize it’s true. “I’m stopping.”

It’s the last thing the shoes want.

I plant my feet on the concrete and hold still.

For a heartbeat, the world seems to lurch, as if motion is the only thing holding it together. The laces bite hard. Pain flares—real, sharp, my old injury roaring back like an insult.

I grit my teeth. Tears sting my eyes, not from rain.

Nike’s gaze sharpens. Hermes goes very still, watching like he can’t believe what he’s seeing.

“Don’t,” Hermes says quietly. “Don’t do that. Don’t make stillness sacred. You’ll break things.”

“Good,” I hiss, and my voice shakes with pain. “Maybe some things should break.”

The shoes vibrate, furious. The tread pattern on my skin burns. I feel something inside my head tug—another memory trying to slip loose as payment for disobedience.

I grab Jae’s hand harder, anchor myself in their warmth.

“Say my name,” I whisper to them, panicked. “Say it.”

Jae looks at me, confused and terrified, then understands. “Nim,” they say, voice fierce. “Imani. Nim. I’m here.”

The pull in my mind eases, like a rope slackening.

Nike smiles, brief and startling. Approval like a blade flash.

Dorian’s face goes pale. “What are you—”

I bend down, fingers trembling, and grab the lace knot again.

This time, because I’m not lying, because I’m not bargaining, because I’m not moving in anyone else’s direction—the knot loosens.

Just a fraction.

Enough.

I yank.

The bow comes undone.

Air hits my foot like freedom and loss at once. Pain surges up my leg, immediate and honest.

I gasp. My vision whites out for a second.

When it clears, I’m holding one lace end in my fist, and the shoe is half off my heel.

Hermes takes a step forward, hand out, quick. “Nim—”

Nike’s voice cuts like a whistle. “No.”

The storm roars, as if amused.

I pull the shoe off completely.

The warehouse tilts. Not physically—my perception. The exits flare bright. The air tastes like pennies. Somewhere far below, I feel the underworld platform like a magnet.

The shoe in my hand is heavier than it should be. Like it’s full of history.

Jae is shouting my name, but their voice is muffled, distant.

I look up at Dorian, at Hermes, at Nike.

“Here’s my victory,” I say, breathless with pain. “Nobody gets to lace me up again.”

And then I do the most dangerous thing I can think of in a room full of gods and men who want to own them.

I throw the shoe.

Not at a person.

At the shelf of toe-tagged deadstock.

The shoe hits plastic. The bin top cracks. A toe tag flutters loose into the storm wind like a white leaf.

The storm surges. The lights flare. The air splits—

—and the warehouse doors slam shut by themselves, sealing us in with the sound of a verdict.

### Chapter 8: Leave Them at the Crossroads

The world comes back in pieces.

Sound first: rain hammering metal, the low groan of the building settling, Jae’s voice ragged from shouting my name.

Then light: emergency exit signs glowing red, a phone flashlight beam jittering across boxes, a lightning flash bleaching everything into negative.

Then pain: my Achilles screaming like it’s been waiting all night to be heard.

I’m on my knees on the warehouse floor, one sneaker off, sock soaked, foot bare on cold concrete. The other shoe is still on, laced, warm, too tight. My removed shoe lies a few feet away near the cracked bin, matte black and somehow immaculate despite what I just did.

Jae is crouched in front of me, hands hovering like they don’t know where to touch.

“Don’t move,” they say, voice breaking. “Don’t—why would you—”

“I had to,” I gasp, and it comes out like a sob. “I had to stop.”

Jae’s eyes are wild. They look past me, scanning the darkness, trying to find the shape of the threat. “Where are they?” they whisper.

Hermes stands in the aisle a little ways off, hands raised like he’s showing he’s harmless, which is the funniest thing he’s ever done. His smile is gone. In the emergency light, he looks tired—old around the eyes in a way he keeps hidden.

Nike is closer, barefoot on the concrete, unmoved by the cold. She looks like she could break the building with a thought. Dorian is near the shelves, face pale, staring at the cracked bin as if he’s watching his life’s work leak out.

The two men with Dorian are gone. Or maybe they were never men in the first place. Maybe they slipped away into the storm when the doors sealed, like shadows looking for a new wall.

A toe tag flutters on the floor near my knee, twisted in the wind that’s still somehow inside the warehouse. I reach for it without thinking.

Jae catches my wrist. “Don’t.”

Their hand is warm. Human. Real.

My fingers shake. “I just—” I swallow. “Names matter.”

Jae’s face tightens. “So do lives,” they say, and then quieter, “So does yours.”

I force myself to breathe through the pain. The removed shoe is a weight on the floor, an invitation and a threat.

Hermes takes a cautious step forward. “Okay,” he says, voice careful, like he’s speaking to a skittish animal. “We can still fix this.”

Nike laughs softly. Not kind.

Hermes glances at her. “Don’t start,” he mutters, then looks back at me. “Nim, you broke containment. You made a mess in someone else’s house. That is… historically a bad move.”

Dorian’s head snaps up. “My house?” he spits. “You brought this to my door.”

Hermes tilts his head. “Did I?” he asks, all innocence. “Or did you build a vault and expect the gods not to notice?”

Dorian’s hands ball into fists. “I was trying to keep people safe.”

Nike’s voice cuts through them both, flat and cold. “You were trying to keep victory,” she says. “And you can’t.”

Dorian’s gaze flicks to me—barefoot, shaking, pain-struck. Something in his face shifts. For a second, he looks less like a businessman and more like a man who remembers being young and scared.

“Nim,” he says, softer. “You don’t understand what you just opened.”

“I understand enough,” I say, and my voice is steadier than my body. “You put toe tags on shoes like you were doing people a favor.”

Dorian flinches. “Names—”

“Names don’t give you permission,” I cut in.

Hermes exhales, impatient now. “This is touching,” he says. “But Zeus is not a metaphor. He’s attention with weather. He’s already here.”

As if on cue, thunder rolls so close the warehouse walls vibrate. Rain slams harder. Somewhere above, a sheet of corrugated metal tears loose with a shriek.

Jae’s eyes widen. “We need to leave,” they say urgently.

I look at the sealed doors. “How?”

Nike steps toward me. The air tightens around her, not magical fireworks—just pressure, like standing too close to a speaker at a concert.

“You made stillness sacred,” she says. Not mocking. Almost… respectful. “Now you have to finish the choice.”

I swallow. “What choice?”

Nike nods at the shoes. One on my foot, one on the floor. “Ownership,” she says. “Or release.”

Hermes makes a frustrated sound. “Release to who?” he demands. “To the first person who finds them? To Dorian’s next buyer? To some kid who thinks a miracle comes without a bill?”

“Everyone thinks that,” I say, and my voice cracks. “Including me.”

My removed shoe feels heavy on the concrete, like it’s anchoring the whole room. My foot aches without it, raw and honest. I can feel my injury again, like a truth.

Jae squeezes my hand. “Nim,” they whisper. “Whatever you do, do it so we get out of here.”

I nod, and look at Nike. “If I give them back,” I say, “you’ll just… what? Take them? Keep them? Hide them?”

Nike’s gaze is steady. “I don’t want them,” she says. “I want my meaning.”

Dorian scoffs softly, but there’s no humor in it. Hermes watches me like his survival depends on my next sentence.

Persephone’s voice comes back to me, calm as deep water: you can visit. you cannot stay.

And then another thought, one I’ve been avoiding because it’s too simple: I can’t fix gods. I can’t fix the city. I can’t fix the fact that people will always chase power and call it need.

But I can choose what I do with what’s on my feet.

I close my eyes for a second and picture my mother’s laugh. Not the exact sound—I can’t. That hole is there, clean-edged, infuriating. But I can picture her mouth, the way her shoulders shook, the way she tried not to laugh and failed anyway.

I open my eyes.

“I’m not giving them to you,” I say to Hermes.

Hermes’s face tightens. “Nim—”

“I’m not giving them to you either,” I say to Dorian.

Dorian’s jaw flexes. “Then to who?” he demands.

I swallow against the pain and the fear and the storm-pressure in the room. “To nobody,” I say.

Nike’s mouth curves, a brief flash of approval.

Hermes looks horrified. “That’s not how it works,” he says.

“It is,” I say, and I don’t know where the certainty comes from except that I’m tired of being moved. “It has to be.”

I reach down to the remaining shoe and tug the laces loose. They resist, biting, as if the shoe is angry. Pain spikes in my Achilles as I shift.

Jae’s other hand comes under my elbow, steadying me without making a big deal of it. They don’t say I told you so. They don’t say hurry. They just hold.

“Say my name,” I whisper, not looking up.

“Nim,” Jae says immediately. “Imani.”

The laces slacken a fraction, like the shoe is forced to acknowledge that I exist outside of it.

I pull again.

The knot gives.

The shoe loosens.

I yank it off, gasping as pain floods back like a wave. It’s so sharp it makes me nauseous. My body, betrayed by comfort, remembers its limits with cruelty.

Both shoes sit in front of me now, matte black, unbranded, wet with rain that isn’t rain.

The warehouse hums, tense.

Hermes takes a step forward, hands out. “Give them to me,” he says, voice stripped of charm. “Please. If you leave them loose, you don’t know what happens.”

“That’s the point,” I say.

Dorian’s voice is raw. “You’re going to kill someone,” he says. “Some kid will find them. Someone will wear them.”

“Maybe,” I say, and my throat tightens. “Or maybe someone will leave them on the shelf. Or maybe they’ll be returned. Or maybe they’ll be stolen. That’s… people. That’s the city.”

Nike’s gaze stays on me, unwavering. “Victory,” she says quietly, “is not control.”

Outside, thunder cracks like a whip. The emergency lights flicker.

I look at Jae. “Can you run?” I ask.

Jae’s mouth trembles. “Yeah,” they say. “Can you?”

I look down at my bare feet, at my wet socks, at my injured leg that’s suddenly very much mine. “Not fast,” I admit.

Jae nods once. “Then we walk,” they say, and there’s a fierce tenderness in it that makes my chest hurt more than my tendon.

Nike steps toward the sealed warehouse door. She doesn’t touch it. She simply looks at it, and the air around the lock seems to shiver, as if the concept of a closed door is being questioned.

The door clicks. Swings inward.

Wind and rain rush in, cold and alive. Streetlight spills across the concrete, turning puddles into mirrors.

Hermes flinches as if the cold air bites him. He looks at Nike, anger and something like respect tangled on his face.

“You’ll regret this,” he mutters.

“Maybe,” Nike says.

Jae helps me up. My leg protests. I hiss through my teeth and put weight on them anyway. Each step is a small, ugly victory: pain endured without shortcut.

We move toward the open door.

Behind us, Hermes’s voice calls, softer now, almost human. “Nim.”

I pause and turn, because I hate myself a little and because I want to understand.

Hermes’s eyes are bright in the emergency light. “If you leave them,” he says, “they’ll be found. They’ll be worn. They’ll keep taking.”

“I know,” I say.

“Then why?” he asks, and for the first time, he sounds genuinely confused.

I look at the shoes on the floor—two black shapes that could be ordinary, that could be ruin. Then I look at Hermes, god of motion, afraid of stillness like it’s death.

“Because I’m not your delivery,” I say. “I’m not Dorian’s inventory. I’m not Nike’s lesson.” My voice shakes. “And if victory is always going to cost something, then I’d rather pay in risk than in my mother’s laugh.”

Hermes’s face tightens, as if I’ve hit the one thing he can’t bargain around. He doesn’t answer.

Jae and I limp out into the storm.

We don’t go far. Just down the block, around the corner, into the cross streets where the city opens up into a four-way intersection—one of those old crossroads where the paint on the crosswalk is worn down by thousands of feet.

I stop there, under a flickering streetlamp, rain pouring down.

Jae looks at me, confused. “What are you doing?”

I take the shoes from my bag—because I did not leave them in Dorian’s warehouse, not like a bomb with a timer. I grabbed them on impulse when we left, the way you grab a hot pan and then realize you’re burning.

They are heavier in my hands now. Or maybe my arms are weaker.

I kneel at the edge of the curb, ignoring the pain, and set the sneakers down side by side on the wet concrete.

A car passes and its headlights sweep over them. For a moment they look like any pair of shoes left behind by someone too drunk, too tired, too broke to care.

Then the light moves on, and they vanish into ordinary darkness.

Jae’s breath hitches. “Nim—”

“I’m leaving them at a crossroads,” I say, and my voice is thin with exhaustion. “Not for money. Not for a god. Just… here. Where choices happen.”

Jae crouches beside me, rain soaking their sleeves. “Someone will take them,” they say.

“Yeah,” I whisper. “Someone will.”

We stand, slowly. My foot screams. I bite down on the inside of my cheek until I taste blood.

Jae puts their arm around my waist, steadying me, and we start to walk home. Not run. Walk.

Behind us, in the rain, a figure steps into the edge of the streetlight—Hermes, watching from a distance, hands in his pockets, sneakers silent on the pavement. He doesn’t come closer. He just looks down at the shoes like they’re a story he can’t fully control.

Further back, half-hidden by the curtain of rain, Nike stands barefoot under the awning of a closed storefront, eyes on the intersection, expression unreadable. She doesn’t smile.

The city keeps moving.

My feet hurt.

And for the first time in a long time, the hurt feels like proof that I’m still here to feel it.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

**Strengths:**

The voice is immediately distinctive and consistent. Nim's interiority feels earned rather than performed—the counting of steps, the self-aware lie ("oil it after shift, meaning I won't"), the grudging pragmatism about the shoes. The courier's relationship to time and money is rendered with specificity that grounds the supernatural elements effectively. The line "forty bucks is groceries and laundry and maybe a roll of athletic tape that isn't from last year" does real character work in a single breath.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the inciting delivery, the mysterious shop, the shoe failure, the swap, the uncanny aftermath. The pacing is well-managed—the mundane is established before the strange intrudes, and the strange intrudes gradually rather than all at once.

The metaphors are mostly earned. "The city is a wet mouth" is a bit of a reach, but "the air smells like burnt coffee and yesterday's fryer oil and the iron tang that comes right before rain" is precise and sensory without being overwrought. "The sole peeling like a mouth" is effective. The stairwell counting ritual ("thirty-two to the landing, thirty-one to the next") pays off the character profile detail naturally.

The shoe-fitting scene is handled with appropriate restraint—the temptation is rendered as human and economic rather than mystical, which is exactly right for this story's register.

**Weaknesses:**

Some metaphors don't quite land. "The city is a wet mouth" is generic urban-grit imagery. "The air sits in the right places" after putting on the shoes is vague where it should be specific. "The building itself is humming" is a cliché of uncanny-building writing.

The mysterious shop owner is somewhat underdeveloped—his dialogue is functional but not particularly distinctive. The line "You ever think about where things go before they get to you?" is a bit on-the-nose as a thematic statement, too neatly telegraphing the story's concerns.

The ending with the green laces is effective as a hook, but the "traitor, animal, courier" construction feels slightly forced—the three-word rhythm is a bit too self-consciously literary for the voice established elsewhere.

The dialogue is sparse but functional. The shop owner's lines are serviceable without being memorable. The protagonist's dialogue ("Love an invisible job") is appropriately dry.

The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the middle section (the building, the service stairs) could be tightened without losing anything essential.

**Overall:** This is accomplished work that successfully establishes voice, world, and character while executing the chapter plan with fidelity. The supernatural elements are introduced with appropriate restraint. The main weaknesses are occasional metaphorical overreach and a shop owner who functions more as a plot mechanism than a character.
