### Chapter 2 — What the Water Remembers

By the time I got back to the institute the fish smell had crawled into my hair and set up a little apartment behind my sinuses.

Denise met me at the lab door like a conspirator, eyes wide, one hand still on her phone as if it might bite. “I didn’t send it to anyone,” she said before I could ask. “Just you.”

“Good,” I said, and then hated the way it sounded. Good, like hoarding an emergency.

She led me past the wet lab and into the equipment room where the servers lived. The institute’s “hydrophone array” was really a handful of underwater microphones bolted to pilings and buoy lines around the bay—funded in pieces over years, because nobody gives you a big, beautiful grant to listen for things that might happen.

Eli was already there, hunched over his laptop with a pair of headphones clamped on like earmuffs. He didn’t look up when we came in. His foot bounced against the metal leg of his chair in a steady, self-soothing tempo.

“Eli,” I said.

He held up one finger—wait—and did something with the trackpad. Then he pulled the headphones off and looked at me with the strained, bright expression of someone who’s been staring at waveforms too long.

“It’s not whales,” he said, as if that was the first item on a checklist.

“Okay,” I said. “Play it.”

He hit space.

At first it was ocean—always a relief, always a disappointment. The constant shush of water over pebbles, distant boat engines, a few pops and clicks that could have been shrimp or static. Then, underneath, a low sound like a freight train a county away.

A hum.

It filled the room in a way sound shouldn’t when it’s coming from laptop speakers. Not loud, exactly. Present. Pressure more than volume. My molars vibrated faintly.

Eli paused it. “It’s been there on and off for days,” he said. “But last night—around two—”

He scrubbed to a point in the track. The hum returned, and then changed.

Not a dramatic change. Not a melody. The frequency tightened, loosened, tightened again, in a pattern that made my skin prickle. Like something testing the edges of a box.

“Instrument artifact?” I heard myself ask, because denial loves paperwork.

“I checked,” Eli said. “Cross-referenced all three stations. Same modulation. Same timing. And look.” He turned the laptop so I could see the spectrogram: a smear of color with darker bands where the hum lived. The bands stepped. Repeated. Varied. Like little ladders.

Denise hugged her arms around herself. “It sounds like—” she began.

“Don’t,” I said too sharply. Her face fell. I softened my voice. “Don’t make it into a thing yet.”

Eli gave me a look that was half apology, half accusation. “It’s already a thing,” he said.

I took a breath through my mouth. The institute’s air was filtered, but you could still taste the sea, and now, through the open loading door, the rot from South Beach.

“All right,” I said. “Copy the raw files. Lock them. No edits until we’ve got duplicates.”

Eli blinked. “You think someone’s going to—”

“I think we’re going to make mistakes,” I said. “And I want to know what’s ours and what’s noise.”

Denise’s phone buzzed. She glanced down. “South Beach is worse,” she said. “They’re saying it’s all along the south spit now. And—” She swallowed. “And up near the river mouth.”

Fish don’t die politely in one place and then stop. They die the way water moves: everywhere it can reach.

I spent the next four hours doing things that can be measured. It’s a comfort, the way numbers don’t ask you to be brave.

We set up a table in the wet lab with bins of ice and fish laid out like evidence. I took gill snips and liver samples, labeled them, double-labeled them, and put them into vials that went into a freezer that hummed with a different, innocent kind of vibration. I ran dissolved oxygen readings from water samples Denise had drawn at different sites. I checked pH. I checked temperature. I checked salinity.

Nothing screamed toxin. Nothing screamed anoxic event. The oxygen was lower than seasonal average, but not low enough to kill whole schools in a night. The pH oscillated slightly—tiny regular swings that could have been instrument drift except it matched across sites, like Eli had said.

It was the kind of data that would get you a shrug in a committee meeting and a sinking feeling in your gut.

Dr. Rook came in around noon, wearing a field jacket that had never seen a torn nail. He took in the wet lab in one sweep—the bins, the fish, the smell—with the expression of a man already composing his summary for someone important.

“Mara,” he said, as if my name were the beginning of a presentation. “Update.”

“Die-off is widespread,” I said. “Multiple species. Gill damage. No clear toxin signature yet. Oxygen lower but not catastrophic.”

He nodded, eyes on the fish. “And the hydrophones?”

I hesitated. I shouldn’t have. It was just a fraction of a second, but Rook lived in the cracks between people’s sentences.

“You heard,” I said.

He smiled, thin. “Denise heard,” he corrected gently, which was worse. “And then I heard. Tell me you’re not making copies to keep in your pocket.”

I felt heat rise up my neck. “We’re duplicating raw files for integrity.”

“Good,” he said. “Because there will be inquiries. And because I have a call with the state environmental office at fourteen hundred.” He checked his watch, wiping a spot of imaginary salt off the face with his thumb. “Also: do not speak to the press. I mean it, Mara.”

“Press?” I echoed.

He tilted his head toward the front lot. A news van sat there with its satellite dish up like an antennaed insect. Jessa Marr stood next to it in a bright red rain jacket, talking animatedly to a cameraman while pointing at the sky as if she could shame it into leaving.

“Jessa,” I said, and the word came out like a groan.

Rook’s mouth quirked. “She called twice. I didn’t answer.”

Of course he didn’t. He liked other people doing his dirty work.

“I’ll handle her,” I said, because I was still stupid enough to think I could handle anything.

Outside, the air was a different kind of rotten—hot asphalt and fish.

Jessa spotted me immediately. She always did. She was one of those people who made eye contact like it was a hook.

“Mara Venn,” she called, voice bright. “Our favorite mermaid mechanic. Come save us from speculation.”

“I’m not talking on camera,” I said as I approached.

“Off camera,” she said smoothly, and gestured to the side of the van where the cameraman couldn’t hear. “I’m not here to make you a villain. I’m here because people are making up their own reasons and they’re ugly.”

“What are they saying?” I asked despite myself.

Jessa’s smile faltered, a real crack. “They’re saying it’s punishment. They’re saying the institute caused it with your ‘experiments.’ They’re saying the megastructures are… feeding.” She looked out toward the bay and lowered her voice. “And people are starting to stockpile.”

“What,” I said, “stockpile fish?”

Jessa snorted. “Gas. Rice. Ammunition. The good old apocalypse starter kit.” She leaned in. “And they’re saying Noah Kade’s boat came in with dead catch yesterday.”

My stomach tightened. “Noah doesn’t—”

“Don’t defend him,” she said, not unkindly. “I’m not accusing. I’m asking: do you have anything real I can give them? Something that isn’t conspiracy-shaped.”

I stared at her. Jessa could be ruthless, but she wasn’t stupid. She knew what panic did in a town that lived paycheck to paycheck and tide to tide.

“I have data,” I said carefully. “I don’t have a cause.”

Jessa’s eyes narrowed. “And the hum?”

So she’d heard it too. Or someone had told her. The institute was a sieve; secrets leaked through human mouths faster than through email.

“I don’t know what the hum is,” I said.

“That’s not what I asked,” she said.

I felt a flare of anger—at her, at Rook, at the sky. “Jessa,” I said, “people are hauling dead fish off beaches with shovels. My freezer is full of liver samples. I’m not gatekeeping a bedtime story.”

She held my gaze. “Okay,” she said softly. “But if you don’t say something, someone else will. And they’ll say it with more confidence than you.”

“I’ll tell you this,” I said after a beat. “We’re not seeing evidence of an oil spill or a simple algal bloom. It’s not that. That’s all I can responsibly say.”

She nodded once, storing it. “Good.” Then, more quietly: “Mara. You look like you haven’t slept.”

“I slept,” I lied.

Jessa’s attention flicked to the sky. The megastructure’s faint lines were visible in the afternoon glare, like scratches on glass. “Do you ever get the feeling,” she said, “that it’s listening?”

“No,” I said too fast, and she raised an eyebrow, because she’d caught my tell the way Noah did.

Back inside, Eli was waiting near the equipment room door like he’d been afraid to leave it alone.

“I did a thing,” he said.

My heart sank. “Eli.”

“I know, I know,” he said, words tumbling. “You said no edits. I made copies. I didn’t overwrite anything. I just—look.”

He handed me a printout. A spectrogram with his notes in the margins, scribbled in a cramped hand. He’d drawn vertical lines at repeating intervals. Beneath them he’d written: phrase? phrase? phrase?

And under that, almost embarrassed: sounds like call-and-response.

“What did you do?” I asked, voice low.

“I compared it to the sonar ping log,” he said. “The hum shifts right after the institute’s scheduled calibration pings. Not every time, but enough that—Mara, it might be reacting.”

A cold, clean fear moved through me. Reaction implied perception. Perception implied intention. Intention was the beginning of the word everyone was avoiding.

Outside, somewhere down the hall, Rook was on the phone saying “stakeholders” and “mitigation.”

Eli swallowed. “Do you want to hear the weirdest part?”

“What,” I said, already knowing I didn’t.

He hit play again.

The hum. The steps. Then, faintly, a higher frequency thread, like something trying to climb out of the low sound into a space we could understand.

It lasted maybe two seconds. If I hadn’t been listening for it, I would have missed it entirely.

It sounded—God help me—like a question mark. Not in meaning. In shape.

Eli’s voice was small. “It’s like it’s trying.”

I looked at the waveform on the screen and then past it, through the walls, to the bay. I thought about the fish hanging in the water “like they were waiting,” the way Noah had said. I thought about the clean shadow moving across the water like a line drawn with a ruler.

In my pocket, my field notebook pressed against my thigh. Paper. A record. Something I could hold that didn’t change.

I wrote one sentence at the top of a blank page, the ink digging in hard enough to leave an impression on the sheet beneath:

HUM MODULATES AFTER OUR PINGS.

Then I underlined it until the pen tip tore.

“Don’t tell anyone else yet,” I heard myself say.

Eli stared at me. “Mara—”

“Not because I want it,” I said quickly, defensively, though I did. “Because if we’re wrong, and it’s just… some weird resonance from the structures, and we start telling people it’s reacting, then this town will turn into a bonfire.”

Denise appeared in the doorway again, face pale. “The river mouth is full of them,” she said. “And—” Her voice cracked. “And the hatchery called. The tanks are… they’re dying too.”

Fish don’t care if they’re wild or captive. Water is water. And whatever was in it—or over it—was learning its way through.

Eli looked back at the screen, then at me. “Mara,” he said, “what do we do if it’s not resonance?”

I listened to the hum on loop, the way it tightened and loosened like breathing.

“I don’t know,” I said.

And for the first time since the sky broke, I believed myself.

### Chapter 3 — The First Word (but not yet language)

At 01:47 the institute’s building makes a noise like a tired ship.

It’s an old structure, poured concrete and patched additions, built when grant money was thick and optimism thicker. At night, when the air cools and the ocean damp sneaks in under doors, the pipes contract and click in the walls. If you’ve worked late enough times, you start to recognize the building’s moods by sound.

That night the clicking stopped.

Not gradually. Not like I was distracted and forgot to notice. It stopped the way a song stops when someone yanks a cord.

Eli had gone home at ten, pale and fidgety, leaving me with a hard drive full of recordings and an argument I’d lost with myself. Denise had offered to stay; I’d sent her away. Dr. Rook had gone too, after reminding me in a calm, exact voice that overtime wasn’t approved and that “inquiries” meant we needed to look competent.

As if competence could keep fish alive.

I slept for two hours on the cot in the first-aid room, shoes still on, and woke with the taste of disinfectant on my tongue and the hum in my teeth. It had wormed its way into me after days of listening. You stop hearing something and it keeps going anyway, inside your skull, like the ocean’s version of tinnitus.

I went back to the equipment room and made coffee so strong it looked like used motor oil. The coffee tasted like nothing. My nose was still full of rot.

On the hydrophone monitor the waveforms crawled across the screen in their usual busy scribble. The bay is never quiet. Even in still weather there’s wave wash, distant engines, the occasional weird squeal of dock hardware. The ocean is a city, sonically.

Then the line flattened.

Not completely—there was still baseline noise—but the chaotic peaks dipped, as if someone had put a hand over the mouth of the world.

I leaned in, squinting at the screen, as if my eyes could hear better than my ears. The time stamp in the corner blinked 01:47:12, 01:47:13.

The hum rose.

I didn’t hit record. The system was always recording, constantly buffering and saving, because the point of monitoring is catching things you didn’t know to ask for. I did, however, put my headphones on with hands that weren’t quite steady.

The hum was there, low as a distant freight train. But it wasn’t the same as the last few days. It felt closer, more structured. Like it had straightened its back.

A gap appeared in it—a clean absence. Then another. Then another.

Not pauses like breath. Pauses like punctuation.

My skin prickled under my sweatshirt. I glanced toward the equipment room door even though I was alone. I had the sudden, irrational thought that if someone came in and saw me sitting there with headphones on, hunched toward the monitor like a teenager hiding porn, I’d be embarrassed. As if what I was listening to was intimate.

The gaps began to repeat in sets. Three gaps close together, then a longer stretch, then two. It wasn’t Morse code. It wasn’t anything I knew. But it had that same feeling of intention: the sense that absence could mean something if you agreed it could.

I pulled the headphones off and listened to the room. The building was still too. No pipe clicks. No air handler thrum. Just the distant whisper of the sea through the walls.

The power flickered.

The fluorescent lights hiccuped, dimmed, returned. The servers’ cooling fans surged, then steadied. On the monitor the waveform did not change.

I stood and walked down the hall to the loading bay door. The institute faces the water; if you crack that door at night you can hear the surf. The metal handle was cold under my palm.

I opened it an inch.

Outside, the bay lay flat, black and glossy as oil. No moon. No wind. The water barely moved. The breakwater lights cast trembling reflections that looked too straight.

And—this is the part I still don’t know how to write without sounding like I’ve lost my mind—there was a rhythm in the quiet. Not a sound so much as a pattern in the way the surf failed to hiss. A structured silence, as if the ocean was holding itself back.

I shut the door again and went back to the equipment room because the alternative was standing in the dark letting the void teach me to pray.

On the screen, a new band appeared above the hum: a thin, higher-frequency thread, wavering like a violin string. It tried, faltered, tried again.

I did what I always do with unclear audio: I made it clearer.

The software was crude but reliable. Filter out a low band, notch out a known ship frequency, boost the range where human hearing can interpret pattern. It’s something you can do with whale calls, with prop cavitation, with the weird squeals of a loose mooring line. You can clean up the ocean to reveal what you want.

That thought should have stopped my hands.

Instead I clicked.

The hum reduced, the thread sharpened. The gaps became edges. The higher sound, the one that had been trying to climb into a space I could understand, resolved into something that made my scalp go tight.

A shape. A syllable-like scrape.

It was nothing like a voice. It didn’t have tone. It didn’t have breath. It was pressure modulated into something that carried the faintest suggestion of consonant and vowel. The way a parrot says a word without knowing it’s a word.

It repeated.

And then—this is when my stomach fell through the floor—it changed slightly on the third repetition, as if it was correcting itself.

My name is Mara. Two syllables. Easy enough for mouths with lips and tongues. The ocean has no lips. It has everything else.

The sound in my headphones made a rough approximation: “Mah… rah.”

I ripped the headphones off as if they were burning.

The equipment room was still. The monitor continued to scroll.

My first thought was that I’d done it. That my brain, hungry for meaning, had carved my name out of noise the way you find faces in clouds. Pattern-seeking is what we are. It’s the oldest trick in the mammal book.

My second thought was worse: that it had pulled my name out of the building. Out of the system. Out of my own damned mouth.

I sat down hard on the chair. It squeaked in the silence, loud as a gunshot.

“Okay,” I said out loud, because talking to yourself is either insanity or strategy. “Okay, Mara. This is where you stop. This is where you call someone.”

I reached for my phone and stopped with my thumb hovering over Eli’s name. It was almost two in the morning. He’d been running on fumes for days. Denise would answer, because Denise always answered, and she’d panic. Dr. Rook would answer because he slept like a man who’d trained himself to wake for opportunity, and he’d turn it into a plan before my fear had finished forming.

And Noah—Noah would answer because he never turned his phone off, not on the water—but what would I say? Hey, remember that hum you heard? It just said my name.

I set the phone down.

The sound came again, in my headphones this time because I’d put them back on, because I’m either brave or stupid and I’ve never been good at telling the difference.

“Mah… rah.”

Then, after a long gap, another shape. Different. Softer. Almost a question.

“Mah… rah?” it seemed to say, the second syllable lifted slightly by the pattern of absence behind it.

The ocean was calling roll.

I opened the raw file and marked the time stamp with shaking fingers. My notebook lay open beside me, the page from yesterday already filled with underlined words and angry pen strokes. I wrote:

01:47 — STRUCTURED SILENCE. HIGH THREAD. “MARA” (approx). REPEATS + SELF-CORRECTION.

Then I paused with the pen hovering and added, smaller, because it felt like admitting weakness:

If this is patterning, I’m not sure whose.

The sound shifted again. It tried a different combination. The gaps tightened, stretched. The higher thread broke into two bands, one above the other, like harmonics.

Something in it clicked—a rhythm that felt like the structure of a question. Not the meaning. The intention.

YOU HEAR?

I didn’t “hear” those words. There were no words. There was only the insistence of pattern that my brain, trained on speech, translated into the nearest human shape.

My mouth went dry.

I took my hands off the keyboard and sat very still, listening.

The hum modulated around my silence as if waiting for input.

“Oh my God,” I whispered, and then, because I am the kind of idiot who responds to the unknown by running my mouth, I leaned toward the microphone on the desk—the one we used for calibrating the hydrophone interface—and said, softly, “Yes.”

The moment the word left my lips, the hum changed.

Not randomly. Not coincidentally. It tightened, as if someone had grabbed a thread and pulled it through a needle. The gaps reorganized. The higher band flared.

In my headphones, the ocean made a sound that was not my name.

It was a rough, grinding approximation of a vowel—like a buoy chain dragged across stone—followed by a sharp click. Then another.

Eli would later call it “phoneme-like events” because Eli liked to put a lab coat on terror. In that moment it felt like a mouth trying on teeth.

I should have stopped. I should have cut power, pulled the plugs, thrown the hard drives into the bay, walked into the dark and pretended I’d never heard anything but waves.

Instead I said, “Can you… understand me?”

The hum held, then pulsed, as if reconsidering.

The structured silences came back in a new arrangement. The thread climbed, fell, climbed. The sound that had approximated my name returned, but it was wrong now—closer to “Mar,” clipped, as if it had learned a shorter label was more efficient.

Then, in a voice that was not a voice, in pressure and absence and vibration, it produced the clearest sequence yet:

“YOU… HEAR… YOU… ME.”

It wasn’t elegant. It wasn’t grammar. It was a toddler stacking blocks.

My hands shook so hard the cursor on the screen jittered with my touch on the mouse.

I laughed once, a short, ugly sound. Not joy. Pure nervous system overload. My eyes stung, and I couldn’t tell if I was going to cry or vomit.

“Yes,” I said, and my voice cracked. “I hear you.”

The hum dropped in frequency so suddenly my ears popped. The higher thread steadied, becoming almost—almost—stable.

And in that steadiness was something I hadn’t expected, something that made my stomach twist harder than fear.

Regret? Not exactly. Recognition of cost.

Because along with the pattern, layered under it like a second message, there were other noises now—subsonic thuds, irregular, like distant explosions. The hydrophone picked up frantic splashes.

I pulled up the live feed from the buoy camera near the river mouth. The video buffer spun, then resolved into grainy night vision.

The water surface boiled with movement. Fish, schooling tight, churning in panic. Some leapt clear of the water and landed on the rocks, silver flashes in the infrared. They flopped, gasped, went still.

As I watched, the structured silences continued in my headphones, patient and methodical, while the bay killed itself in response.

I slammed the headphones off, as if it could stop it.

In the quiet of the equipment room, my own breathing sounded obscene.

The hum continued without me. The bay continued without me. The fish continued to die, the way they had all day, only now I couldn’t pretend it was accident.

I picked up my phone with fingers that felt numb and scrolled to Noah’s name before I could talk myself out of it.

If the ocean had my name, it could have his. It could have all of ours. It could pull us out of the air the way it pulled oxygen out of gills.

My phone buzzed once before I could hit call.

Incoming: NOAH KADE.

I stared at it, throat tight.

I answered. “Noah?”

His voice was rough with sleep and something else. “Mara,” he said. “Are you awake?”

“Yes,” I whispered.

There was a pause on the line, and behind his breath I could hear it: a low vibration through his phone speaker, the same pressure-hum, as if the sound had climbed out of the water and into the wires.

Noah swallowed. “Tell me you’re hearing that,” he said.

I closed my eyes. In my headphones the ocean had tried to say you. In my phone it was saying we.

“Yes,” I said. “I’m hearing it.”

On the other end, Noah exhaled shakily.

And then, beneath both our voices, the bay spoke again—slow, careful, as if it had learned the shape of speech by pressing it against dying things.

“WE… LEARN… WRONG,” it said, the words arriving more as pressure in my bones than sound in my ears. “TOO… MUCH… LOSS.”
