## Chapter 2 — Daylight Doesn’t Fix It

By morning the text had taken on the sticky unreality of a dream. Asha half expected to open her phone and find it gone, like her brain had invented the whole thing out of revision stress and too much cold brew.

It wasn’t gone.

🩸🩸🩸 sat there in her notifications, smug as anything. The photo of the bridge crown. MEET. WHERE. YOU. LEFT. IT.

She lay in bed and watched the ceiling for a full three minutes, doing the kind of breathing exercise the internet swore would calm you down. It did not. It only made her more aware she was alive and therefore could be perceived, judged, ruined.

Her mum knocked once and came in anyway, because privacy was a myth you saw on American films.

“You’re up,” Mum said, flicking the curtains open with the violence of someone who didn’t believe in gradual transitions. Grey light flooded the room. Everything looked worse in daylight: the highlighter stain on the duvet, the pile of notes like shed skin, Asha’s own reflection in the dark screen of her laptop.

“Mm,” Asha said.

Mum stood there in her dressing gown, hair scraped back, phone already in hand. She was always holding a phone, like it was an extra organ. “You were awake late. I heard the floor.”

“I dropped my book.”

“What book makes you pace?” Mum asked, not unkindly. “Come down. Tea’s on. And eat something. You’ll faint in assembly and then they’ll ring me and I’ll have to come up there in my shop shoes.”

“I’m not going to faint,” Asha said automatically, which was the sort of thing you said right before fainting.

Downstairs, the shop was quiet. The shutters were up halfway, letting in the empty high street: closed charity shop, closed bank, a man in a hi-vis jacket smoking like it was an occupation. Mum moved behind the counter, putting out milk, straightening packets that didn’t need straightening.

Asha sat on the stool by the kettle and stared at her phone on the counter as if it might start bleeding.

Mum slid a plate towards her. Toast, slightly burnt. “Eat.”

Asha ate because it was easier than explaining she felt sick.

Her phone buzzed with the group chat.

Dead Serious (no really)

> Connor: everyone alive

> Mina: barely. i dreamed about my phone exploding

> Theo: i didn’t sleep

Asha typed with her thumb under the counter like she was dealing drugs.

> Asha: same. don’t talk about it at school

> Connor: obviously. no one says anything. we’re not giving whoever it is a show

Asha’s stomach twisted at the word “show.” She glanced at Mum, who was watching a daytime quiz programme on the little telly behind the counter. Ordinary. Normal. The kind of morning where nothing happened.

Except something had.

School smelled like wet coats and disinfectant. The corridor buzz was louder than usual, like everyone had been given a low-voltage electric shock.

Asha walked in with her bag strap digging into her shoulder and tried not to look like she was listening.

Two Year 10s by the lockers were huddled around a phone, shrieking.

“…and then he sent three BLOOD EMOJIS,” one of them said, delighted.

“It’s a challenge,” the other said. “My cousin said if you get it, you have to go under the bridge at midnight or you die.”

“You don’t die,” a boy chimed in. “You just get haunted. Like by… a canal man.”

Asha’s throat went tight.

She kept walking.

Someone had already turned it into content.

At her locker, she opened the door and pretended to focus on the timetable stuck inside. Her hands were steady, which surprised her. She could do steady. She could do normal. She could do this.

Connor appeared like he’d been summoned by the sound of gossip. He leaned on the locker next to hers, too casual. His eyes were bright in that way they got when he was pretending something was funny so it couldn’t hurt him.

“You alright?” he asked, quietly enough that it could pass as banter.

“I’m fine,” Asha said. “Don’t talk about it here.”

“Wasn’t going to,” Connor said. Then, lower: “Whole school’s on about it though. Blood emoji plague.”

Asha shut her locker with more force than necessary. “It’s not a plague. It’s someone being weird.”

Connor’s smile flickered. “Yeah. Someone being weird about… you know.”

Asha felt heat rise in her face. “Don’t.”

Connor held up his hands. “Alright, alright. I’m not saying it.”

Mina joined them, hood up even though it was against uniform, eyeliner sharp enough to cut glass. She didn’t say hello. She didn’t do hellos when she was angry.

“Assembly,” she said, like she was announcing a funeral.

Theo arrived last, pale, hair styled too carefully, like if it looked right maybe he’d feel right. His eyes flicked over Asha’s face, then away. He looked like he’d been crying or wanted to.

They moved together down the corridor as a unit. Asha hated the way people’s eyes tracked them like they were a thing. It was probably in her head. Everything was probably in her head.

In the hall, everyone sat cross-legged on the floor, knees pressed into knees. The air was warm and damp. Someone’s Lynx sprayed earlier had merged with teenage sweat into a smell that should be illegal.

Mr Durnham, Head of Year, stood at the front with his clipboard and his face of Concerned Adult. Behind him, the projector screen displayed a slide that said:

STAY SAFE ONLINE

Asha nearly laughed. It came out as a cough.

“Morning, Year Eleven,” Mr Durnham began. “We’ve had reports of some… messages circulating.”

Asha’s stomach dropped.

Connor’s elbow nudged hers, a warning. Don’t react.

Mr Durnham continued. “Now, I’m not going to name specifics, because we’re not here to amplify rumours. But if you receive a message that makes you feel uncomfortable, you should tell a trusted adult. You should not respond. You should not share it. And you should certainly not go anywhere you are instructed to go.”

Mina muttered, “Revolutionary.”

Theo made a sound that might have been a laugh if it wasn’t also a sob trapped in his throat.

Mr Durnham smiled like he’d just solved crime. “We’re also reminding you that the canal area is not a safe place to be at night. We’ve had complaints about… gatherings there.”

Asha stared at the floor. Her knee bounced. She pressed her hand on it to stop it, like she could press herself into calm.

After assembly, they didn’t speak until they were in the science block corridor, where the walls were lined with faded posters of the digestive system and nobody lingered unless they were avoiding someone.

Connor leaned against a radiator. “Right. That was helpful.”

“Don’t be a dick,” Mina said.

“I’m not being a dick,” Connor said. “I’m being realistic. ‘Tell a trusted adult.’ The trusted adults are the ones who told us last time to keep it quiet.”

Asha’s head snapped up. “No one told us to keep it quiet.”

Connor’s eyes held hers. “They did, though, didn’t they? Not in those words. But… you know.”

Asha’s chest felt tight. The corridor seemed too bright. She could hear the distant roar of year groups changing lessons like a tide.

Theo said, “I can’t do this.”

Mina’s gaze flicked to him. “Do what? Be guilty in public?”

Theo flinched. “I’m not—”

Asha cut in, too sharp. “We are not having this conversation here.”

Mina’s mouth twisted. “Where, then? Under the bridge? At midnight?”

Connor looked away, jaw working.

Asha tried to slow down, to choose her words like stepping stones. “We don’t go. It’s a stupid text. We ignore it.”

Mina raised her eyebrows. “Ignoring things has worked brilliantly so far.”

Theo squeezed his eyes shut. “Someone’s going to post something. I can feel it. Like… like the universe is waiting.”

Asha wanted to shake him. Instead she said, “What did you mean last night? When you said it knows about Rowan.”

Theo opened his eyes. They were glassy. “I… I didn’t mean anything. I was just—”

Connor said, “Theo.”

Theo swallowed. “It just feels… targeted. Like whoever it is, they’re not just sending it to randoms. They sent the bridge. They sent the crown. That’s not… that’s not general.”

Asha’s phone buzzed in her pocket. She didn’t take it out. She was suddenly terrified of looking, like the screen might have a face.

Mina’s phone buzzed too. She yanked it out and looked, eyes narrowing.

“It’s in my requests,” Mina said.

Connor’s hand came up automatically, like he wanted to grab the phone and crush it. “What?”

Mina read aloud. Her voice was flat, but her fingers were tight around the phone.

“🩸🩸🩸,” she said. “And then—”

She stopped.

Asha’s stomach turned. “And then what?”

Mina’s gaze flicked up to Asha, then back to the screen. “It says: YOU KEPT IT.”

Connor swore, quietly. “Okay. Okay. So it’s us. It’s definitely us.”

Theo made a small noise and covered his mouth with his hand.

Asha pulled her phone out despite herself. There was a new notification: message request. Same unknown number, like it was perched in her life now, settled.

🩸🩸

Then: YOU KEPT IT.

Asha’s skin prickled.

The number of drops had changed. Two now, not three. Like a countdown. Like someone tapping their foot.

Connor took a breath and did the thing he did when he wanted to sound older than seventeen. “We should tell someone.”

Mina looked at him like he’d suggested sacrificing a goat. “Who? Mr Durnham with his PowerPoint? Your dad?”

Connor flinched at “dad” like it had teeth. “My dad would take it seriously.”

Asha heard herself say, “No.”

Both of them looked at her.

She forced her voice even. “If Connor’s dad gets involved, it becomes… official.”

“Good,” Mina said.

Asha shook her head. “Not good. Not for us.”

There it was. The truth under everything: she didn’t just want it solved. She wanted it solved without consequences.

Connor’s mouth tightened. “Asha.”

Theo said, “Maybe it wants the video.”

Asha’s heartbeat stumbled. She hated how that word kept appearing, like a body floating up no matter how many rocks you tied to it.

Mina’s voice went low. “There isn’t a video.”

Theo didn’t answer.

Asha’s phone buzzed again. A text, not WhatsApp this time. She pulled it out, hands suddenly clumsy.

Unknown number.

A photo: a screenshot, cropped close. A chat bubble, timestamped last summer. A message she remembered typing and then deleting before anyone could screenshot it.

Only it had been screenshotted. By someone.

The message read:

Asha: don’t ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE

Asha’s mouth went cold.

The corridor tilted slightly, like the school was a ship and she’d stepped onto the wrong part of it. She gripped the radiator behind her, fingers burning against the heat.

Connor leaned in and read it. His face changed. The joking mask slipped and something frightened and young showed underneath.

Mina’s eyes widened, just a fraction. “That’s… that’s from—”

Theo made a choking sound. “Oh my God.”

Asha couldn’t breathe properly. The screenshot sat on her screen like a piece of her peeled off and held up to the light.

Connor’s phone buzzed. He looked at it and went pale.

“It’s got me too,” he said, voice barely there. He swallowed. “It’s got all of us.”

Mina’s jaw clenched. “Where did it get that?”

Theo whispered, “Someone kept it.”

Asha’s skin crawled. The message YOU KEPT IT wasn’t an accusation in general. It was specific. It was personal.

Kept what. Kept *everything.*

Asha’s phone buzzed once more. Another text.

Unknown number.

00:03 TOMORROW.

MEET WHERE YOU LEFT IT.

Asha stared at the timestamp. Not midnight exactly. Three minutes past. Petty. Human.

Mina exhaled through her nose. “Okay,” she said, quiet and furious. “So someone’s playing.”

Connor’s voice came out rough. “Or someone’s—”

Asha cut him off. “We’re not going.”

Her own voice sounded too loud in the corridor, like a declaration you couldn’t take back.

But even as she said it, she could feel the shift. The point at which ignoring stopped being a plan and became a choice.

And choices, inconveniently, had consequences.
step_outputs.8:
## Chapter 3 — Receipts

Asha tried to get through the rest of the day by turning herself into a machine.

Walk to English. Answer questions about a poem that involved a dead man on a moor. Laugh in the right places. Don’t look at anyone’s phone. Don’t check her own.

She failed at all of it.

In English, Miss Talbot asked, “What’s the effect of the repetition?” and Asha’s brain offered: *🩸🩸🩸.* She wrote it in the margin by accident and had to scribble it out until the paper tore slightly. Miss Talbot gave her a look like she’d just witnessed a small tragedy.

At break, in the toilets, Asha watched two girls in the mirror rehearse concern.

“I heard it’s like… to do with that thing, you know?” one whispered, checking her lip gloss.

“What thing?” the other said, eyes bright.

“The bridge thing.”

“Oh my God,” the second girl breathed, delighted.

Asha left without washing her hands properly, then hated herself for that, because it was exactly the kind of detail your brain clung to later as proof you were a bad person.

By lunch they’d agreed, in the thin sheltered strip behind the maths block, that they’d meet after school.

Not under the bridge. Not at midnight. Somewhere normal.

Connor suggested McDonald’s, as if the smell of fryer oil could ward off guilt. Mina vetoed it because, in her words, “the McDonald’s here is where hope goes to die.” Theo suggested the library with the seriousness of someone presenting a new constitution.

They ended up at the park by the leisure centre, because it was public enough that nobody could stab them, but not so public that someone’s mum would see them and start a WhatsApp chain.

Asha got there first and sat on the cold metal swing, not swinging, just holding the chains so tightly the links pressed into her palms.

She watched the road beyond the fence. The leisure centre sign flickered like it was tired. A kid in primary uniform dropped a packet of crisps and burst into tears. His dad picked them up, gave them back, crisps covered in grit. The kid ate them anyway.

Connor arrived with his hood up and a Tesco meal deal in his hand like it was a peace offering. Mina arrived with her tote bag and the expression of someone attending an execution. Theo came last, as if he’d been pushed here by a strong wind.

They didn’t say hello. The air between them was full of what they weren’t saying.

Asha spoke first because she couldn’t stand it. “Okay. We need to work out who has those screenshots.”

Connor sat on the opposite swing and immediately started swinging, too hard, like he was trying to outrun the conversation. “Someone from the group chat,” he said. “Obviously.”

Mina folded her arms. “There were more people in that chat, though. Not just us.”

Asha nodded. There had been others, briefly. People added and removed as the night unfolded. Names she didn’t want to say.

Theo’s voice came out small. “Could be someone who… recovered them. Like, deleted doesn’t mean deleted.”

Connor snorted. “Are you telling me the canal has a recycling bin for our sins?”

Mina’s eyes flashed. “Stop. Joking.”

Connor’s swing slowed. He stared at his shoes. “Sorry.”

Asha tried to speak gently, and it came out like a teacher. “We need to make a list. Who was there that night. Who could be doing this.”

Theo flinched at the words “that night” like they were a slap.

Mina said, “Leah.”

Asha blinked. “What?”

Mina shrugged, but her mouth was tight. “Rowan’s sister. She’s older, she hates us, she knows what happened. She’d have motive.”

Connor’s head snapped up. “Leah’s not… she’s not some hacker mastermind.”

“Doesn’t need to be,” Mina said. “All you need is screenshots and an attitude.”

Asha’s stomach churned. Leah Bennett: sixth-form legend in the way older kids were legends to you when you were younger. Sharp eyes, sharp tongue, always at the edge of fights. Rowan’s sister, yes, but also a person who’d once told Asha in Year 9 to stop apologising for taking up space.

Asha hadn’t seen her in months.

Connor rubbed his forehead. “Even if it’s Leah, how’s she got the messages? I deleted mine. Asha deleted hers. Theo—”

All three of them looked at Theo.

Theo’s face went blank, then too animated. “Why are you looking at me like that?”

Mina said, “Do you still have it?”

Theo blinked rapidly. “Have what?”

Connor’s laugh was one sharp bark. “Don’t do that. Don’t play stupid. The video.”

Theo’s throat bobbed. “There isn’t—”

Asha cut in, voice tight. “Theo. Be honest.”

Theo’s eyes darted. To the ground. To the fence. To the leisure centre sign. Anywhere but them.

“It’s not like—” he began, then stopped. His hands were twisting the strap of his bag. “It’s not like I kept it to be a freak.”

Mina’s voice was quiet. “So you kept it.”

Theo swallowed. “It’s not the whole thing.”

Connor scoffed. “That’s comforting.”

Theo’s eyes flashed, suddenly angry. “You filmed too!”

Connor’s mouth opened, then shut. He looked at Asha like she’d betrayed him by letting the accusation exist.

Asha’s pulse hammered. “I didn’t film.”

Theo laughed without humour. “No. You just told everyone what to say after.”

The words landed. Asha felt them, physically, like cold water down her back.

Mina’s gaze snapped to Asha. “Did you?”

Asha’s cheeks burned. She could hear her own heartbeat, loud as a bassline. She wanted to say no. She wanted to say it wasn’t like that. She wanted to say she’d been trying to help.

Instead she said, “I was trying to stop it getting worse.”

Connor muttered, “It did get worse.”

Asha’s fingers tightened on the swing chain until it hurt. Pain was good. Pain was controllable.

“Where is it?” Mina asked Theo.

Theo hesitated. “On an old phone.”

Connor swore. “Why do you even have an old phone?”

Theo looked offended. “Because some of us don’t smash our phones every time we get nervous.”

Connor’s jaw flexed. “I didn’t smash mine. It… fell.”

Mina rolled her eyes. “Are we actually doing this?”

Theo’s shoulders sagged. “It’s in my drawer. It doesn’t have a SIM. It’s just— it’s just there.”

Asha said, “Get it. Tonight.”

Theo’s head jerked up. “Tonight?”

“No,” Asha said quickly. “Not the bridge. Not midnight. Just… bring it so we can see what it is. If that’s what they want, we need to know.”

Connor stared at the ground. “If we watch it, it’s like… making it real again.”

Mina’s laugh was soft and horrible. “Mate, it’s been real. We’ve just been pretending it’s a story we can pause.”

Connor looked like he wanted to argue and couldn’t find a joke to hide behind.

Asha’s phone buzzed in her pocket, and all four of them froze like animals in headlights.

She pulled it out. A WhatsApp notification from someone in Year 11 she barely spoke to.

Tilly: omg ash r u ok x

Asha’s skin went cold. “It’s started,” she said.

Connor leaned in. “What?”

Asha turned the screen so they could see. Then another message popped up.

Tilly: have u seen what’s going round??? 😳

Mina hissed through her teeth. “Show me.”

Asha’s fingers were clumsy as she opened Instagram. Her hands felt too big. Too stupid.

On someone’s story—Josh Ward, who thought vaping made him an adult—there was a screenshot. A group chat message from last summer. Connor’s name at the top.

Connor: lol he’s fine. he’s just being dramatic

Under it, someone had scrawled in red marker: 🩸🩸🩸

Connor made a sound like he’d been punched. “That’s not—”

Mina’s eyes narrowed. “That’s real.”

Theo covered his mouth. “Oh my God.”

Asha felt her throat tighten. That message. She remembered it. She remembered Connor saying it aloud too, laughing, and then going quiet when Rowan didn’t get up quickly enough.

She scrolled. Another story. Another screenshot. Asha’s own message. The one from this morning’s text.

Asha: don’t ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE

Someone had added captions: “PLEASE 😭” and “so caring x”

Her stomach lurched.

Mina’s voice was low. “They’re making you the villain.”

Asha stared at the screen. “I am a villain.”

Connor snapped, “No you’re not.”

Asha looked at him. His face was flushed, eyes bright, angry at the world for daring to point a finger at one of them.

Mina said, “We don’t know who’s posting yet. Could be Leah. Could be someone else who got hold of it. But it’s spreading.”

Theo whispered, “My mum follows Josh Ward.”

Connor groaned. “Why would she—”

“Because she thinks he’s ‘polite,’” Theo said miserably. “He held the door for her once.”

Asha’s phone buzzed again. Another DM.

Unknown number: 🩸🩸

Then: TOMORROW 00:03

Then, a new message:

BRING WHAT YOU STOLE.

Connor stared at it. “What did we steal?”

Mina’s eyes flicked to Theo. “The truth.”

Theo’s face crumpled. “We didn’t steal it. We just— we just didn’t—”

“You kept it,” Mina said softly, and it wasn’t even aimed at Theo now. It was aimed at all of them. “You kept it to yourselves.”

Asha’s chest hurt. “We didn’t know what to do.”

Mina’s gaze held hers. “You knew what to do. You didn’t want to.”

Connor’s swing was still now. He sat with his elbows on his knees, meal deal untouched. “Okay,” he said, voice rough. “Practical. How do we find out who it is?”

Asha forced her brain into motion. “We can try tracing the number,” she said. “Or— or we can… bait them. Reply.”

Mina snorted. “And say what? ‘Hello, unknown blackmailer. Please identify yourself and your motives.’”

Connor said, “We could ask around. Who’s posting the stories.”

Theo shook his head. “If we ask, it makes it worse.”

Asha looked at Theo. “Everything makes it worse now.”

Theo’s eyes filled. He blinked hard like he could blink the wet away. “I can’t be—” he started, then stopped. His voice broke. “I can’t be the reason Rowan—”

No one spoke. Saying Rowan’s name out loud in daylight felt like stepping on something fragile.

Asha stood up from the swing, legs unsteady. “We need to know what’s on that phone,” she said. “Theo, you bring it tonight. After dinner. To mine. Mum will be downstairs.”

Theo nodded, too fast. “Yeah. Yeah, okay.”

Connor exhaled. “Fine.”

Mina looked between them. “And then what?”

Asha opened her mouth, and nothing came out. Because “then what” was the part she’d been avoiding for a year.

Her phone buzzed again—this time not unknown, not gossip.

A new follower request on Instagram.

Name: leahb__ (private account)

Asha stared at it.

Mina leaned in. “Is that—?”

Asha’s fingers hovered. She could decline. Block. Pretend she hadn’t seen it.

Or accept and step into whatever was coming.

Before she could choose, Connor said, “Don’t. Not now.”

Asha’s throat tightened. She hit decline.

Instantly, another request appeared. Same name. Like a hand knocking again, harder.

Asha’s stomach dropped.

Mina’s voice was very quiet. “She’s not going to let you ignore it.”

Asha’s phone buzzed, one last time, with a new text. Not emojis.

Just four words.

SEE YOU AT THE BUS.

## Chapter 4 — Public Places, Private Panic

By the time Thursday rolled around, the blood emoji had become a joke you could buy.

Someone—probably Josh Ward, because he had the entrepreneurial spirit of a rat—was selling “🩸🩸🩸” stickers for £1 a sheet out of his blazer pocket at break. People were putting them on planners, water bottles, the back of toilet doors. Someone had drawn three little drops on the whiteboard in maths and Mr Patel had sighed like he’d aged ten years in one lesson.

Asha watched it happen like she was underwater.

Every time her phone buzzed, her body reacted before her brain: a tightness in the chest, a cold drop in the stomach, a flush up her neck. She started leaving her phone face down in lessons like it was an animal that might bite.

She couldn’t leave it off, though. Not properly. That felt like walking into traffic with your eyes shut.

At lunch, they met in the same strip behind the maths block, because rituals were comforting even when they were stupid.

Theo had dark shadows under his eyes. Connor looked like he’d slept, technically, but only because he’d passed out from exhaustion rather than peace. Mina’s eyeliner was sharper than ever, like she’d applied it with fury.

Connor said, “Right. The bus. Why the bus?”

Mina leaned back against the wall, arms folded. “Because it’s public. Because she thinks we won’t do anything in public. She’s right.”

Asha stared at the ground. She’d been googling blood donation buses at three in the morning like a normal person. The NHS one came to town once a month and parked outside the big supermarket because it was the only place with a car park that didn’t require a sacrifice to enter.

Theo whispered, “What if it’s not her?”

Mina’s eyes narrowed. “Who else would it be?”

Theo’s hands twitched at his sides. “I don’t know. Someone who… someone who hates us.”

Connor’s laugh was brittle. “That’s a long list, mate.”

Asha forced herself to speak. “We don’t have to do anything. We can just… go and look. See if anyone’s there. We’re not bringing anything.”

Connor glanced at her. “We’re not bringing anything *yet*, you mean.”

Asha’s cheeks burned. She hated that they all knew, now, about her message, about the way she’d tried to steer it. She hated that she deserved it.

Mina said, “What about your old phone?” to Theo.

Theo flinched. “I couldn’t— I didn’t—”

Connor cut in, “He didn’t bring it.”

Theo’s face crumpled. “My mum came into my room and started asking why I was ‘off’ and then she went through my drawers because she thinks I vape and—”

Mina scoffed. “Do you vape?”

Theo looked offended. “No. I just look like someone who vapes.”

“Accurate,” Connor muttered, but there was no bite in it.

Asha’s throat tightened. “So we have nothing to bring even if we wanted to.”

Mina’s gaze flicked to Asha’s face. “You’re relieved.”

Asha opened her mouth, then shut it. She was relieved. She was also terrified that relief meant something about her.

They agreed to meet after school at the corner shop by the supermarket—Asha refused to say “outside the donation bus” like it was a plan rather than a looming humiliation.

The day crawled.

In history, someone turned around and whispered, “Did you get the blood text?” with a grin like it was gossip about a celebrity.

Asha said, flatly, “No,” and hated how convincing she sounded.

At 3:20, she told Mum she was going to the library.

“You are going to the library?” Mum repeated, suspicious, like “library” was a slang term for “doing crimes.”

“Yes,” Asha lied. “With… Mina.”

Mina’s name worked like a badge of legitimacy. Mina’s mum would probably report their exact book choices to the council.

Mum sighed. “Be back for dinner. And don’t go near that canal. They’ve been talking about it on the radio.”

Asha’s stomach lurched. “The radio?”

“The radio,” Mum said, turning to straighten a shelf of crisps. “People have nothing better to do. There’s always some nonsense. Just come home.”

Asha nodded too hard. “Yeah. Of course.”

Outside, the air was damp and cold in that late-autumn way that got under your clothes. Connor was already by the trolley bay, hands shoved deep in his pockets, trying to look like he wasn’t waiting.

Theo arrived, breathless, hair too neat. Mina arrived last, hood up, tote bag swinging.

None of them spoke for a moment. The supermarket doors whooshed open and shut. A man pushed a trolley full of Red Bull and frozen chips like he was preparing for war. Somewhere, a child screamed.

“Right,” Connor said, forcing brightness. “Let’s go donate blood. Bit of civic duty.”

Mina shot him a look. “Don’t.”

“Sorry,” Connor said immediately, and then, quieter, “Sorry.”

They walked across the car park as if it was the longest distance any of them had ever travelled.

The bus was there: a big white vehicle with NHS branding and cheerful posters of smiling people holding plasters. A volunteer in a hi-vis vest stood by the steps handing out leaflets. A queue of mostly older people waited, chatting like it was a normal errand.

Asha’s heart hammered. It was so ordinary it felt unreal, like someone had photoshopped their panic into someone else’s day.

Theo muttered, “This is… insane.”

Connor whispered, “Maybe the blood emojis were just… advertising.”

Mina deadpanned, “Yes. Because nothing sells blood donation like terrorising teenagers.”

Asha’s phone buzzed in her pocket. She didn’t take it out. She could feel it vibrating against her thigh like a pulse.

They hovered near the trolley bay pretending to check their phones. Asha watched the bus steps. Watched faces. Tried to spot someone who looked like a threat.

Leah wasn’t subtle when she appeared.

She didn’t burst out of nowhere. She simply walked across the car park with the steady pace of someone going to work. Hair scraped back. Practical coat. Work trainers. She carried a paper coffee cup. She looked tired in a way that made Asha suddenly aware that Leah had been dealing with the aftermath for a year, every day, while Asha had been revising for exams and pretending she wasn’t thinking about it.

Leah’s eyes found them immediately. No hesitation. No scanning. Like she’d been looking at them already, in her head, for months.

Connor’s shoulders tensed. “That’s her,” he whispered.

Mina’s face went still. Theo made a small sound, like a swallowed gasp.

Asha’s mouth went dry. She felt frozen and furious at herself for freezing.

Leah stopped a few metres away. Close enough that the smell of coffee reached them. She didn’t smile. She didn’t look dramatic. She looked like she’d run out of patience.

“You lot,” she said.

Connor attempted a laugh. “Alright, Leah.”

Leah’s gaze flicked over him and dismissed him like he was background noise. Her eyes landed on Asha.

Asha’s heart lurched. She hated being seen.

“You declined my request,” Leah said.

Asha swallowed. “I didn’t know it was you.”

Leah’s mouth twitched, almost a smile and not at all. “Course you did.”

Mina’s voice was sharp. “What do you want?”

Leah looked at Mina, properly, like she’d noticed her presence now. “You,” she said. Then she looked back at Asha. “And you.”

Theo shifted, trying to make himself smaller. Leah’s gaze flicked to him and sharpened.

Theo’s voice came out thin. “We didn’t—”

Leah cut him off. “Don’t,” she said, and it wasn’t a shout. It was worse: calm, like she’d heard every version of their denial already and none of them mattered.

The volunteer by the bus called, cheerfully, “Next, please!” as if this was all a normal Tuesday.

Connor stepped forward, trying to regain control with charm. “Look, we’re not doing this here.”

Leah stared at him. “Why? Because there’s adults around? Because there’s cameras? Because it’s public?” Her eyes flicked to the bus. “You like public when it’s jokes and stickers.”

Asha’s cheeks burned. She hated that Leah knew about the stickers. She hated that Leah knew anything.

Mina said, low, “This is about Rowan.”

Leah’s jaw tightened. For the first time, something moved behind her eyes—pain, maybe, or rage. “Yes,” she said. “It is.”

Asha forced herself to speak. “Leah, if you’re the one sending the messages—”

Leah’s laugh was short, humourless. “If?” She took a step closer. Asha could see the rawness at the edge of Leah’s mouth where she’d been chewing it. “You still doing that thing where you pretend you don’t know? Like it’s going to save you?”

Asha’s throat tightened. “I don’t know what you want from us.”

Leah looked at her for a long moment. Then she said, very quietly, “He still can’t hold a pen properly.”

The car park noise didn’t stop. A trolley clattered. Someone beeped their car. A baby cried.

But Asha felt like the world had shifted.

Theo made a choked sound. Connor went very still. Mina’s face tightened, and for a second she looked younger than fifteen.

Leah continued, voice flat. “He’s got exercises. Every day. Like it’s a job. He gets tired. He gets angry. And then he feels stupid for being angry because everyone says ‘at least you’re alive’ like that’s the whole point of living.”

Asha’s eyes stung. She blinked hard, furious with herself for it.

Leah’s gaze slid to Connor. “You remember him being funny? Loud? He doesn’t talk much now. Not because he can’t. Because he doesn’t want to. Because you lot made him—” She stopped. Her throat worked. She took a breath, like swallowing something sharp.

Mina’s voice was rough. “We didn’t mean—”

Leah snapped, finally, a crack in the calm. “I don’t care what you meant.”

People in the queue glanced over. The volunteer frowned, uncertain.

Connor put his hands up, palms out. “Alright. Alright. What do you want? Just— tell us.”

Leah looked back at Asha. “You kept it,” she said, and the words landed with weight now, not creepiness. “You kept the messages. You kept the video. You kept the truth. You let them say it was an accident and you let him become a rumour.”

Asha’s voice shook despite her efforts. “We didn’t keep—”

Leah’s eyes narrowed. “Don’t lie to me. Not now. Not here.” She leaned in slightly. “You don’t get to tidy this up.”

Asha felt something inside her—anger, shame—rise like bile. “I was trying to stop it getting worse!”

Leah’s expression didn’t change. “For who?”

Asha opened her mouth. No answer came.

Leah stepped back. Her voice dropped again, controlled. “00:03,” she said. “Under the bridge. Bring what you stole.”

Connor said, “No.”

Leah’s gaze flicked to him. “Then don’t. Stay home. See what happens.”

Theo whispered, “What happens?”

Leah looked at him, and Asha saw something like disgust. “You’ll find out,” Leah said.

Then she turned and walked away, coffee in hand, blending into the car park like she’d never been there.

For a second they stood frozen.

Then Mina breathed out. “Well,” she said. “That was… extremely clarifying.”

Connor swore under his breath. “She’s mental.”

Mina’s eyes flashed. “She’s grieving.”

Connor’s jaw clenched. “She’s threatening us.”

“She’s threatening you with the truth,” Mina shot back. “Different.”

Theo’s voice was small. “We have to do something.”

Asha’s phone buzzed. She pulled it out. Not a text. Instagram notifications.

Josh Ward’s story had been updated.

A new screenshot. A new red marker scrawl. 🩸🩸🩸.

But this one was worse.

It was Theo’s message from last year, timestamped, and beneath it a blurry still image: a flash-lit shape on the ground, someone’s leg bent wrong, someone laughing in the background.

Asha felt her stomach drop through the tarmac.

Theo made a strangled sound. “No. No, no, no.”

Connor grabbed Asha’s phone and stared. His face drained. “That’s… that’s the video.”

Mina’s voice went cold. “It’s out.”

Asha’s hands shook. She took her phone back and scrolled, desperate, as if scrolling could undo it.

Comments were already appearing.

“omg”  
“is that rowan??”  
“wtf you lot are sick”  
“it was an accident leave them”  
“🩸🩸🩸”

Asha’s vision blurred. She could taste metal in her mouth.

Theo whispered, “My mum’s going to see.”

Connor’s voice was tight. “We need to get ahead of it.”

Mina laughed, sharp. “Ahead? We’re already behind. We’ve been behind for a year.”

Asha stared at the still image on the screen. Her own message, her own plea not to ring anyone, had been turned into mockery. The thing she’d tried to bury was now being dug up by strangers with emojis.

Her phone buzzed with a message request.

Unknown number.

Not emojis this time. Just:

TOO LATE TO BE QUIET.

## Chapter 5 — Who Gets to Tell the Story

By Friday morning, the school had decided it was having an incident.

You could tell because there were more adults in high-vis vests than usual and because the Headteacher stood at the gates smiling too hard, like she was greeting Ofsted and not a pack of teenagers with phones full of someone else’s worst night.

Asha walked in with her stomach in a knot and her hood up until a prefect told her to take it down in the tone of someone who’d discovered authority last week and was drunk on it.

“Uniform,” the prefect said, eyes flicking to Asha’s face like she expected blood drops to appear there.

Asha forced her mouth into something that wasn’t a scowl. “Cheers.”

“Don’t be rude,” the prefect snapped automatically, because teenagers.

Asha went past, shoulders tight. Every few steps she caught her name in someone else’s mouth. Not shouted. Not confronted. Just the soft, delighted passing of it, like contraband.

“—that’s her.”  
“—she was there.”  
“—saw her message.”

In the corridor outside tutor, Josh Ward was holding court, phone in hand, grinning like he’d invented gossip.

“It’s on private now,” he told a cluster of Year 10s, too loud. “You have to follow me. Proper exclusive.”

Asha’s skin crawled. Mina was right. The scariest part wasn’t the midnight texts. It was that the story had become entertainment.

Connor appeared at her side and muttered, “Ignore him.”

Asha didn’t look at Connor. She couldn’t, not yet. She’d seen his face last night in the car park—angry, scared, protective—and she’d hated how much she wanted to hide behind it.

Mina was already at the end of the corridor, arms folded, leaning against the wall like she was holding the building up through pure spite. Theo wasn’t there.

Asha’s phone buzzed. Another DM.

Tilly: ppl are saying u lot pushed him. is that true??

Asha stared at the message until the words stopped meaning anything and became a shape: accusation.

She typed and deleted four different replies. *No* felt too thin. *It was an accident* felt like a lie, even if it wasn’t entirely.

She put the phone in her pocket and walked to Mina.

“Where’s Theo?” she asked.

Mina’s eyes slid to Asha. “Avoiding being alive.”

Connor said, “He texted me. His mum took his phone.”

Mina’s mouth twisted. “Well. That’ll solve it, then.”

Connor’s jaw tightened. “Can you not—”

Before Mina could reply, Mr Durnham appeared, clipboard in hand, wearing the expression of someone trying not to panic in front of children.

“Asha,” he said, too brightly. “Connor. Mina.” He glanced around. “Theo Mercer as well, when you see him.”

Asha’s stomach dropped. “What—”

“Don’t worry,” Mr Durnham said, which was how you knew you should worry. “We just need a quick chat. Come with me.”

They followed him down the corridor. Heads turned. Whispering followed. Asha felt like she was walking naked.

Mr Durnham led them into a small meeting room off the main office. There was a bowl of sweets on the table like someone had read a manual on How To Make Teenagers Confess and decided sugar was the key.

Mrs Kline, the safeguarding lead, sat waiting. Her face was kind in the way of someone who’d seen too much.

“Sit,” she said gently.

Asha sat. Connor sat. Mina stayed standing for a second, then sat with exaggerated reluctance.

Mr Durnham closed the door and stood by it like a bouncer.

Mrs Kline folded her hands. “We’ve had reports,” she began, “of distressing messages being circulated and of images connected to an incident last summer.”

Asha’s throat tightened. She kept her face blank. She could do blank. She’d practised.

Connor said, “It’s not us sending it.”

Mrs Kline nodded slowly. “We’re not here to accuse you of sending anything. We’re here to make sure everyone is safe. There are rumours of students being pressured to meet at the canal late at night.”

Mina snorted. “Canal’s got more security warnings than the nuclear code.”

Mr Durnham shot her a look. Mrs Kline didn’t react. She’d probably heard worse.

“Asha,” Mrs Kline said, voice gentle, “did you receive any messages?”

Asha’s instinct screamed: deny. Keep it contained. Don’t make it official. Official meant records. Records meant adults. Adults meant consequences that didn’t care about her intentions.

She heard Leah’s voice in her head: *For who?*

Asha swallowed. “Yes,” she said, quietly.

Connor’s head snapped towards her. Mina’s eyes flicked, interested despite herself.

Mrs Kline nodded. “Thank you for telling me. Can you show me?”

Asha’s hands shook as she pulled out her phone. The bowl of sweets sat between them like an offering.

She opened the texts. The emojis. The bridge photo. MEET WHERE YOU LEFT IT. YOU KEPT IT.

Mrs Kline’s expression tightened, but she kept her voice calm. “Who do you think is sending these?”

Asha’s mouth went dry. She could say Leah. She could say Rowan’s sister. She could point a finger and let the school do what the school did: contain, manage, protect itself.

Connor spoke before she could. “We don’t know.”

Mina said, flat, “Leah Bennett.”

Connor hissed, “Mina—”

Mina didn’t look at him. “It’s her. She wants us at the bridge at 00:03. She thinks we ‘stole’ something.”

Mrs Kline leaned forward. “Leah Bennett? Rowan Bennett’s sister?”

Asha felt her stomach drop. So they did know. They’d always known. It hadn’t been a secret. It had just been a thing adults filed away under *Handled.*

Mr Durnham cleared his throat. “Leah is not a student here,” he said quickly. “So it’s— it’s not within our—”

“Within your what?” Mina snapped. “Your jurisdiction? Your little email bubble?”

Connor’s voice was sharp. “Mina, stop.”

Mina’s eyes flashed at Connor. “Why? Because you’re scared of your dad? Because you’re scared of consequences? Newsflash: Rowan already got consequences.”

The room went very still.

Mrs Kline’s face softened, and that softness made Asha feel worse. “Mina,” she said quietly, “I understand you’re angry. But we need to keep this calm.”

Mina laughed under her breath. “Sure.”

Mrs Kline turned back to Asha. “Asha, can you tell me what ‘you kept it’ might refer to?”

Asha’s mind flashed: Theo’s old phone. The video. The drafted “what we say” message. Her own frantic text telling them not to ring.

Asha looked at the bowl of sweets. A fun-size Mars Bar sat on top, wrapper gleaming. The absurdity of it made her throat burn.

Connor said, “Nothing. It’s just— it’s just to scare us.”

Mrs Kline held Connor’s gaze. “Connor. I need honesty.”

Connor’s jaw worked. He looked suddenly seventeen in a room with adults, the bravado stripped away. “It’s about… last summer,” he said. “About Rowan.”

Mr Durnham’s shoulders stiffened. “We’re not revisiting—”

Mina cut in, voice low. “We never visited it properly in the first place.”

Asha’s chest tightened. In her head she saw the canal path, the flash of phone lights, Rowan on the ground making a sound like he was trying not to scream, Connor swearing, Theo filming, Mina yelling to ring someone, Asha saying *don’t ring, don’t ring* because she could already see the headlines and the parents and the police.

She’d thought she was being responsible. She’d been being scared.

Mrs Kline said, “Did you go to the canal last night?”

Asha shook her head. “No.”

Connor nodded. “No.”

Mina hesitated, then said, “No.”

Mrs Kline exhaled. “Good. You must not go. If you feel threatened, if you are being blackmailed or harassed, we need to involve the police.”

Connor’s face tightened. “No.”

Mrs Kline blinked. “Connor—”

Connor’s voice was strained. “My dad’s a police officer.”

Mr Durnham looked relieved, as if this solved something. “Well then,” he said, “we can handle this quietly—”

Mina’s head snapped around. “Quietly. There it is.”

Mr Durnham flushed. “I didn’t mean—”

Mina stood, chair scraping. “You always mean quietly.”

Mrs Kline’s voice sharpened slightly. “Mina, sit down.”

Mina stayed standing. “No. Because I’m not doing this again.”

Asha stared at Mina, heart hammering. Again. Mina had been the one last summer who’d wanted to ring. Asha remembered the look Mina had given her in the dark: betrayal, confusion, anger. A look Asha had avoided ever since by pretending it hadn’t happened.

Mrs Kline said, “I’m going to call your parents. All of your parents. There will be a meeting.”

Connor’s head jerked up. “You can’t.”

Mrs Kline’s expression hardened gently. “I can. This is safeguarding.”

Asha’s stomach dropped into her shoes. Parents. Meetings. Official.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket, like it was laughing at her.

Unknown number: 🩸🩸🩸

Then, a message.

MIDNIGHT. ONE CHANCE.

Asha’s mouth went dry. She pulled the phone out under the table, hiding it with her thigh like a delinquent.

Unknown: VIDEO + CHAT.

Unknown: OR I SEND EVERYTHING.

Unknown: INCLUDING THE PART YOU EDITED OUT.

Asha’s vision blurred. Edited out. She hadn’t edited anything. Had she? Had she convinced herself she hadn’t?

Her mind raced, grasping. There had been a moment—brief—where Rowan had tried to get up and someone had shoved him back down, laughing. Not hard. Not meant to hurt. Just—

Asha’s breath caught. She’d never told herself that part clearly. She’d always filed it under *chaos,* under *not my fault,* under *accident.*

Connor’s voice cut through. “Asha? You alright?”

Asha looked up. Everyone was looking at her.

Mrs Kline said, “Asha?”

Asha forced a smile that felt like cracking glass. “Yeah,” she said. “Just… stressed.”

Mrs Kline nodded as if that was the whole story. “We will handle this,” she said. “Do not respond. Do not engage. We will speak to the police liaison and—”

Mina laughed, sharp. “Police liaison. Like it’s a careers advisor.”

Connor said, “Can we go now?”

Mr Durnham opened the door, eager to end the discomfort.

They spilled back into the corridor. The air felt colder out here, like the school had exhaled them.

Connor grabbed Asha’s elbow. “What did it say?” he whispered. “You went weird.”

Asha pulled her arm away too quickly. “Nothing.”

Mina’s eyes narrowed. “It said something.”

Asha’s throat tightened. She could lie. She could keep controlling. That was what she did.

But Leah’s question echoed: *For who?*

Asha looked at Connor and Mina, then down the corridor where students flowed around them like water around stones, all of them with phones, all of them with mouths.

“It wants the video,” she said, voice low. “And the chat. And—” She swallowed. “It says there’s a part we edited out.”

Theo wasn’t there to hear it. Maybe that was for the best. Maybe it was for the worst.

Connor’s face went pale. “What part?”

Asha opened her mouth. The memory hovered, slippery and sharp.

Mina’s voice was quiet. “The part where it wasn’t an accident.”

Asha flinched. “It was an accident.”

Mina held her gaze. “Was it?”

Asha couldn’t answer. Because she didn’t know which version was true anymore, or if she’d ever known.

Connor’s phone buzzed. He glanced at it and swore under his breath. “My dad’s calling,” he said.

Mina’s laugh was brittle. “Perfect.”

Asha looked at her own phone again. The message sat there, patient.

MIDNIGHT. ONE CHANCE.

Asha could feel the day closing in around that point, like everything was being pulled towards it.

Daylight didn’t fix it. It just showed more clearly how many people were watching. And how little control she had left.

## Chapter 6 — Midnight Is a Stupid Time to Be Brave

Asha got home to find her mum sitting at the kitchen table with her phone in her hand like it was a weapon.

Asha froze in the doorway. Her blazer was damp from rain that wasn’t quite rain, just air that had decided to be wet. She could taste metal in her mouth, like she’d been chewing coins.

Mum looked up. Her face did that thing where it went very still before anger arrived, like the pause in a microwave before it beeps.

“Sit,” Mum said.

Asha’s stomach dropped. “What—”

“Sit,” Mum repeated, sharper. “Now.”

Asha sat.

On the table was a printed screenshot. Actual paper. Someone had gone to the effort of using a printer in 2025. The absurdity of it made Asha want to laugh and cry at the same time.

The screenshot was of Asha’s message from last summer.

Asha: don’t ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE

Under it, in someone’s handwriting, was 🩸🩸🩸, like a curse.

Mum tapped the paper. “Explain.”

Asha’s throat tightened so hard it felt like swallowing glass. “Where did you get that?”

Mum’s eyes flashed. “School rang. They said there are ‘online rumours’. They said your name is being mentioned. And then Mrs Patel from the shop next door sent me this because her niece is in your year and apparently everyone thinks it’s fine to share other people’s messages like sweets.”

Asha’s hands curled under the table. “It’s taken out of context.”

Mum stared at her. “Context?”

Asha swallowed. She could do the safe version. She could say: prank. Blackmail. Someone’s being weird. She could avoid Rowan’s name.

But Mrs Kline was calling parents. The school was already in it. The truth was going to arrive at Mum’s door anyway; Asha could either control how or be steamrollered.

“It was last summer,” Asha said, voice small. “There was an accident.”

Mum’s gaze sharpened. “The canal?”

Asha flinched. Mum had been listening. The radio. The town. Of course Mum knew.

Asha nodded.

Mum’s face changed, pain flickering through the anger. “Rowan?”

Asha’s breath caught. Hearing his name here, in their kitchen, made her want to curl into the cupboard under the sink.

“Yes.”

Mum looked away, jaw working, and then looked back with fury. “And you told them not to ring anyone?”

Asha’s eyes stung. “I didn’t— I didn’t want anyone to get in trouble.”

Mum’s laugh was short and ugly. “You didn’t want *you* to get in trouble.”

Asha opened her mouth. Closed it. Because yes. Because she’d been thinking about consequences like they were a disease you avoided by disinfecting the evidence.

Mum leaned forward. “Are the police involved?”

“No,” Asha said quickly. “Not— not yet.”

Mum’s eyes narrowed. “Not yet.”

Asha’s phone buzzed in her blazer pocket. She didn’t move.

Mum held out her hand. “Give me your phone.”

Asha’s heart lurched. “Mum—”

“Give,” Mum said, and her voice was the voice that had made Asha eat vegetables and apologise when she didn’t mean it and stop walking home with boys who catcalled her. It was not a negotiable voice.

Asha stared at her phone like it was a limb she was about to lose. Her mind flashed: unknown number, emojis, screenshots, midnight, Leah. If Mum took her phone, Asha would lose the only warning system she had. She would be blind.

“I need it,” Asha said. “For— for schoolwork.”

Mum’s mouth twisted. “You can do your schoolwork on paper like God intended. Phone. Now.”

Asha’s hand shook as she pulled it out and placed it on the table. Mum flipped it over, face down, and Asha felt strangely exposed, like she’d just handed over her skin.

Mum stood. “You are not going out tonight,” she said.

Asha’s stomach dropped. “Mum, I wasn’t—”

Mum cut her off. “You were. I know you. You were going to go and ‘sort it’. You are a child, Asha. You don’t sort things like this at midnight. You come to me.”

Asha looked up, eyes burning. “And what? You ring the school and they ring the police and everyone thinks I’m a monster?”

Mum’s face tightened. “If you did something wrong, you face it.”

Asha flinched. “We didn’t— we didn’t push him.”

Mum’s gaze held hers. “Did you leave him there?”

Asha’s breath caught. She remembered the towpath, Rowan’s face in phone light, the moment they moved away because someone said they’d heard a car, the fear that they’d be caught, the ridiculous thought of detention in the middle of emergency.

“No,” she lied.

Mum’s shoulders sagged a fraction, like she didn’t believe her but didn’t want to break her. “Go upstairs,” Mum said quietly. “I’m ringing Mrs Kline back.”

Asha stood on legs that felt like borrowed stilts and went to her room.

Upstairs, she sat on her bed and stared at the wall. Without her phone, the room felt too quiet. She could hear Mum’s voice downstairs, low and urgent, the clink of cups.

Asha’s laptop sat on her desk. She opened it and logged into Instagram like a starving person. Her feed was a mess of blood emoji jokes and people pretending to be horrified while liking everything.

She searched Leah’s name.

leahb__

Private.

The profile picture was a blurry shot of two kids: Leah and Rowan, younger, faces pressed together, laughing. Rowan’s grin was wide and stupid and alive.

Asha’s throat tightened.

A ping: a message request. On her laptop. Leah had found a way around the phone.

leahb__: midnight. one chance.

Asha’s hands went cold.

leahb__: video + chat.

Asha typed without thinking.

Asha: i don’t have the video

The reply came instantly.

leahb__: you don’t need to have it to know it exists.

Asha’s stomach lurched.

leahb__: if you don’t bring it, i send everything. to school. to parents. to police. to whoever wants a story.

Asha’s fingers hovered. She could type an apology. She could type a threat. She could type *please*.

Instead she typed:

Asha: what do you want

There was a pause long enough for Asha to imagine Leah staring at her screen, jaw clenched, deciding whether to be human or cruel.

Then:

leahb__: i want you to stop hiding.

Asha’s eyes stung.

leahb__: i want someone to say what actually happened. not “accident” like it’s weather.

Asha’s throat tightened. She typed, because she couldn’t not.

Asha: it was an accident

Three dots appeared. Vanished. Appeared again.

leahb__: was it?

Asha’s breath caught. The shove. The laugh. The moment that had been filed away under chaos.

Downstairs, Mum’s voice rose slightly. “No, I’m telling you, she’s not leaving the house—”

Asha’s blood went cold. She imagined Mrs Kline’s calm voice, Mr Durnham’s clipboard, Connor’s dad’s uniform.

Asha slammed the laptop shut like it had bitten her.

She sat there, shaking. Midnight. One chance. She couldn’t go. Mum had her phone. Mum would lock the door if she had to.

Asha stared at her window. The garden gate below. The alley that led to the side street. She hated herself for immediately calculating exits. She wasn’t brave. She was panicked.

A tap at the window made her jump so hard she nearly fell off the bed.

She froze.

Another tap. Then a whisper, muffled through glass. “Asha. Open up.”

Connor.

Asha yanked the curtain aside. Connor stood in the garden, hood up, face pale in the security light, looking like a boy about to do something stupid.

Asha cracked the window. Cold air rushed in.

“What are you doing?” she hissed.

Connor’s breath puffed in little clouds. “You’re not answering. Thought you’d been murdered by your mum.”

Asha’s throat tightened. “She took my phone.”

Connor’s eyes widened. “Christ.”

Asha shook her head. “You can’t be here.”

Connor glanced at the kitchen window downstairs. “Is she—”

“On the phone,” Asha whispered.

Connor leaned closer. “We have to go tonight.”

Asha’s stomach dropped. “No. Connor, no.”

He swallowed. The bravado was gone. “Theo’s… he’s gone weird. Mina’s fuming. Leah’s not stopping. And my dad— my dad’s sniffing round. If my dad finds out from someone else, it’ll be worse.”

Asha stared at him. “Why are you here?”

Connor’s laugh was shaky. “Because you’re the one who thinks in straight lines when the rest of us are just… flailing.”

Asha swallowed. “That’s not a compliment.”

Connor’s eyes held hers. “Come with us.”

Asha’s chest hurt. “Mum won’t let me.”

Connor’s mouth tightened. “Then sneak.”

Asha stared at him. The idea of climbing out of her bedroom window like a Victorian orphan made her want to laugh, but her body was too full of adrenaline for laughter to fit.

Asha whispered, “Mina?”

Connor nodded towards the gate. “She’s round the corner. Like a bat. Judging me.”

Asha’s throat tightened. She glanced at the door. Mum’s voice downstairs, still talking. Asha imagined staying. Staying safe. Staying quiet.

Then she imagined waking up to her whole life on someone’s story, edited into a villain montage with emojis.

Her hands shook as she opened the window wider.

“Okay,” she whispered. “Give me two minutes.”

Connor nodded. “Bring a coat. And—” His mouth twisted. “Sorry. For earlier.”

Asha swallowed. “Me too.”

She climbed out onto the small flat roof over the shop front, the cold seeping through her socks. She lowered herself into the garden with more noise than she’d like. Connor caught her elbow, steadying her. His hand was warm.

Mina appeared from the shadow by the gate. Hood up, eyes bright. “Took you long enough,” she murmured.

Asha’s voice shook. “Theo?”

Mina’s mouth tightened. “He’s not answering. His mum took his phone too. Or he’s ignoring us. Either way.”

Connor exhaled. “We can’t go without him.”

Mina’s eyes flashed. “We can’t not go because of him.”

Asha felt the word *midnight* pressing down on her skull. A stupid time to be brave. A stupid time to be anything except asleep.

But they were moving anyway, three figures slipping out of a garden into a town that looked normal and wasn’t.

As they walked, Asha’s laptop notification buzzed in her room upstairs, unseen.

Midnight was coming, with or without her. And she had the sick feeling that the “one chance” wasn’t Leah being dramatic.

It was Leah being accurate.

## Chapter 7 — The Worst Version of You, On Someone Else’s Screen

When Asha got home in the early hours—mud on her shoes, cold in her bones, Connor’s frantic “mate mate mate” still echoing in her head—her mum was waiting in the kitchen like a final boss.

She didn’t shout.

That was how Asha knew she’d crossed a line that didn’t lead back to normal.

Mum sat at the table with Asha’s phone in front of her and a mug of tea that had gone skin-cold.

Asha stopped in the doorway. “Mum—”

Mum held up a hand. “Don’t.”

Asha’s throat tightened. She could still taste canal air: damp stone, algae, old cigarettes. Her hair smelled like rain and panic.

Mum said, very quietly, “Where were you.”

Asha opened her mouth and felt the lie rise like a reflex. Library. Mina’s. Walk. Anywhere but—

“I went out,” Asha said, voice shaking.

Mum’s eyes were wet. That made Asha’s chest hurt more than anger would have.

“I was on the phone to Mrs Kline,” Mum said. “And to your auntie. And I was sitting here like an idiot thinking, at least she’s upstairs, at least she’s safe, at least I can keep her in the house.”

Asha swallowed. “I’m sorry.”

Mum laughed, sharp and broken. “Sorry? Do you know what sorry is for? Spilled milk. Not… this.”

Asha’s hands curled into fists. “I didn’t mean for any of this to happen.”

Mum’s gaze snapped up. “But it did. And you made choices.”

Asha’s stomach twisted. Choices. That word again. Like it was a thing you could measure.

Mum picked up Asha’s phone and slid it across the table. “Read.”

Asha’s breath caught. On the screen was an email from school, sent to parents at 01:17.

Subject: Important: Safeguarding Concern

The body was polished and bland: we are aware, we are investigating, we are supporting, please monitor your child’s internet use. The words were designed to contain.

Below it, a WhatsApp message from Connor’s mum to Mum.

Connor’s Mum: did u hear abt the video??? they say ash told them not to call ambulance?? is she ok?? x

Asha’s vision blurred. Her name was now a rumour adults passed like a casserole dish.

Mum’s voice was flat. “There is a meeting at school today. Ten o’clock. You are coming.”

Asha’s throat tightened. “Mum, I have—”

“Exams?” Mum snapped, finally. “Your exams will still be there. This will not go away because you have a maths paper.”

Asha flinched. “Okay.”

Mum stared at her for a long moment, then said, softer, “Go upstairs. Sleep. We’ll talk in the morning.”

Asha went upstairs and lay in bed staring at the ceiling until the sky began to grey. She didn’t sleep so much as fall into a shallow, twitchy half-state where every time she closed her eyes she saw phone flash on Rowan’s face.

At 08:03, her phone buzzed.

Connor: you awake

Asha: yeah

Connor: you ok

Asha stared at the words. *You ok* was a joke. A meaningless social tick. But Connor meant it as much as he could.

Asha: no

Connor: same

Asha’s chest tightened.

Connor: i told my dad last night

Asha’s stomach dropped. She sat up, dizzy.

Asha: what

Connor: not all. but enough. he saw the stories. he was gonna find out anyway

Asha typed with shaking fingers.

Asha: what did he say

There was a pause. Then:

Connor: he said “hand your phone over” in that voice

Asha swallowed.

Connor: meeting at school. my mum’s crying. which is grim

Asha stared at the chat. It felt like being trapped in a lift with the floor numbers flashing too fast.

At 09:12, a new Instagram story popped up from an account called TowpathTruth (private, 0 posts yesterday, 6 posts today). Someone had followed half the year group overnight.

Mina: have you seen this

Asha clicked.

The story was a screenshot of another message from last summer’s group chat. This one wasn’t even dramatic. It was just—cruel.

Connor: lol he’s fine. he’s just being dramatic

Under it: 🩸🩸🩸

Next slide: a still from the video—Rowan on the ground, people’s shoes visible around him, someone laughing. It wasn’t gore. It was worse: ordinary. The kind of clip people filmed and forgot. Until it became evidence.

Next slide: a poll.

“should they get expelled? yes/no”

Asha’s throat tightened. She felt sick. The story wasn’t about justice; it was about attention. Votes. Engagement. Entertainment.

Her phone buzzed again.

Mina: i swear to god if that’s josh ward i’m putting him in a bin

Asha: meeting at 10

Mina: i know. i’m coming. i’m not letting them make you the scapegoat.

The words made Asha’s eyes sting.

At 10:00, the school meeting room smelled of cheap coffee and panic. Asha sat beside her mum, who wore her shop jumper because she’d come straight from opening up, hair scraped back, jaw set.

Across the table sat Mrs Kline, Mr Durnham, the Headteacher, and—unexpectedly—PC Hayes. Connor’s dad. Uniform off, but the posture remained.

Connor sat next to his mum, shoulders hunched, eyes fixed on the table like if he didn’t look up, no one would see him.

Mina sat alone, no parent in sight, chin raised like she’d come to court voluntarily.

Theo wasn’t there.

The Headteacher began with the kind of voice people used at funerals and awards ceremonies. “Thank you all for coming. I want to reassure you—”

Mina muttered, “Here we go.”

Asha’s mum shot her a look, not unkindly, more like *same, honestly.*

Mrs Kline said, “We are concerned about the wellbeing of several students. There has been harassment. Threats. There are also serious allegations relating to last summer’s incident.”

PC Hayes leaned forward. “I’m here because there’s potential criminality,” he said bluntly. He didn’t look at Connor. He looked at Asha.

Asha’s skin went cold.

Connor’s mum said, voice trembling, “My son didn’t do anything on purpose.”

The Headteacher smiled tightly. “We are not here to apportion blame at this stage.”

Mina laughed once. “You’re always at this stage.”

Mr Durnham bristled. “Mina—”

Mina’s eyes flashed. “No. Because you keep saying ‘allegations’ like Rowan wasn’t actually on the floor.”

Asha’s breath caught. Rowan’s name in this room, spoken like a fact, not a rumour. It hit her like a wave.

PC Hayes’s gaze sharpened. “Rowan Bennett,” he said. “The case was closed as an accident.”

Asha’s stomach twisted. Closed. Filed. Put away. Like her own notes app.

Mum’s hand found Asha’s knee under the table, gripping, grounding.

PC Hayes continued, “If there is new evidence—video, messages—we need to see it.”

Connor’s head jerked up. “No, you don’t,” he blurted. “It’s— it’s private.”

Asha watched Connor’s dad look at him, and something in Connor seemed to fold.

PC Hayes said, quieter, “Connor.”

Connor’s eyes filled. He blinked hard. “I didn’t mean—”

The Headteacher cleared her throat. “We also need to consider the impact of this on the school community.”

Mina’s laugh was bitter. “There it is. Impact on the school.”

Asha felt something inside her snap, small but clean.

The school was still trying to contain. To protect itself. To manage optics. And if Asha let them, they’d pick the easiest person to blame: the girl whose message said don’t ring anyone. The girl who looked responsible enough to be guilty.

She pulled her phone out. Mum’s grip tightened, then loosened, letting her choose.

“I have it,” Asha said, voice shaking.

Everyone turned to her.

Asha’s heart hammered. She forced the words out before she could swallow them again. “I have the messages. I have the texts from Leah. I don’t have the video, but I know it exists. And I—” Her breath caught. “I delayed calling for help. I told them not to ring. That’s my message.”

Mum made a small sound beside her, like pain.

Connor stared at Asha like she’d slapped him.

The Headteacher’s face went very still. Mrs Kline’s eyes softened. PC Hayes’s gaze sharpened.

Asha continued, voice cracking. “And we… we agreed what to say afterwards. We deleted things. We tried to make it smaller.”

Mina’s chin trembled once, then steadied.

PC Hayes said, “You understand that’s serious.”

Asha nodded, throat tight. “Yes.”

Connor’s voice came out rough. “Why are you doing this?”

Asha looked at him. “Because it’s already out,” she said, and her voice went sharp with something that wasn’t fear now. “And because I’m not letting Josh Ward and some anonymous account decide what the story is.”

The Headteacher said, quickly, “Asha, perhaps we should—”

“No,” Asha said. The word surprised her, how solid it sounded. “I’m giving a statement. Properly. Not… not through rumours.”

Mina exhaled, a sound like relief and grief mixed. “Good.”

Connor’s mum began to cry properly then, hands over her mouth.

PC Hayes nodded once, brisk. “Alright,” he said. “We’ll do this correctly.”

Asha felt sick. She felt lightheaded. She felt like she’d stepped off a cliff and only now noticed there was no ground.

Outside the meeting room, the corridor was full of students pretending not to look. Eyes flicked. Whispers followed.

Connor caught up with Asha near the stairs. His face was blotchy, eyes red, jaw tight.

“What have you done?” he hissed.

Asha’s throat tightened. “I told the truth.”

Connor’s laugh was sharp and broken. “Your truth. You’ve just— you’ve just made it all about you.”

Asha flinched. “No, I—”

Connor stepped back. “You always do that,” he said, voice low. “You always decide what’s best and then act like it’s responsibility.”

Asha’s chest hurt. “Connor—”

He shook his head, eyes wet. “Don’t.”

He turned and walked away into the crowd.

Asha stood there, shaking, watching his back disappear, feeling the cost arrive in her body like a bruise.

Her phone buzzed.

A new message request from TowpathTruth.

No words.

Just: 🩸

One drop now.

And for the first time, it didn’t feel like a threat.

It felt like a receipt.
