## Chapter 8 — After

The school’s email went out at 16:42, because that was the hour the school liked best for bad news: late enough that teachers could go home, early enough that parents would still be awake and furious.

Subject: Update: Student Welfare and Online Content

Asha read it three times and still couldn’t tell what it said, because it didn’t say anything.

It thanked families for their patience. It reminded everyone about “appropriate online conduct.” It mentioned “support” and “counselling” and “working with external agencies.” It did not mention Rowan’s name. It did not mention what had happened last summer. It did not mention why a blood emoji had been stuck to every surface in Year Eleven like an infestation.

It was, in other words, perfect.

Mum read it once, snorted, and said, “They’ve written that so it can be used again next year.”

Asha lay on her bed in her uniform, shoes kicked off, staring at the ceiling.

Her phone was back in her possession now, but it didn’t feel like freedom. It felt like holding a live wire. Notifications stacked like plates. DMs from people who’d never spoken to her before. Missed calls from Connor that had stopped abruptly at lunchtime and never restarted.

Mina was the only person who’d texted something that wasn’t a question or an accusation.

Mina: i’m coming round later. don’t argue.

Asha didn’t argue. She didn’t have the energy to be stubborn.

Her mum moved around the flat with the purposeful anger of someone cleaning to avoid crying. She wiped the kitchen counter twice. She reorganised the tea bags. She told Asha she’d made dal even though Asha hadn’t asked.

At seven, Mum sat on the edge of Asha’s bed and handed her a plate. Asha ate because the alternative was her body doing something dramatic in a way that would inconvenience everyone.

After dinner, Mum said, “The police called.”

Asha’s stomach dropped. “PC Hayes?”

Mum nodded. “Not him. Another one. A woman. She asked if you’d be willing to make a formal statement.”

Asha swallowed. “I already said I would.”

Mum’s eyes were tired. “Do you understand what that means?”

Asha stared at her hands. “It means… it’s real.”

Mum exhaled. “It means it will follow you for a long time. It means people will be angry. It means you might be punished.”

Asha’s throat tightened. “I know.”

Mum’s voice softened. “Why did you do it then?”

Asha opened her mouth. She could say: because it was right. She could say: because Leah forced me. She could say: because I was scared.

The truth was all of them, tangled.

“Because I’m sick of lying,” Asha whispered. “And because everyone’s already decided what happened without asking. I wanted… I wanted it to be the real version, not the TikTok version.”

Mum’s face tightened with something like grief. “The real version is going to hurt.”

Asha nodded. “It already did.”

Mum sat for a moment longer, then stood. At the door she paused. “Mina’s good,” she said, as if remembering. “She’s always been good.”

Asha’s throat tightened. “Yeah.”

Mum left.

Asha lay back and stared at the ceiling again. She could hear the shop fridge humming downstairs through the floorboards, steady and indifferent. It had hummed through last summer too. It would hum through whatever came next.

At 20:13, there was a knock at the door. Asha heard Mum answer, heard Mina’s voice—low, blunt, familiar.

Mina came into Asha’s room without asking, dropped her tote bag on the floor, and sat on the edge of the bed like she owned it. She looked exhausted, eyeliner slightly smudged for the first time Asha could remember.

Asha tried for humour. “You look like you’ve fought a bear.”

Mina snorted. “I fought a school. Bears are honest.”

Asha’s laugh came out weak. Mina looked at her, and her expression softened in a way that made Asha’s eyes sting.

“You did it,” Mina said.

Asha’s throat tightened. “Yeah.”

Mina nodded once, as if confirming something she’d already decided. “Connor’s furious.”

Asha flinched. “I know.”

Mina leaned back, arms folded. “He’s not wrong to be. But he’s also… Connor. He thinks if he can keep it contained, it doesn’t count.”

Asha stared at her duvet. “I didn’t want to make him the villain.”

Mina’s voice sharpened. “Then stop acting like you’re the only person who can decide what everyone is.”

Asha looked up. Mina held her gaze, not cruelly, just… accurately.

Asha swallowed. “Fine. I did it because I didn’t want to be the villain. I wanted it to be… a clean story.”

Mina’s mouth twisted. “There are no clean stories. Only stories people wash and then sell.”

Asha let out a shaky breath. “TowpathTruth is still posting.”

Mina rolled her eyes. “Of course they are.”

Asha reached for her phone, hesitated, then opened Instagram. TowpathTruth had added more slides since morning.

Screenshots, captions, polls. People voting on what should happen to them like it was Love Island.

Asha felt sick. “It’s not even— they don’t care about Rowan. They care about—”

“Content,” Mina said, flat. “Welcome to the internet. It’s a graveyard with ads.”

Asha scrolled and stopped. New story. Different account.

leahb__ (private)

Asha’s heart lurched.

She hadn’t accepted Leah’s request. She hadn’t blocked her either. She’d just… existed in that tense middle space.

Asha tapped.

It wasn’t a screenshot. It wasn’t a threat.

It was a black screen with white text, like someone had typed it into Notes and posted it raw.

i shouldn’t have done it like this.  
i’m sorry for scaring you.  
but you don’t get to forget him.

Then, a second slide:

he has physio tomorrow. 9am.  
he hates the green building.  
he says it smells like soup.

Asha stared. Her throat tightened.

Mina leaned in, reading over Asha’s shoulder. Mina’s expression changed—just a flicker. Sadness, maybe. Or something like recognition.

Asha whispered, “That’s… Rowan.”

Mina nodded. “Yeah.”

Asha’s hands shook. “So it was Leah.”

Mina’s mouth tightened. “Probably. Or someone using her name. But…” She shrugged. “It sounds like her.”

Asha stared at the screen until it dimmed. The soup smell. The green building. A detail so stupid and human it hurt more than any blood emoji.

Mina said, “What did actually happen that night?”

Asha’s breath caught. She’d said it to adults in a meeting, in careful, clipped phrases, but Mina was asking differently. Not for a statement. For truth between friends.

Asha swallowed. “We were at that party,” she began, voice thin. “By the towpath. Someone had a speaker. Theo was filming everything because he films everything. Connor was… Connor.”

Mina didn’t interrupt. She didn’t fill silence. She just watched.

Asha continued, words stumbling. “Rowan was there because Leah was working and— and he wanted to seem older. He was trying to keep up.” Her throat tightened. “He climbed on the wall by the bridge. We were laughing. Not… not at him, just— you know. And then he slipped. Or—”

Asha closed her eyes. The memory shifted, refusing to be neat. “Someone bumped him,” she whispered. “Like a joke. Like, ‘move over’.”

Mina’s jaw clenched.

Asha opened her eyes. “I don’t know if it was a shove. I don’t know if it was enough to count as… anything. But he went. He went down. He hit the concrete. And he made a sound and everyone went quiet for half a second and then—”

She swallowed hard. “Connor said he was being dramatic. And I… I said don’t ring anyone.”

Mina’s voice was hoarse. “Why?”

Asha stared at her hands. “Because I thought if we called, we’d be in trouble. And if we were in trouble, it would be… the end.”

Mina’s eyes glistened. “And if we didn’t call?”

Asha’s breath caught. “I thought he’d get up. I thought… it would undo itself.”

Mina let out a shaky breath. “It didn’t.”

“No,” Asha whispered. “It didn’t.”

For a moment, there was nothing but the hum of the fridge downstairs and the distant sound of a car passing on wet roads.

Mina wiped her eye with the heel of her hand like she was annoyed at it. “You’re going to give your statement,” she said, more firmly, as if decision-making could be a raft.

Asha nodded. “Yeah.”

Mina’s voice tightened. “And when they try to make you the only villain, you don’t let them. You don’t let them do what they always do. Find one person and let everyone else sigh in relief.”

Asha’s throat tightened. “Connor will hate me.”

Mina’s expression hardened. “Connor’s not the most important person in this story.”

The words stung because they were true. Asha nodded anyway, because she didn’t know what else to do.

Mina stood, picked up her tote bag. “I have to go,” she said. “My mum will start ringing hospitals if I’m late.”

Asha gave a weak laugh. “Fair.”

At the door, Mina paused. “I’m not saying you’re forgiven,” she said, blunt. “I’m saying you’re here. Which is more than some people can manage.”

Asha’s eyes stung. “Thanks,” she whispered.

Mina left.

Asha lay back on her bed and stared at her phone. The notifications didn’t stop. The world didn’t pause to let her breathe.

Just after midnight, her phone buzzed.

Asha’s body went rigid, every nerve lit.

Not TowpathTruth. Not Leah. Not Connor.

A notification from Photos.

1 year ago today.

A thumbnail: blurred lights in darkness. A crowd. The red crown under the bridge, bright as fresh paint.

Asha’s throat tightened until she could barely swallow. Her finger hovered. She could delete the memory. She could hide it. She could do what she always did.

Instead, she stared at it, breathing through the panic, letting it exist.

Her phone buzzed again. A message request.

Unknown number.

Asha’s heart hammered as she opened it.

No emojis this time.

Just: you did the right thing.

Below it, a second line:

i’m still angry.

Asha’s eyes stung. She didn’t know whether it was Leah. She didn’t know whether it mattered.

She typed, slowly, thumbs steadying.

Asha: i’m sorry.

Then, because apologies without truth were just decoration, she added:

Asha: i won’t pretend it didn’t happen anymore.

She sent it.

The ticks didn’t turn blue. There was no reply.

Outside, the town stayed dark and damp and ordinary. The shop fridge hummed. Somewhere, a fox screamed again, as if to remind her that the world was still full of raw, living noise.

Asha turned her phone face down.

This time, it wasn’t to ignore it.

It was to let herself, for one moment, exist without watching the screen for blood.

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter's emotional architecture is well-constructed. The school email opening is genuinely sharp — "It did not mention Rowan's name. It did not mention what had happened last summer. It did not mention why a blood emoji had been stuck to every surface in Year Eleven like an infestation. It was, in other words, perfect." This is the darkly comic British YA voice working at its best: the irony is earned, not performed.

The Mina scene is the chapter's strongest section. The dialogue is largely excellent — "I fought a school. Bears are honest" is a good line, and "There are no clean stories. Only stories people wash and then sell" is sharp without being too aphoristic. Mina's challenge to Asha — "stop acting like you're the only person who can decide what everyone is" — is character-consistent and dramatically useful. The relationship between them feels real and earned.

The Leah Instagram story is a genuinely creative choice. "he says it smells like soup" is the kind of specific, mundane detail that does more emotional work than any dramatic revelation could. This is the chapter plan's instruction to keep Rowan real without making him a plot device, executed well. The soup detail lands.

The confession scene — Asha telling Mina what actually happened — is handled with appropriate messiness. "Someone bumped him. Like a joke. Like, 'move over'" captures the horrible ambiguity of the incident without resolving it neatly. The refusal to be definitive ("I don't know if it was a shove") is honest and effective.

The closing sequence — the Photos memory notification, the unknown message — is restrained and earns its emotional weight. "you did the right thing / i'm still angry" is a perfect two-line encapsulation of Leah's character and the story's refusal of easy resolution.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter occasionally tips into telling rather than showing, particularly in the emotional beats. "Asha's throat tightened" appears four times in the chapter, which is a significant repetition problem. Similarly, "Asha's eyes stung" appears twice. These are stock physical responses that signal emotion rather than embodying it. The plan called for showing subtext do work, but these moments reach for shorthand.

The fox screaming at the end — "a fox screamed again, as if to remind her that the world was still full of raw, living noise" — is the chapter's weakest moment. The "as if to remind her" construction is exactly the kind of forced interpretive metaphor that the scoring criteria flags. The fox has appeared earlier in the story (per the plan's dead-end scene), so its return has some logic, but the explanatory gloss ruins it. Let the fox scream; don't explain what it means.

The fridge hum is used twice as a structural anchor, which works the first time and becomes slightly mechanical the second. "It had hummed through last summer too. It would hum through whatever came next" is a little too neat in its temporal symmetry.

The mum scene is competent but slightly underpowered. The dialogue is functional rather than distinctive. "The real version is going to hurt" / "It already did" is the kind of exchange that sounds like a YA novel rather than a real conversation — slightly too polished, too aphoristic.

The chapter follows the plan closely and hits most of its beats: the school email, Mina's visit, the glimpse of Rowan's reality (physio, green building, soup smell), Leah's partial acknowledgment, the closing midnight notification. The plan called for "one relationship ends; one shifts into something quieter; one remains unresolved" — Connor's silence is the unresolved one, Mina's is the shifted one, but the "ended" relationship isn't clearly present. This is a minor gap.

The dark comedy is present but lighter in this chapter than earlier ones, which is appropriate for a denouement — the chapter earns its shift in register.

Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Asha's self-awareness about her own motives ("I did it because I didn't want to be the villain") is exactly right. Mina's bluntness-with-care is well-rendered. Connor's absence (furious silence) is characteristically him.

Overall: a well-executed final chapter with some repetitive emotional shorthand and one or two moments where the prose reaches for significance rather than earning it. The soup detail alone is worth significant credit.

chapter_raw_judge_text.5:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CRAFT

The narrative voice is consistently Asha's: observational, dry, anxious, and self-aware. Lines like "the school had decided it was having an incident" and the prefect "who'd discovered authority last week and was drunk on it" are sharp and earned. The Josh Ward detail—"grinning like he'd invented gossip"—is economical and precise. The bowl of sweets as a detail ("like someone had read a manual on How To Make Teenagers Confess and decided sugar was the key") is genuinely funny and tonally right for the genre.

The prose is mostly clean and controlled. There are occasional moments where the metaphors strain slightly—"a smile that felt like cracking glass" is a touch overwrought, and "her stomach dropped into her shoes" is a cliché—but these are minor. The writing generally avoids purple prose and earns its darker moments.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. Mina's lines are particularly well-calibrated: "Within your what? Your little email bubble?" and "You always mean quietly" feel authentic to her character profile and carry genuine thematic weight without being on-the-nose. Connor's "My dad's a police officer" landing as both explanation and deflection is nicely handled. Mr Durnham's relief at the mention of Connor's dad—"Well then, we can handle this quietly"—is a small, devastating institutional moment.

The dialogue does occasionally tip toward characters articulating the theme too cleanly. Mina's final line, "The part where it wasn't an accident," is effective but slightly too neat—it does the thematic work too explicitly, telling us what the chapter has been building toward rather than letting it remain in the subtext. Similarly, "We never visited it properly in the first place" is a little on-the-nose.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

All four characters behave consistently with their profiles. Connor's defensive loyalty and his instinct to contain ("No" to police) is well-rendered. Mina's moral absolutism and her refusal to sit down is exactly right. Asha's internal conflict between narrative control and truth is the chapter's emotional spine and is handled with real skill—particularly the moment where she realizes she's been filing a specific memory under "chaos" rather than confronting it. Theo's absence is used well; his non-presence creates its own pressure.

Leah appears only through the texts, which is appropriate for this chapter. The texts themselves ("INCLUDING THE PART YOU EDITED OUT") are effective because they're specific and personal rather than generically threatening.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely. The school assembly/response is present (though rendered as gate-standing and a meeting rather than a formal assembly—a reasonable adaptation). The institutional self-protection is well-executed through Mr Durnham's "quietly" and Mrs Kline's measured competence. The splinter in the group is present. The jigsaw recollection of the night is handled through Asha's fragmented memory rather than a flashback, which is the right choice. The end turn—Leah's midnight ultimatum—lands as planned.

The chapter plan called for Asha to remember "her own worst act: not the delay itself, but drafting the 'what we say if asked' message." This is gestured at ("Asha saying don't ring, don't ring") but the specific detail of drafting the message isn't quite as foregrounded as planned. The memory of someone shoving Rowan back down is a good addition that complicates the "accident" narrative effectively.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The chapter is emotionally engaging, particularly in the meeting room scene. The moment where Asha sees Mina's look from last summer—"betrayal, confusion, anger. A look Asha had avoided ever since by pretending it hadn't happened"—is quietly devastating and earns its weight. The chapter builds genuine dread without melodrama.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter's main weakness is occasional over-explicitness. The final exchange ("Was it?" / "I don't know which version is true anymore") does the thematic work too directly. The chapter plan called for subtext to do work, and here the text surfaces it too cleanly. The "daylight doesn't fix it" callback at the end is a bit tidy. Some of the institutional dialogue (Mrs Kline's lines especially) is slightly functional rather than fully characterized.

The chapter is also slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the corridor/whisper material at the opening could be tighter.

Overall, this is a confident, well-executed chapter that serves the story's larger architecture without sacrificing moment-to-moment texture.

chapter_raw_judge_text.2:
[Analysis]

This is a strong chapter that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Asha's voice is well-maintained throughout. The internal logic of her character—managing narrative rather than managing truth—comes through clearly in moments like "she didn't just want it solved. She wanted it solved without consequences." This is exactly the kind of self-aware but self-deceiving beat the character profile calls for. Connor's defensive joking, Mina's blunt moral clarity, and Theo's theatrical panic are all rendered consistently with their profiles. The line "I'm not being a dick. I'm being realistic" is very Connor. Mina's "Revolutionary" during the assembly is pitch-perfect.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong and naturalistic. The group chat snippets are appropriately sparse and feel authentic. The assembly scene dialogue works well. However, there are moments where characters articulate things slightly too cleanly for the situation—Theo's "Maybe it wants the video" is a bit on-the-nose as a plot-advancing line, and Mina's "Where, then? Under the bridge? At midnight?" is a little too wry and constructed for someone who's genuinely frightened. These are minor issues in an otherwise solid dialogue chapter.

PLOT EXECUTION

The chapter follows its plan closely: the school rumour mill, the assembly, the group meeting, the institutional non-response, and the escalating messages. The plan called for "YOU KEPT IT" plus a cropped screenshot of a deleted group chat message—the chapter delivers this effectively. The screenshot of Asha's own message ("don't ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE") is a genuinely chilling reveal that earns its moment. The countdown logic (three drops to two drops) is a nice touch not explicitly in the plan but consistent with it.

SHOW VS TELL

The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but there are some lapses. "She didn't just want it solved. She wanted it solved without consequences" is explicit thematic statement rather than dramatised revelation—though it works because it's framed as Asha's self-recognition. The line "choices, inconveniently, had consequences" at the end is the weakest moment in the chapter: it's a closing aphorism that tells the reader what to think rather than trusting the scene to land. It's the kind of line that sounds writerly but is actually doing the reader's work for them.

METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY

The prose is generally clean and controlled. Some metaphors land well: "like a body floating up no matter how many rocks you tied to it" for the video is effective and appropriately dark. "The corridor tilted slightly, like the school was a ship and she'd stepped onto the wrong part of it" is a bit strained—the ship metaphor doesn't quite earn its place. "Eyeliner sharp enough to cut glass" is a cliché. "The joking mask slipped and something frightened and young showed underneath" is competent but not distinctive. The prose occasionally reaches for effect without quite getting there, but it's not egregiously purple.

TONE AND DARK COMEDY

The darkly comic British YA tone is well-handled. The assembly scene is the highlight: Mr Durnham's "STAY SAFE ONLINE" slide, Mina's "Revolutionary," the Lynx-and-sweat smell description. These land with the right mixture of absurdity and dread. The canal man joke from the Year 10s is a nice touch—showing how the group's guilt has already been absorbed into teen folklore.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The chapter builds tension effectively. The reveal of the screenshot is genuinely unsettling because it's specific and personal—"don't ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE" tells us something real about what happened without over-explaining. The countdown from three drops to two drops creates dread through restraint. The chapter earns its darkness.

WEAKNESSES

The closing line is too neat and aphoristic. One or two metaphors don't land. Theo's "Maybe it wants the video" is slightly too convenient as a plot beat. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the corridor scene could be tighter. The "Rowan" information is revealed in fragments as planned, but the fragments are perhaps slightly too legible—we understand the situation quite clearly by the end, which slightly reduces the mystery the plan intended to preserve.

OVERALL

This is accomplished YA fiction with a strong sense of voice, consistent characterisation, and effective pacing. It follows its plan faithfully and serves the prompt well. The flaws are real but not severe: occasional over-explanation, one weak closing line, a few strained metaphors. It's not a masterpiece but it's genuinely good work.

[Analysis]

This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Asha's voice is the chapter's greatest strength. The internal narration is sharp, specific, and genuinely funny without being try-hard. "Three blood drops. Not even the nice ones, the cartoon ones. These looked like they'd been peeled off a GCSE Biology worksheet and sent to her by someone who thought haemoglobin was a personality" — this lands. It's character-revealing, tonally precise, and earns its wit. The voice is consistent throughout: observational, deflecting, controlling. The detail of her taking a screenshot as "the first sensible thing you did in a horror film before you inevitably walked into the cellar with a candle and got eaten" is both funny and characterologically accurate — she's already in evidence-collection mode.

The group chat dynamics are well-handled. Connor's jokes land as defensive rather than purely comic, Mina's sudden seriousness ("stop saying murder canal" / "i'm serious. don't joke") registers as a genuine tonal shift, and Theo's delayed responses and single-line panic feel authentic. The chat is used sparingly and purposefully, not as a wall of text.

PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The midnight text arrives, the group chat reaction unfolds, the photo of the canal bridge with the hair clip escalates things, and Theo's phone call ends with the revelation about Rowan. The "YOU KEPT IT" message and "MEET. WHERE. YOU. LEFT. IT." arrive as planned. The chapter plan called for ending with Theo saying "It knows about Rowan" — the chapter delivers this, though it's slightly diffused by having Theo say "I just know" rather than something more concrete, which is actually more realistic and less on-the-nose than the plan suggested.

METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most metaphors land. "The lie appeared in the chat with the casual confidence of something she'd practised" is excellent — economical and revealing. "Her brain did that horrible thing where it tried to pretend it didn't know what it meant, like if you played stupid, the universe would accept it" is good. However, there are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "The word landed like a slap" is a cliché. "Something that had been buried starting to crawl back towards the surface" as the closing image is functional but not particularly fresh — it's the kind of ending that announces its own significance a bit too loudly. The fox screaming "like it was being murdered for content" is a nice contemporary twist on a familiar image.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong. Connor's voice is consistent and his jokes feel like actual teenager jokes rather than adult-written approximations. Mina's bluntness registers. Theo's phone call is well-handled — his "We didn't do anything wrong. It was an accident" is doing real work, revealing the rehearsed quality of the group's self-justification. The only weakness is that the phone call between Asha and Theo is slightly functional — it exists to deliver information and emotional temperature rather than to reveal character in unexpected ways.

SHOW VS TELL: The chapter is largely showing rather than telling. The backstory of Rowan is introduced through fragments — "the video," "that night," "Don't ring, don't ring" — rather than exposition. This is handled well. The one moment of slight over-explanation is "Rowan wasn't in the chat. Rowan wasn't in school. Rowan wasn't— Asha didn't even know what Rowan *was* now, exactly, because they never said it out loud anymore." This is close to telling, though it's filtered through Asha's consciousness in a way that partially redeems it.

TONAL BALANCE: The dark comedy and creeping dread are well-balanced. The chapter doesn't tip into either pure comedy or pure horror, which is exactly what the brief requires. The mundane details (shop fridge humming, motorbike outside, revision notes bleeding neon yellow) ground the supernatural-feeling threat in ordinary life effectively.

WEAKNESSES: The closing paragraph is slightly overwrought — "split the air open" and "crawl back towards the surface" in quick succession is one metaphor too many for a chapter that has otherwise been disciplined. The hair clip as a specific detail is good, but its significance is slightly over-telegraphed by Asha's reaction. The chapter could trust the reader more at the end.

FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter hits all its planned beats: midnight text, group chat reaction, escalating second message, photo of canal bridge, Theo's call about Rowan. The "YOU KEPT IT" message appears as planned. The mum detail (landing light clicking off) is present though slightly underplayed compared to the plan's "Mum texts from downstairs." Minor deviation but not a problem.

Overall this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter with a strong voice, good tonal control, and mostly earned prose. Its weaknesses are minor: a slightly soft ending, occasional cliché, and one or two moments of over-signalling.

chapter_raw_judge_text.6:
[Analysis]

Chapter 6 is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Asha's voice is well-maintained throughout. Her tendency to calculate exits even while panicking ("She hated herself for immediately calculating exits"), her controlling nature, and her self-awareness about her own worst impulses all track with the character profile. The moment where she lies to her mum ("No," she lied) and the narration simply notes it without editorialising is particularly effective — it trusts the reader. Connor's arrival via window tap is consistent with his impulsive protectiveness and his reliance on Asha's "straight lines." Mina's brief appearance ("Like a bat. Judging me") is economical and accurate to her profile. Theo's absence is handled well — his non-appearance creates tension rather than feeling like an oversight.

The mother is a strong minor character. Her anger is specific and earned, not generic parental fury. "The voice that had made Asha eat vegetables and apologise when she didn't mean it" is a good, grounded detail.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. The exchange between Asha and Leah via Instagram DM is tense and well-paced. Leah's lines ("i want you to stop hiding," "was it?") are appropriately minimal and devastating. Connor's window-scene dialogue is naturalistic and carries his character's blend of bravado and genuine fear. The line "Because you're the one who thinks in straight lines when the rest of us are just… flailing" is a little on-the-nose as character exposition — it tells us what we already know about Asha rather than showing it through action — but it's forgivable in context.

METAPHOR AND PROSE STYLE

The prose is generally controlled. Some metaphors land well: "like the pause in a microwave before it beeps" is fresh and specific. "Legs that felt like borrowed stilts" is slightly weaker — "borrowed" is doing odd work there. "Swallowing glass" is a cliché. The "skin" metaphor when Asha hands over her phone ("like she'd just handed over her skin") is a bit overwrought. The opening description of rain as "air that had decided to be wet" is charming and original. Overall, the metaphor density is appropriate for YA and most of them land, though a few are strained.

PLOT AND CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely. Asha tells her mum "some of it" and faces parental panic/control. Connor arrives at the window. Mina is present. Theo is missing. They go to the midnight meet — or at least, they're heading there by chapter's end. The chapter plan specified the conversation with Leah happening at the canal bridge, but this chapter ends before they arrive, which is a reasonable structural choice that builds suspense. The Instagram DM exchange with Leah is an addition not in the plan but serves the chapter's purposes well.

One deviation: the plan says Asha "tells her mum some of it" but the chapter shows Asha lying ("No," she lied, when asked if they left Rowan). This is actually more interesting than the plan — it shows Asha's compulsive self-protection even in a moment of partial confession. Good instinct.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The scene with the printed screenshot on the kitchen table is effective — the detail of someone using a printer in 2025 is both funny and horrifying in the right way. The Rowan profile picture (two kids laughing) lands without being manipulative. The moment Asha's mum says "Did you leave him there?" and Asha lies is the emotional peak of the chapter and it's handled with restraint.

DARK COMEDY

The chapter plan emphasised darkly comic British YA, and this chapter is lighter on comedy than earlier chapters presumably were. The printer detail and Connor's "Like a bat. Judging me" are the main comic beats. This is appropriate given the chapter's escalating stakes, but the tonal balance could be slightly richer.

WEAKNESSES

The phrase "borrowed stilts" is awkward. "Swallowing glass" is a cliché. The ending — "It was Leah being accurate" — is a little too neat and aphoristic, the kind of closing line that announces its own profundity. The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes structurally; the Instagram DM exchange, while good, could be tighter. The revelation that Asha lied about leaving Rowan is powerful but slightly underplayed — it deserves a beat more weight.

OVERALL

This is confident, controlled YA writing with strong character work, mostly effective prose, and good structural instincts. It's not a masterpiece — some metaphors strain, the ending line is a touch self-congratulatory, and the comedy is thinner than the genre demands — but it's well above average and largely earns its emotional moments.

chapter_raw_judge_text.4:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically.

VOICE AND TONE: The chapter opens with a genuinely sharp observation — the blood emoji becoming a commodity Josh Ward sells for £1 a sheet — which immediately grounds the story in recognisable British school culture while maintaining the dark irony the genre demands. The line "Mr Patel had sighed like he'd aged ten years in one lesson" is economical and funny. The voice is consistent throughout: dry, observational, slightly exhausted. The "civic duty" joke from Connor lands correctly as defensive humour.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely excellent. It does real work: characterising, advancing plot, and revealing emotional states simultaneously. Leah's lines are particularly well-crafted. "He still can't hold a pen properly" is devastating in its specificity and restraint — exactly the kind of small, awful detail the plan called for. "You don't get to tidy this up" and "For who?" are sharp and land with weight. The group's banter ("Do you vape?" / "No. I just look like someone who vapes.") is naturalistic and funny without feeling forced. There are occasional moments where dialogue edges toward functional exposition ("This is about Rowan") but these are minor.

CHARACTERISATION: Characters are consistent with their profiles. Connor's charm-as-armour is present throughout; his "civic duty" joke followed by an immediate "Sorry" when Mina shuts him down is a nice beat. Mina's moral clarity cutting through ("She's grieving" vs Connor's "She's mental") is exactly right. Theo's physical cowardice and self-minimising are well-rendered. Asha's internal conflict — relieved they have nothing to bring, then ashamed of the relief — is the chapter's best character moment. Leah is handled with real skill: not a villain, not a saint, just someone running on grief and adrenaline who has refined her anger into something practical.

SHOW VS TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. Asha's guilt is conveyed through physical reactions (the cold drop in the stomach, the flush up the neck, leaving her phone face-down) rather than stated. The moment where Leah's words land — "The car park noise didn't stop. A trolley clattered. Someone beeped their car. A baby cried. But Asha felt like the world had shifted." — is effective in its contrast between mundane continuity and internal rupture. However, "She hated being seen" is a slight tell, and "pain, maybe, or rage" is a minor instance of the narrator doing interpretive work that could be left to the reader.

METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is clean and controlled. Metaphors are used sparingly and mostly land. "Like she'd been looking at them already, in her head, for months" is good. "Like it was an animal that might bite" for the phone is slightly familiar but functional. The "photoshopped their panic into someone else's day" line is the most inventive and earns its place. There's no purple prose to speak of — the writing is disciplined.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The blood donation bus setting is used well — the absurdity of the location is mined for dark comedy without being overdone. The confrontation with Leah is handled with appropriate messiness: no villain speech, just presence and specific grief. The chapter ends with the screenshot leak appearing on Josh Ward's story, which is the planned "end turn." The escalation feels earned rather than mechanical.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter's main weakness is that the confrontation scene, while well-written, is slightly longer than it needs to be. Leah's speech about Rowan ("He's got exercises. Every day...") is powerful but edges toward being slightly too articulate for the moment — it risks making Leah sound like she's delivering a prepared statement rather than speaking from raw grief. The plan called for her to say "something small and awful" and then leave; instead she delivers several devastating lines in sequence, which slightly dilutes the impact of "He still can't hold a pen properly" by following it with more explanation. The ending with the Instagram comments ("omg," "is that rowan??") is realistic but the rapid escalation from confrontation to leak feels slightly compressed — though this may be a structural necessity.

The "TOO LATE TO BE QUIET" final message is effective but slightly more on-the-nose than the emoji-only communications that preceded it.

OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing that serves the genre and the plan well. It avoids the pitfalls of melodrama and moralising, keeps its characters human and contradictory, and uses its mundane setting (blood donation bus, supermarket car park) to excellent effect. The dark comedy is genuinely funny without undercutting the emotional weight.

chapter_raw_judge_text.3:
[Analysis]

Chapter 3 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it critically across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CRAFT

The chapter opens with a sharp, economical sentence — "Asha tried to get through the rest of the day by turning herself into a machine" — and the voice is consistent throughout. The English class detail (writing 🩸🩸🩸 in the margin by accident) is genuinely good: it's specific, funny, and psychologically true. The two girls in the toilet mirror rehearsing concern is an excellent piece of social observation — "eyes bright" at gossip is exactly right, and the detail about Asha not washing her hands properly and then hating herself for it as "proof you were a bad person" is the kind of layered self-awareness that elevates the writing above competent YA.

The park setting works well. The leisure centre sign flickering "like it was tired," the kid eating gritty crisps — these are grounded, specific details that feel earned rather than decorative. The McDonald's/library exchange is a nice piece of character comedy that doesn't overstay its welcome.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is largely strong. The exchange where Theo is caught out about the video is well-handled — his evasions feel authentic, and the escalation to "You filmed too!" followed by Connor's silence is dramatically effective. Mina's line "Stop. Joking." as a two-word paragraph is well-deployed. The line "We've just been pretending it's a story we can pause" is perhaps the chapter's best — it's thematically resonant without being on-the-nose, and it fits Mina's voice perfectly.

However, some dialogue edges toward the functional. "Practical. How do we find out who it is?" from Connor is a bit of a gear-shift that feels slightly mechanical. The "What did we steal?" / "The truth." exchange is the chapter's weakest moment — it's too neat, too aphoristic, the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually a shortcut. It's the one place where the writing tells rather than shows.

CHARACTERS

All four characters are consistent with their profiles. Connor's defensive joking, his inability to sit still (the swinging), his flash of anger when Asha is implicated — all ring true. Theo's evasions, his touching the bag strap, his eyes darting — the physical tells are well-chosen. Mina's moral clarity delivered without sanctimony is handled well; she gets the best lines without becoming a mouthpiece. Asha's "I was trying to stop it getting worse" followed by Connor's "It did get worse" is a quietly devastating exchange.

The Leah reveal at the end — the Instagram follow request, the persistence of the second request, the final text — is well-paced. The detail of Leah's private account appearing twice, like a hand knocking harder, is effective without being overwrought.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely: the group argues about what exists, Theo admits to having something, they attempt practical steps, there's a dead-end beat (the park rather than a physical location drop, which is a slight deviation — the plan called for them going somewhere expecting a drop and finding nothing, but here the "dead end" is more diffuse), and the end turn delivers the third message with a location. The chapter plan called for "🩸🩸🩸" plus a location (the blood donation bus), and the chapter delivers "🩸🩸" plus "TOMORROW 00:03" plus "BRING WHAT YOU STOLE" plus "SEE YOU AT THE BUS." This is close but slightly compressed — the blood donation bus specificity from the plan is replaced with just "THE BUS," which is less vivid. The plan's "dead-end / realism beat" (fox screams, man with dog) is replaced with the park scene, which is less eerie but arguably more realistic.

WEAKNESSES

The "What did we steal?" / "The truth." exchange is the chapter's most amateurish moment — it's the kind of exchange that would appear in a lesser YA novel. Similarly, "I am a villain" / "No you're not" is slightly on-the-nose, though it's partially redeemed by Connor's motivation being transparent self-protection rather than genuine reassurance.

The chapter occasionally over-explains emotional states. "Pain was good. Pain was controllable." is a slight lurch into telling — the physical detail of the chain pressing into her palms already did that work. A few of the internal observations are slightly too neat ("like animals in headlights" is a cliché).

The Tilly messages feel slightly convenient as a mechanism to show the spreading screenshots, though they're realistic enough.

OVERALL

This is a confident, well-executed chapter that maintains voice, advances plot, develops character, and earns its darker moments. The comedy is present but not forced. The darkness is earned. The chapter's main weakness is occasional over-neatness in dialogue and a couple of tell-don't-show moments, but these are minor against a generally strong execution.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

Chapter 7 is the penultimate chapter before the denouement, and it carries the weight of the story's central moral reckoning. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Asha's voice is well-maintained throughout. Her tendency to manage narrative, her observational sharpness, and her contradictory impulse to confess while protecting herself all come through. The detail of her lying rising "like a reflex" before she stops herself is consistent with her profile. Connor's defensive loyalty and his accusation at the end ("You always decide what's best and then act like it's responsibility") is perhaps the chapter's strongest character moment—it's a genuinely cutting observation that feels earned by both characters' profiles. Mina's blunt interventions in the meeting room ("Here we go," "You're always at this stage") are pitch-perfect. The absence of Theo is noted but not explained within the chapter, which is a minor gap—his absence is significant given his role with the video, and the chapter doesn't quite account for it.

PC Hayes is handled with appropriate ambiguity—he's not a saviour, he's institutional, and his "we'll do this correctly" is bureaucratic rather than reassuring. The Headteacher's concern for "impact on the school" is a nice satirical touch consistent with the plan's aim to show institutional self-protection.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong and naturalistic. "You always do that. You always decide what's best and then act like it's responsibility" is excellent—specific, character-revealing, and painful. Mina's courtroom-style interjections feel authentic. The Headteacher's polished evasions work well. Some lines are slightly on-the-nose: "Because it's already out" as Asha's justification is a bit too neat, slightly undercutting the messiness the plan called for. Connor's "What have you done?" is functional but predictable.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely: parents contacted, Head of Year summons, Connor's dad involved but protecting his son first, the school's priority being reputation, and Asha making the active choice to tell the full truth. The TowpathTruth account serves as the mechanism for the leak spreading into parents' WhatsApps (though the Facebook local group element from the plan is absent). The ending with the single 🩸 and Asha's reinterpretation of it as "a receipt" rather than a threat is effective and serves the plan's end turn.

However, the plan specified that Leah "posts/forwards the material wider than intended" and that it "seeps into the year group, then parents' WhatsApps, then the local Facebook page." The chapter handles this somewhat—the TowpathTruth account and Connor's mum's WhatsApp message are present—but the seeping, gradual spread feels slightly compressed. The plan also noted the school's priority is reputation, which is shown, but the "Connor's dad suddenly involved but in a way that protects his son first" element is present though slightly underplayed.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The chapter is emotionally effective. The mum waiting in the kitchen with cold tea is a strong, specific image. The meeting room scene builds genuine tension. Connor's final confrontation with Asha lands with real pain. The cost of Asha's confession is felt in her body ("like a bruise") and in Connor's departure. The single 🩸 at the end recontextualises the story's central symbol effectively.

SHOW VS. TELL

The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but there are moments of slight over-explanation. "She felt sick. She felt lightheaded. She felt like she'd stepped off a cliff and only now noticed there was no ground" is a triple-tell that slightly undercuts what the scene has already shown. The cliff metaphor is also a bit generic. Similarly, "feeling the cost arrive in her body like a bruise" is telling-with-metaphor rather than showing.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and controlled, appropriate for the YA register. There are occasional moments of slightly forced imagery: "like a casserole dish" (for rumour passing) is a good, specific British detail. "Like a final boss" is a nice YA-appropriate metaphor. "Canal air: damp stone, algae, old cigarettes" is economical and effective. The "stepped off a cliff" metaphor is the weakest—generic and slightly purple. "Snap, small but clean" for Asha's internal moment is good.

DARK COMEDY

The plan called for darkly comic British YA, and this chapter is the most serious of the arc—appropriately so. The dark comedy is present but muted: Mina's courtroom interjections, the school email's bland language, the poll ("should they get expelled? yes/no") as social media spectacle. This feels right for the chapter's position in the story, though the plan's note about "dark comedy and ache" is more fully realised in the plan for Chapter 8.

COHERENCE

The chapter is coherent and well-structured. The timeline (early hours, morning texts, 10am meeting, corridor aftermath) is clear. The TowpathTruth account as the leak mechanism is consistent with the story's social media texture.

ORIGINALITY

The meeting room scene is well-executed but follows a fairly familiar YA/drama template. The most original element is Connor's accusation at the end, which avoids the expected "you betrayed us" and instead cuts to something more psychologically specific about Asha's controlling nature. The reinterpretation of 🩸 at the end is genuinely effective.

OVERALL

This is a competent, emotionally engaging chapter that follows its plan faithfully and maintains character consistency. Its weaknesses are minor: some tell-don't-show moments, one generic metaphor, Theo's absence unexplained, and the spread of the leak feeling slightly compressed. The dialogue is mostly strong, the emotional beats land, and the ending recontextualises the story's central symbol with genuine resonance.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is an ambitious, multi-chapter YA thriller with a darkly comic British sensibility. Let me assess it comprehensively.

STRENGTHS:

The voice is the piece's greatest asset. The narrator's interiority is sharp, contemporary, and genuinely funny without undermining the gravity of the situation. Lines like "a fox screamed like it was being murdered for content" and "the kind of breathing exercise the internet swore would calm you down. It did not" land consistently. The British teenage vernacular feels authentic rather than performed. The comedy is earned through specificity—Josh Ward selling blood emoji stickers for £1, Theo looking like "someone who vapes," the fun-size Mars Bar in the safeguarding meeting.

The central mystery is well-constructed. The escalating emoji countdown (three drops to two to one) is a clever structural device. The reveal of Leah as the sender is satisfying because it's telegraphed without being obvious, and her motivations are genuinely complex—grief weaponised into a campaign for accountability.

Character differentiation is strong. Connor's defensive humour, Mina's furious clarity, Theo's cowardice, and Asha's controlling instincts are all distinct and consistent across eight chapters. The group dynamic feels real.

The moral architecture is sophisticated for YA. Asha isn't simply guilty or innocent—she delayed calling for help out of self-preservation, coached the group's story, and has been filing the truth under "accident" for a year. The story refuses easy absolution.

WEAKNESSES:

The metaphor density is occasionally excessive. "Her brain did that horrible thing where it tried to pretend it didn't know what it meant, like if you played stupid, the universe would accept it" is fine, but the piece stacks these observations so frequently that some feel like filler rather than insight. "Like a body floating up no matter how many rocks you tied to it" is a strong image, but it arrives in a paragraph already dense with figurative language.

Some metaphors don't quite land: "her legs felt like borrowed stilts" is awkward, and "the truth under everything: she didn't just want it solved. She wanted it solved without consequences" is telling rather than showing—the narrative has already demonstrated this without needing to state it.

The pacing in the middle chapters (3-5) becomes slightly repetitive. The pattern of: group meets, phone buzzes, new revelation, group argues, repeat—starts to feel mechanical. Chapter 4's bus confrontation with Leah is the strongest scene in the piece, but it arrives after two chapters of similar tension-building.

Leah's final Instagram story ("he says it smells like soup") is the emotional peak of the piece and genuinely moving, but it arrives somewhat abruptly. The transition from threatening blackmailer to grieving sister posting vulnerable details feels slightly compressed—not entirely unearned, but rushed.

The school meeting in Chapter 7 is where the piece most strains credibility. PC Hayes attending a school welfare meeting and Asha spontaneously confessing in front of everyone feels dramatically convenient. Real safeguarding processes don't work this way, and the scene's theatricality undercuts the grounded realism established elsewhere.

Asha's transformation in Chapter 7—deciding to tell the truth publicly—is the story's central turn. It's mostly earned through the accumulation of pressure, but the specific trigger (not wanting Josh Ward to control the narrative) feels slightly thin as a motivation for such a consequential decision. The deeper motivation (exhaustion with lying) is present but could be more fully dramatised.

Connor's reaction ("you always decide what's best and then act like it's responsibility") is the most incisive character moment in the piece and rings completely true. His subsequent disappearance from the narrative is a slight weakness—we don't see his arc resolve.

The ending is appropriately ambiguous and restrained. The "you did the right thing / I'm still angry" message is exactly right—it refuses false reconciliation without being nihilistic. The fox screaming again as a callback is a bit neat but forgivable.

OVERALL: This is genuinely accomplished YA fiction with a strong, consistent voice, real moral complexity, and mostly earned emotional beats. Its weaknesses are the occasional over-reliance on metaphor, some repetitive middle-chapter pacing, and one dramatically convenient plot turn. It's well above average but not quite masterpiece territory.

chapter_raw_judges_text.8:
[Analysis]

This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter's emotional architecture is well-constructed. The school email opening is genuinely sharp — "It did not mention Rowan's name. It did not mention what had happened last summer. It did not mention why a blood emoji had been stuck to every surface in Year Eleven like an infestation. It was, in other words, perfect." This is the darkly comic British YA voice working at its best: the irony is earned, not performed.

The Mina scene is the chapter's strongest section. The dialogue is largely excellent — "I fought a school. Bears are honest" is a good line, and "There are no clean stories. Only stories people wash and then sell" is sharp without being too aphoristic. Mina's challenge to Asha — "stop acting like you're the only person who can decide what everyone is" — is character-consistent and dramatically useful. The relationship between them feels real and earned.

The Leah Instagram story is a genuinely creative choice. "he says it smells like soup" is the kind of specific, mundane detail that does more emotional work than any dramatic revelation could. This is the chapter plan's instruction to keep Rowan real without making him a plot device, executed well. The soup detail lands.

The confession scene — Asha telling Mina what actually happened — is handled with appropriate messiness. "Someone bumped him. Like a joke. Like, 'move over'" captures the horrible ambiguity of the incident without resolving it neatly. The refusal to be definitive ("I don't know if it was a shove") is honest and effective.

The closing sequence — the Photos memory notification, the unknown message — is restrained and earns its emotional weight. "you did the right thing / i'm still angry" is a perfect two-line encapsulation of Leah's character and the story's refusal of easy resolution.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter occasionally tips into telling rather than showing, particularly in the emotional beats. "Asha's throat tightened" appears four times in the chapter, which is a significant repetition problem. Similarly, "Asha's eyes stung" appears twice. These are stock physical responses that signal emotion rather than embodying it. The plan called for showing subtext do work, but these moments reach for shorthand.

The fox screaming at the end — "a fox screamed again, as if to remind her that the world was still full of raw, living noise" — is the chapter's weakest moment. The "as if to remind her" construction is exactly the kind of forced interpretive metaphor that the scoring criteria flags. The fox has appeared earlier in the story (per the plan's dead-end scene), so its return has some logic, but the explanatory gloss ruins it. Let the fox scream; don't explain what it means.

The fridge hum is used twice as a structural anchor, which works the first time and becomes slightly mechanical the second. "It had hummed through last summer too. It would hum through whatever came next" is a little too neat in its temporal symmetry.

The mum scene is competent but slightly underpowered. The dialogue is functional rather than distinctive. "The real version is going to hurt" / "It already did" is the kind of exchange that sounds like a YA novel rather than a real conversation — slightly too polished, too aphoristic.

The chapter follows the plan closely and hits most of its beats: the school email, Mina's visit, the glimpse of Rowan's reality (physio, green building, soup smell), Leah's partial acknowledgment, the closing midnight notification. The plan called for "one relationship ends; one shifts into something quieter; one remains unresolved" — Connor's silence is the unresolved one, Mina's is the shifted one, but the "ended" relationship isn't clearly present. This is a minor gap.

The dark comedy is present but lighter in this chapter than earlier ones, which is appropriate for a denouement — the chapter earns its shift in register.

Characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. Asha's self-awareness about her own motives ("I did it because I didn't want to be the villain") is exactly right. Mina's bluntness-with-care is well-rendered. Connor's absence (furious silence) is characteristically him.

Overall: a well-executed final chapter with some repetitive emotional shorthand and one or two moments where the prose reaches for significance rather than earning it. The soup detail alone is worth significant credit.

[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CRAFT

The narrative voice is consistently Asha's: observational, dry, anxious, and self-aware. Lines like "the school had decided it was having an incident" and the prefect "who'd discovered authority last week and was drunk on it" are sharp and earned. The Josh Ward detail—"grinning like he'd invented gossip"—is economical and precise. The bowl of sweets as a detail ("like someone had read a manual on How To Make Teenagers Confess and decided sugar was the key") is genuinely funny and tonally right for the genre.

The prose is mostly clean and controlled. There are occasional moments where the metaphors strain slightly—"a smile that felt like cracking glass" is a touch overwrought, and "her stomach dropped into her shoes" is a cliché—but these are minor. The writing generally avoids purple prose and earns its darker moments.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. Mina's lines are particularly well-calibrated: "Within your what? Your little email bubble?" and "You always mean quietly" feel authentic to her character profile and carry genuine thematic weight without being on-the-nose. Connor's "My dad's a police officer" landing as both explanation and deflection is nicely handled. Mr Durnham's relief at the mention of Connor's dad—"Well then, we can handle this quietly"—is a small, devastating institutional moment.

The dialogue does occasionally tip toward characters articulating the theme too cleanly. Mina's final line, "The part where it wasn't an accident," is effective but slightly too neat—it does the thematic work too explicitly, telling us what the chapter has been building toward rather than letting it remain in the subtext. Similarly, "We never visited it properly in the first place" is a little on-the-nose.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

All four characters behave consistently with their profiles. Connor's defensive loyalty and his instinct to contain ("No" to police) is well-rendered. Mina's moral absolutism and her refusal to sit down is exactly right. Asha's internal conflict between narrative control and truth is the chapter's emotional spine and is handled with real skill—particularly the moment where she realizes she's been filing a specific memory under "chaos" rather than confronting it. Theo's absence is used well; his non-presence creates its own pressure.

Leah appears only through the texts, which is appropriate for this chapter. The texts themselves ("INCLUDING THE PART YOU EDITED OUT") are effective because they're specific and personal rather than generically threatening.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely. The school assembly/response is present (though rendered as gate-standing and a meeting rather than a formal assembly—a reasonable adaptation). The institutional self-protection is well-executed through Mr Durnham's "quietly" and Mrs Kline's measured competence. The splinter in the group is present. The jigsaw recollection of the night is handled through Asha's fragmented memory rather than a flashback, which is the right choice. The end turn—Leah's midnight ultimatum—lands as planned.

The chapter plan called for Asha to remember "her own worst act: not the delay itself, but drafting the 'what we say if asked' message." This is gestured at ("Asha saying don't ring, don't ring") but the specific detail of drafting the message isn't quite as foregrounded as planned. The memory of someone shoving Rowan back down is a good addition that complicates the "accident" narrative effectively.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The chapter is emotionally engaging, particularly in the meeting room scene. The moment where Asha sees Mina's look from last summer—"betrayal, confusion, anger. A look Asha had avoided ever since by pretending it hadn't happened"—is quietly devastating and earns its weight. The chapter builds genuine dread without melodrama.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter's main weakness is occasional over-explicitness. The final exchange ("Was it?" / "I don't know which version is true anymore") does the thematic work too directly. The chapter plan called for subtext to do work, and here the text surfaces it too cleanly. The "daylight doesn't fix it" callback at the end is a bit tidy. Some of the institutional dialogue (Mrs Kline's lines especially) is slightly functional rather than fully characterized.

The chapter is also slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the corridor/whisper material at the opening could be tighter.

Overall, this is a confident, well-executed chapter that serves the story's larger architecture without sacrificing moment-to-moment texture.

The plan called for "YOU KEPT IT" plus a cropped screenshot of a deleted group chat message—the chapter delivers this effectively. The screenshot of Asha's own message ("don't ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE") is a genuinely chilling reveal that earns its moment. The countdown logic (three drops to two drops) is a nice touch not explicitly in the plan but consistent with it.

SHOW VS TELL

The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but there are some lapses. "She didn't just want it solved. She wanted it solved without consequences" is explicit thematic statement rather than dramatised revelation—though it works because it's framed as Asha's self-recognition. The line "choices, inconveniently, had consequences" at the end is the weakest moment in the chapter: it's a closing aphorism that tells the reader what to think rather than trusting the scene to land. It's the kind of line that sounds writerly but is actually doing the reader's work for them.

METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY

The prose is generally clean and controlled. Some metaphors land well: "like a body floating up no matter how many rocks you tied to it" for the video is effective and appropriately dark. "The corridor tilted slightly, like the school was a ship and she'd stepped onto the wrong part of it" is a bit strained—the ship metaphor doesn't quite earn its place. "Eyeliner sharp enough to cut glass" is a cliché. "The joking mask slipped and something frightened and young showed underneath" is competent but not distinctive. The prose occasionally reaches for effect without quite getting there, but it's not egregiously purple.

TONE AND DARK COMEDY

The darkly comic British YA tone is well-handled. The assembly scene is the highlight: Mr Durnham's "STAY SAFE ONLINE" slide, Mina's "Revolutionary," the Lynx-and-sweat smell description. These land with the right mixture of absurdity and dread. The canal man joke from the Year 10s is a nice touch—showing how the group's guilt has already been absorbed into teen folklore.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The chapter builds tension effectively. The reveal of the screenshot is genuinely unsettling because it's specific and personal—"don't ring anyone. just get him up. PLEASE" tells us something real about what happened without over-explaining. The countdown from three drops to two drops creates dread through restraint. The chapter earns its darkness.

WEAKNESSES

The closing line is too neat and aphoristic. One or two metaphors don't land. Theo's "Maybe it wants the video" is slightly too convenient as a plot beat. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes—some of the corridor scene could be tighter. The "Rowan" information is revealed in fragments as planned, but the fragments are perhaps slightly too legible—we understand the situation quite clearly by the end, which slightly reduces the mystery the plan intended to preserve.

OVERALL

This is accomplished YA fiction with a strong sense of voice, consistent characterisation, and effective pacing. It follows its plan faithfully and serves the prompt well. The flaws are real but not severe: occasional over-explanation, one weak closing line, a few strained metaphors. It's not a masterpiece but it's genuinely good work.

This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Asha's voice is the chapter's greatest strength. The internal narration is sharp, specific, and genuinely funny without being try-hard. "Three blood drops. Not even the nice ones, the cartoon ones. These looked like they'd been peeled off a GCSE Biology worksheet and sent to her by someone who thought haemoglobin was a personality" — this lands. It's character-revealing, tonally precise, and earns its wit. The voice is consistent throughout: observational, deflecting, controlling. The detail of her taking a screenshot as "the first sensible thing you did in a horror film before you inevitably walked into the cellar with a candle and got eaten" is both funny and characterologically accurate — she's already in evidence-collection mode.

The group chat dynamics are well-handled. Connor's jokes land as defensive rather than purely comic, Mina's sudden seriousness ("stop saying murder canal" / "i'm serious. don't joke") registers as a genuine tonal shift, and Theo's delayed responses and single-line panic feel authentic. The chat is used sparingly and purposefully, not as a wall of text.

PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The midnight text arrives, the group chat reaction unfolds, the photo of the canal bridge with the hair clip escalates things, and Theo's phone call ends with the revelation about Rowan. The "YOU KEPT IT" message and "MEET. WHERE. YOU. LEFT. IT." arrive as planned. The chapter plan called for ending with Theo saying "It knows about Rowan" — the chapter delivers this, though it's slightly diffused by having Theo say "I just know" rather than something more concrete, which is actually more realistic and less on-the-nose than the plan suggested.

METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most metaphors land. "The lie appeared in the chat with the casual confidence of something she'd practised" is excellent — economical and revealing. "Her brain did that horrible thing where it tried to pretend it didn't know what it meant, like if you played stupid, the universe would accept it" is good. However, there are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "The word landed like a slap" is a cliché. "Something that had been buried starting to crawl back towards the surface" as the closing image is functional but not particularly fresh — it's the kind of ending that announces its own significance a bit too loudly. The fox screaming "like it was being murdered for content" is a nice contemporary twist on a familiar image.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong. Connor's voice is consistent and his jokes feel like actual teenager jokes rather than adult-written approximations. Mina's bluntness registers. Theo's phone call is well-handled — his "We didn't do anything wrong. It was an accident" is doing real work, revealing the rehearsed quality of the group's self-justification. The only weakness is that the phone call between Asha and Theo is slightly functional — it exists to deliver information and emotional temperature rather than to reveal character in unexpected ways.

SHOW VS TELL: The chapter is largely showing rather than telling. The backstory of Rowan is introduced through fragments — "the video," "that night," "Don't ring, don't ring" — rather than exposition. This is handled well. The one moment of slight over-explanation is "Rowan wasn't in the chat. Rowan wasn't in school. Rowan wasn't— Asha didn't even know what Rowan *was* now, exactly, because they never said it out loud anymore." TONAL BALANCE: The dark comedy and creeping dread are well-balanced. The chapter doesn't tip into either pure comedy or pure horror, which is exactly what the brief requires. The mundane details (shop fridge humming, motorbike outside, revision notes bleeding neon yellow) ground the supernatural-feeling threat in ordinary life effectively.

WEAKNESSES: The closing paragraph is slightly overwrought — "split the air open" and "crawl back towards the surface" in quick succession is one metaphor too many for a chapter that has otherwise been disciplined. The hair clip as a specific detail is good, but its significance is slightly over-telegraphed by Asha's reaction. The chapter could trust the reader more at the end.

FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter hits all its planned beats: midnight text, group chat reaction, escalating second message, photo of canal bridge, Theo's call about Rowan. The "YOU KEPT IT" message appears as planned. The mum detail (landing light clicking off) is present though slightly underplayed compared to the plan's "Mum texts from downstairs." Minor deviation but not a problem.

Overall this is a confident, well-executed opening chapter with a strong voice, good tonal control, and mostly earned prose. Its weaknesses are minor: a slightly soft ending, occasional cliché, and one or two moments of over-signalling.

Chapter 6 is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and demonstrates genuine craft. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Asha's voice is well-maintained throughout. Her tendency to calculate exits even while panicking ("She hated herself for immediately calculating exits"), her controlling nature, and her self-awareness about her own worst impulses all track with the character profile. The moment where she lies to her mum ("No," she lied) and the narration simply notes it without editorialising is particularly effective — it trusts the reader. Connor's arrival via window tap is consistent with his impulsive protectiveness and his reliance on Asha's "straight lines." Mina's brief appearance ("Like a bat. Judging me") is economical and accurate to her profile. Theo's absence is handled well — his non-appearance creates tension rather than feeling like an oversight.

The mother is a strong minor character. Her anger is specific and earned, not generic parental fury. "The voice that had made Asha eat vegetables and apologise when she didn't mean it" is a good, grounded detail.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. The exchange between Asha and Leah via Instagram DM is tense and well-paced. Leah's lines ("i want you to stop hiding," "was it?") are appropriately minimal and devastating. Connor's window-scene dialogue is naturalistic and carries his character's blend of bravado and genuine fear. The line "Because you're the one who thinks in straight lines when the rest of us are just… flailing" is a little on-the-nose as character exposition — it tells us what we already know about Asha rather than showing it through action — but it's forgivable in context.

METAPHOR AND PROSE STYLE

The prose is generally controlled. Some metaphors land well: "like the pause in a microwave before it beeps" is fresh and specific. "Legs that felt like borrowed stilts" is slightly weaker — "borrowed" is doing odd work there. "Swallowing glass" is a cliché. The "skin" metaphor when Asha hands over her phone ("like she'd just handed over her skin") is a bit overwrought. The opening description of rain as "air that had decided to be wet" is charming and original. Overall, the metaphor density is appropriate for YA and most of them land, though a few are strained.

PLOT AND CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely. Asha tells her mum "some of it" and faces parental panic/control. Connor arrives at the window. Mina is present. Theo is missing. They go to the midnight meet — or at least, they're heading there by chapter's end. The chapter plan specified the conversation with Leah happening at the canal bridge, but this chapter ends before they arrive, which is a reasonable structural choice that builds suspense. The Instagram DM exchange with Leah is an addition not in the plan but serves the chapter's purposes well.

One deviation: the plan says Asha "tells her mum some of it" but the chapter shows Asha lying ("No," she lied, when asked if they left Rowan). This is actually more interesting than the plan — it shows Asha's compulsive self-protection even in a moment of partial confession. Good instinct.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT

The scene with the printed screenshot on the kitchen table is effective — the detail of someone using a printer in 2025 is both funny and horrifying in the right way. The Rowan profile picture (two kids laughing) lands without being manipulative. The moment Asha's mum says "Did you leave him there?" and Asha lies is the emotional peak of the chapter and it's handled with restraint.

DARK COMEDY

The chapter plan emphasised darkly comic British YA, and this chapter is lighter on comedy than earlier chapters presumably were. The printer detail and Connor's "Like a bat. Judging me" are the main comic beats. This is appropriate given the chapter's escalating stakes, but the tonal balance could be slightly richer.

WEAKNESSES

The phrase "borrowed stilts" is awkward. "Swallowing glass" is a cliché. The ending — "It was Leah being accurate" — is a little too neat and aphoristic, the kind of closing line that announces its own profundity. The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes structurally; the Instagram DM exchange, while good, could be tighter. The revelation that Asha lied about leaving Rowan is powerful but slightly underplayed — it deserves a beat more weight.

OVERALL

This is confident, controlled YA writing with strong character work, mostly effective prose, and good structural instincts. It's not a masterpiece — some metaphors strain, the ending line is a touch self-congratulatory, and the comedy is thinner than the genre demands — but it's well above average and largely earns its emotional moments.
