I lay there and listened until my own thoughts started to slur at the edges. I told myself I was being ridiculous. I told myself I would sleep.

Then, as if the thought had triggered it, the mattress shifted.

Not a big movement. Just the subtle redistribution of weight that means someone has pushed up on an elbow.

My eyes were closed. My body went alert anyway, that animal part of you that hears a twig snap in the dark.

I stayed still. Kept my breathing slow. In for four, out for six. If I opened my eyes, I’d have to do something with what I saw.

The mattress dipped again. She sat up.

I could feel it more than hear it. The springs responding, the blanket tugging slightly. Beside me, the air cooled as her body lifted away.

The silence in the room changed. It grew a center.

I was aware of my mouth suddenly, of how soft it was, how it moved without permission when you dream. I pressed my lips together. My tongue wanted to wet them. I didn’t.

There was the faintest whisper of movement above me. Hair shifting. Fabric creasing. Mara didn’t smell like sleep; she smelled like the mint gum and the lavender lotion she sometimes used on her hands. Underneath it, that stale cabinet air again.

I kept my eyes closed and let my eyelashes rest against my cheeks, but I aimed my attention the way I’d aim a microphone: toward her.

She didn’t speak right away this time. She waited, and the waiting had a patience to it that made my throat constrict. I pictured her face in the dark, eyes wide and shining, fixed on the place where words start.

I thought: It’s just Mara. It’s just her. She’s sleepwalking. She’ll lie down again if you don’t startle her.

Her breath came shallow, like yesterday. Little sips. In-in-in. A pause. Out. Too controlled for sleep. Too quiet.

“Hey,” she whispered.

Not my name this time. Just the sound, placed carefully.

I fought the reflex to answer. My chest wanted to rise too fast. I slowed it down. I gave her the pattern of sleep.

A beat passed. Two.

Her hand brushed the blanket near my stomach. Not on me. Near. I could feel the disturbance in the fabric. Then her fingers pressed lightly into the comforter and traced a small circle, as if checking whether I would flinch.

I didn’t move.

Something in her shifted—an impatience, or a recalculation. Her hand slid upward. Two fingertips touched the corner of my mouth again.

This time, I let my lips part a fraction on purpose, then closed them. A test. A mistake I could blame on sleep.

Her fingers froze. She leaned closer; I felt the warmth of her face near mine. Her breath tickled my upper lip.

She whispered, almost pleased, “Mm.”

The sound shot through me. My eyes stayed shut. I could feel my pulse in my neck.

Her fingers moved to my throat. Light pressure, not choking—more like feeling for the vibration of speech, the place where words become sound. Her thumb pressed the notch under my jaw.

I swallowed. Hard.

Her grip tightened by a degree, as if to steady herself. Then it loosened. She took her hand away slowly, carefully, as if she’d learned something and didn’t want to disturb it.

She sat back. The mattress adjusted. The waiting returned.

I forced myself to make my breathing deeper, to mimic sleep more convincingly. In for five, out for seven. My body wanted to shake with adrenaline. I dug my fingernails into my palm under the blanket until I felt the sting.

Minutes passed. Or it felt like minutes. My sense of time in the dark was unreliable; it stretched and snapped like rubber.

Then I heard a tiny sound from Mara’s side of the bed. A dry click, like her tongue against her teeth.

Again.

Again.

A rhythm.

She was practicing.

Her mouth made shapes without forming words. The click, the soft inhale, the click. Like someone rehearsing a line in silence.

The thought that came with it was absurd and immediate: She’s learning how to talk like herself.

I couldn’t help it. My eyelids tightened. That wasn’t sleep. That wasn’t normal. That—

Her hand touched my cheek, not tender, not rough—just placed there. Weight. A claim.

“If you’re listening,” she whispered, and the cadence was almost conversational, almost bored, “you can stop pretending.”

My breath hitched. I caught it, forced it back into the slow pattern, but the damage was done. A hitch is a tell. Mara knew my tells. Whatever this was, it knew enough to aim at them.

Her fingers pressed into my cheekbone, as if to turn my face toward her.

I didn’t let her. Not by resisting—resisting would be an admission—but by going limp. Dead weight. Like in sleep when someone tries to move you and you roll.

She let go. The mattress shifted. Her body leaned away.

The tension in my chest eased a fraction, and in that fraction my mind tried to rationalize again, desperate: She’s dreaming. She’s half-awake. She’s talking in her sleep.

Then, from somewhere below—down the hall, toward the stairs—I heard a soft sound. Not a creak. Not a pipe. A small, deliberate knock.

One. Two. Three.

My scalp prickled.

Mara’s breathing changed.

She turned her head toward the sound. I knew she did because the air moved and I caught the faint brush of hair against her shoulder. For the first time, she made a noise that sounded like annoyance.

“Tch.”

She shifted her weight as if considering getting up. The mattress dipped on her side. My muscles tensed. If she stood and left the room—

She didn’t. After a beat, she settled back down, still sitting upright. The patience returned, but now it felt shared with something else, something in the house that had knocked and been acknowledged.

I couldn’t keep track of how long she stayed there. My body started doing what bodies do when they’re exhausted: slipping. Not full sleep, but little drops, like losing your footing on stairs and catching yourself. Each time I dipped, panic jerked me back. I was terrified of being genuinely asleep, of giving her the whole of me without even the lie of resistance.

At some point, Mara lay down again. The mattress rose, the blanket eased. Her breath deepened, became human. The room lost its center. The fridge hummed. A car passed outside. Somewhere a dog barked once and stopped.

I waited until I was sure—until her breathing had that soft hitch at the end of the exhale that always meant she was really out. Then I opened my eyes.

The ceiling was a dim gray. The bars of streetlight on the wall had shifted. My eyes felt gritty.

Mara lay on her side facing away from me, one hand curled under her pillow. Her back rose and fell evenly. Her hair was a dark spill across the fabric.

She looked like herself. She looked like the woman who would, tomorrow morning, complain about spackle and ask if I wanted eggs.

My mouth tasted like blood. I realized my teeth had been pressed into my tongue.

I lay there until my alarm went off, not because I needed the alarm but because it gave me permission to move.

In the bathroom mirror, the skin under my eyes looked bruised. My tongue had a raw crescent where I’d bitten it. I rinsed and spat pink into the sink.

Mara knocked on the door with the back of her knuckles. “You okay in there?” she called. “You’ve been in there forever.”

“Yeah,” I said, voice thick. “Just—morning.”

When I opened the door, she was standing in the hallway in her robe, hair up, phone in hand. She looked at me and frowned.

“You didn’t sleep,” she said. Not a question.

“I slept,” I lied again, automatically. The lie was becoming muscle memory.

Mara’s gaze dropped to my mouth. For half a second, just half, her eyes lingered there in a way that made my stomach twist. Then she blinked and the moment passed.

“Eli.” She stepped closer, put her hand on my chest. Warm. Real. “If you’re stressed about the house, just say so. Don’t do the silent martyr thing.”

“I’m not,” I said, too fast. “It’s not that.”

She waited. Her hand stayed on my chest, as if feeling for something I wasn’t saying. Her thumb moved in a small, absent stroke against my shirt.

“You’re scaring me,” she said quietly, and I knew she meant my distance, my watchfulness. Not the thing I couldn’t name.

I almost told her. The words rose like a reflex: You sit up at night and stare at me. You touch my mouth. You ask me to stop pretending.

But the moment I imagined it, I imagined her face. Offended. Hurt. Angry. The argument that would follow—about trust, about being watched. About me being dramatic. About her being tired too.

And underneath all of that, the fear: what if I said it out loud and that made it more real?

“I’m fine,” I said. “It’s just… I need to get back on a normal sleep schedule.”

Mara exhaled through her nose, sharp. “Okay. Sure.” She stepped back, the warmth gone from her posture. “Just don’t shut me out, okay?”

“I won’t,” I said, and hated that I didn’t know if it was true.

She turned toward the kitchen. As she walked away, I noticed something on the back of her heel—pale dust caked in the crease where her skin folded.

Plaster dust.

We hadn’t sanded anything yesterday. We’d both been working. The hallway was still taped off.

I stared at her heel until she disappeared around the corner, and I felt the house around me: the narrow hall, the patched wall, the smell of old wood and new paint.

Downstairs, something in the pipes ticked once, like a knuckle against metal.

And in the kitchen, I heard Mara open the drawer where we kept the junk bowl—the place she’d said she’d toss the wrapped packet on her way out.

I heard the drawer slide shut again.

When I came in, the counter was clear. The packet was gone.

“Mara?” I said, trying to keep my voice casual.

She didn’t look up from the coffee machine. “What?”

“Did you… throw out that thing from the wall?”

“No,” she said, too quick, and then, after a beat, “I put it somewhere better. It was freaking me out, sitting there.”

My mouth went dry. “Where?”

Mara shrugged without turning around. “Somewhere. It’s just junk, Eli. Stop looking at it like it’s going to bite you.”

But her shoulders were tight. Her hands, when she lifted the mug, trembled a little.

And when she finally looked at me, her eyes flicked—just for a second—to my mouth, as if checking whether I was going to answer something I hadn’t heard.
step_outputs.8:
I tried to work that day. I sat at my desk with my headphones on and the waveform open on my screen, a man laughing in one ear, a sponsor script in the other. I moved breaths around. I cut ums and ers. I tuned out the hiss of the cheap mic. It was the kind of work that usually soothed me because it was simple: this sound belongs here, that sound doesn’t.

My mind kept drifting to the kitchen drawer sliding shut.

Somewhere better, Mara had said. Somewhere. She’d said it like it didn’t matter, like she’d put an expired coupon in the recycling. But I’d watched her shoulders. I’d watched the tiny tremor in her hand. She had been lying, or she had been frightened, and I couldn’t tell which was worse.

At noon I walked downstairs to get water and found her on a call in the living room, laptop open on her knees, hair twisted tight on top of her head. She was nodding, making agreeable sounds, doing her work-voice. When she saw me, she lifted a finger—one second—and turned away slightly.

I went into the kitchen and opened every drawer I could justify opening. Cutlery. Junk bowl. Spices. Dish towels. The packet was gone. No yellowed cloth. No twine. No gross little weight of it.

In the bottom drawer under the oven mitts I found an old receipt of mine, curled and faded. Grocery store, three months ago. I held it and felt my throat tighten with a stupid, irrational relief. Here was something that belonged to me that hadn’t moved.

I set the receipt back where it was and closed the drawer carefully.

That afternoon I caught myself watching Mara’s mouth when she spoke. The way her lips shaped the words. The way her tongue flashed briefly on a hard consonant. She noticed and asked, irritated, if I was even listening. I said yes. I apologized. I hated myself for the way my apologies came out—thin, functional. A patch on a crack.

By evening the air in the house felt too thick, as if the heat had nowhere to go. Mara came home from grabbing more spackle with a paper bag under her arm and a bright, forced energy that didn’t fit her tired eyes.

“Okay,” she announced, dropping the bag on the counter. “New rule. No work after seven. We’re sanding tomorrow and I refuse to be a zombie.”

I watched her move around the kitchen. She avoided the junk drawer without looking at it. She didn’t mention the packet again. When I tried to ask—casually, as if I didn’t care—she cut me off with a look.

“Don’t,” she said. “Please. Not tonight.”

So we ate in front of the TV like teenagers. A stupid reality show. Mara’s feet in my lap, her toes warm through my sweatpants. She laughed at the right places. Once, when her laughter didn’t reach her eyes, I felt a sharp sting of something like grief.

In bed she turned off her phone and put it face down. Another new rule.

“Come here,” she said, and pulled me toward her. She wanted closeness the way some people want air. Her hand slid under my shirt and rested on my ribs, feeling my breathing.

I lay there and let her hold me. The warmth of her was real. The rhythm of her chest against mine was real. My body wanted to surrender to it.

“Sorry I snapped,” she murmured into my shoulder. “I’m just… I don’t know. This house is getting to me.”

“It’s okay,” I said.

She lifted her head and looked at me. In the dark, I felt my muscles tense. But her gaze was normal—annoyed, soft, human.

“You’ve been weird,” she said. “Like you’re listening for something.”

I hesitated. My mouth felt like a liability.

Mara’s fingers pressed lightly into my ribs. “Tell me.”

I could have told her then. I could have said, you sit up at night and stare at my mouth, you touch my face like you’re checking it, you whisper like you’re practicing.

Instead I said, “The pipes. They’re loud.”

Mara stared at me a beat too long, as if deciding whether to let me get away with it. Then she sighed and rolled over. “Fine. Blame the plumbing.”

The light went out.

I lay on my back staring at the ceiling until my eyes burned. When Mara’s breathing deepened, I waited longer, listening for the tell. Soft hitch. The drop of her weight into the mattress. The room settling around her.

I didn’t want to pretend tonight. I wanted to watch, to know. But there’s no brave way to do that. There’s only the decision to break your own trust.

I kept my eyes closed anyway, a sliver of lid over them, and trained my attention on her side of the bed.

The mattress shifted.

My heart jumped. My body went rigid. I forced the breath out slowly through my nose, as if asleep.

She sat up.

There was no hesitation this time. No quiet minutes of waiting. As if she’d learned that the waiting didn’t pay off.

Her hand found my face faster, more certain. Fingers on my cheekbone, thumb near the corner of my mouth. A practiced grip. She turned my head slightly, not gently but not violent either—just efficient.

I let my head go with it, heavy. A dead thing.

Her breath was close. I smelled mint, and underneath it that stale, closed air.

“Eli,” she whispered.

The sound of my name in the dark made my stomach turn. It felt like bait.

I didn’t answer. I didn’t move.

Her thumb pressed into the corner of my mouth and tugged down, just enough to part my lips. A small invasion. Like a nurse checking a patient’s gums.

My tongue jerked back instinctively. She made a sound, a soft click of irritation.

The mattress beside me dipped again as she leaned closer. I felt her hair brush my cheek, cold and dry.

“Open,” she said.

Not Mara’s voice. Not fully. The same pitch, but the shape of the word was wrong—too flat, too careful, like someone who’d learned English from a recording.

My throat tightened. I kept my breathing slow. In for four, out for six. My tongue pressed to the roof of my mouth.

Her hand slid down to my throat again. Two fingers there, feeling for vibration. Testing.

“You can hear,” she whispered. “You can.”

A thud sounded downstairs, muted, as if something heavy had been set down on wood. Not a knock this time. A placement.

She paused. Turned her head toward the sound. I felt the shift in the air.

Then, to my shock, she stood up.

The mattress rose under her absence. Coolness moved in where her heat had been. The blanket slid slightly. I kept my face slack, eyes closed, heart hammering.

Bare feet padded across the bedroom floor, soft and careful. The door opened without a creak—Mara had oiled the hinges last week because she couldn’t stand the sound. She stepped into the hall.

I heard her descend the stairs.

The house held its breath with me. Every small noise sharpened. The fridge hum. The far-off rush of a car.

I opened my eyes, just a crack, and stared at the ceiling in the dim light. My hands were clenched. I forced them open.

Part of me wanted to run. To follow her. To turn on every light and shout her name until she snapped out of it or punched me. Anything. But I was afraid of catching her doing whatever she was doing and making it worse. Afraid that seeing would be a kind of answering.

I lay there and listened.

Downstairs, a drawer slid open. A pause. A faint rustle of cloth. Then the slow, deliberate sound of footsteps moving across the kitchen floor.

The fridge door opened, then closed.

Something metal clinked lightly, like a spoon set in a mug.

Then silence.

Minutes stretched. My eyes watered from staring. My body started to sink, despite myself, into that dangerous place between waking and sleep where thoughts smear.

A sound in the hallway snapped me back: footsteps returning up the stairs, slow and careful. The bedroom door opened. Mara’s feet padded in.

She stood by the bed. I could feel her presence like heat.

The mattress dipped as she sat down. Not on her side. Closer. At the edge near my hip.

I kept my eyes shut. My skin went tight with anticipation.

Something cold touched my chest through my shirt.

Not a hand. An object. Small and firm.

She placed it there and left her fingers on it a moment, as if anchoring it. The weight was familiar in a way my brain didn’t want to admit.

The packet.

Even through the fabric I could feel the contours of it: the knot of twine, the uneven lump inside, the slight sharp edge of something hard.

Her breath hovered over my face. I could hear the shallow sips again. She was watching.

Not my eyes. My mouth.

“Keep,” she whispered, and the word sounded like a command and a prayer at the same time.

I felt panic surge up hot and sudden. The urge to gasp, to sit up, to fling the packet away. I kept still by force. I kept the lie of sleep.

Her fingers slid from the packet to my jaw. Gentle pressure, turning my face up.

“Say,” she whispered.

My lips were dry. My tongue stuck to my teeth. I could feel the packet cooling my skin, stealing heat.

She waited. Patient.

A tear gathered at the corner of my eye—not from sadness, from strain, from the body’s stupid leak under pressure. It slid toward my hairline.

Her thumb moved to the corner of my mouth again. Pressed.

“Say it back,” she murmured, and something in the cadence thrilled, like a child waiting for a parent to repeat a word.

I thought of Mara in daylight. Her laugh at the TV. Her warm foot in my lap. The way she’d said, Don’t shut me out.

My chest tightened so hard it hurt. My body wanted to speak to her, to pull her back into herself with her name.

I pressed my tongue hard against the roof of my mouth. Hard enough that my jaw cramped.

The pressure at my mouth increased. Not cruel, but insistent. A teacher correcting posture. A mother pushing food onto a fork.

I kept still. I kept breathing. In for four, out for six.

At last—after a span of time too long to measure—her hand released my jaw. The thumb left the corner of my mouth. The weight of her attention lifted slightly, like a shadow moving away.

She took the packet from my chest. The coldness left behind a faint ache.

The mattress dipped as she leaned down, close to my ear.

“If you won’t,” she whispered, and the voice was almost Mara’s now, almost affectionate, “then I will keep trying.”

She lay down.

The blankets shifted. The mattress resettled under her. Her breathing deepened, became the familiar rhythm. A soft hitch on the exhale. Sleep.

I lay there until my muscles started to cramp with stillness. Then, carefully, I reached across the bed in the dark, hand searching.

Mara’s side was warm. Her body was there.

I slid my hand lower, toward the edge of the mattress, toward where the packet had been. My fingers brushed fabric—crumpled, tucked near the bedframe.

I pulled it out.

In the thin light from the blinds, the packet looked more obscene than it had in the kitchen. The cloth was yellowed and damp at one corner, as if it had been in someone’s mouth. The twine was tight, knotted with a care that made my skin crawl.

I held it at arm’s length and realized my hand was shaking.

On my chest, under my shirt, a faint cold lingered. When I pressed my fingers there, I felt the tender outline of something—not a bruise, not yet, but a soreness as if skin had been pressed too long by a small hard shape.

Beside me, Mara sighed in her sleep and rolled closer, her arm flinging over my waist in the familiar, thoughtless way she always did.

Her hand settled just above my mouth.

In the morning, when she woke, she would have no memory of bringing the packet into our room and placing it on my chest like an offering.

And I didn’t know how many more nights I could keep pretending without eventually, stupidly, mercifully answering.
step_outputs.9:
Mara woke with her hand on my throat.

Not gripping. Just resting there, palm warm against my skin, fingers curved into the hollow at the base of my neck as if she’d fallen asleep mid-check. Her face was soft with sleep. Her mouth was slightly open. She looked harmless.

My eyes snapped open anyway. My body had learned the pattern: hand, breath, waiting.

“Mara,” I said before I could stop myself.

Her eyelids fluttered. She blinked up at me, unfocused. “Mm?”

Her hand tightened reflexively, the way anyone’s does when they’re startled. For half a second it was pressure. My breath caught. Then she let go, frowning, and her hand slid down to my chest.

“What?” she muttered. “Why are you saying my name like that?”

I swallowed. My throat felt tender where her palm had been, as if the skin had remembered pressure from the night. “Nothing. Sorry. You were—your hand was…”

Mara looked down at where her hand lay, then up at my face again. Irritation flared. “My hand was what? Touching you? In our bed?”

“I didn’t mean—” I sat up, the sheets sticking to my back. The room was already too bright; the blinds made hard lines of sunlight. “It’s just… you’ve been doing stuff at night.”

Mara’s expression changed in stages. Confusion first, then offense like a door slamming. “Doing stuff.”

“Sleepwalking,” I said quickly, as if the word could pad the accusation. “Maybe. Or talking in your sleep. I don’t know. You keep sitting up.”

Mara pushed herself up on one elbow. Her hair stuck out at odd angles, making her look younger, softer. The softness didn’t last. “So what, you want me to—what? Apologize for being asleep?”

My tongue felt thick. I hated this part, the part where language failed. “You brought that… packet into the room last night.”

The color drained from her face, then came back in a rush. “What packet.”

“The thing from the wall. The cloth thing.”

Mara’s eyes flicked away, toward the dresser, toward the corner of the room as if the object might be visible. Then she looked back at me with anger sharpened by fear. “That is not funny.”

“I’m not joking.”

“You’re saying I got up, went downstairs, got that gross thing, and put it on you.” She laughed once, harsh. “Why would I do that?”

“I don’t know,” I said. “That’s the point. I don’t know.”

Mara stared at me. Her gaze dropped—without her meaning to, I think—to my mouth. She looked away quickly, as if caught.

“You’re making me feel like I’m crazy,” she said, and her voice cracked a little on crazy. “Like I’m some… I don’t know. Like I’m dangerous.”

“I’m not trying to—”

“You recorded me,” she blurted, and there it was, the other wound. “You recorded me and you didn’t tell me. And now you’re telling me I’m sleepwalking around like a freak and putting trash on you in the middle of the night.”

My stomach sank. “I shouldn’t have recorded you. I’m sorry. I deleted it.”

It came out too smoothly. Another lie. I watched her face for the reaction, the way I’d watched her mouth in the dark. I hated myself for it.

Mara swung her legs out of bed. She stood, robe falling open, and yanked it closed with a hard tug. “Where is it,” she demanded.

“What?”

“The packet. If I did it, where is it now?”

I hesitated. I’d shoved it under my side of the mattress before dawn, like a teenager hiding cigarettes. The act had felt pathetic and also protective. If it was in the open, she’d throw it out. If she threw it out and something got worse, I would never forgive myself.

Mara saw the hesitation and her face went flat. “Oh my god. You have it. You hid it.”

“I didn’t—”

“You did.” Her voice rose. “Jesus, Eli. This is—this is you. This is you trying to make things make sense by controlling them. Like when you were convinced the neighbor’s dog barking meant someone was casing the house and you wouldn’t let me go for a run at night.”

“That was different.”

“It was not different.” She rubbed her face with both hands, hard, as if trying to erase the conversation. “I’m going to work. I can’t do this before coffee.”

“Mara, please.” I reached for her wrist. She jerked away.

Her eyes were bright. “Stop. Just—stop watching me like I’m not me.”

The words hit harder than I expected because they were true in the worst possible way. I watched her storm into the bathroom. The shower started. Water pounded. Steam filled the hall.

I sat on the edge of the bed and stared at the floor. My tongue still ached from where I’d bitten it. My throat felt tender. My chest felt cold under my shirt where the packet had been placed, as if that cold could be remembered.

When Mara left, she didn’t kiss me goodbye. She didn’t say love you. She grabbed her keys with a tight hand and slammed the front door hard enough to rattle the picture frame we still hadn’t hung.

The house fell quiet.

I called a clinic because it was the thing I could do that looked reasonable. I said the right words: trouble sleeping, new environment, stress. I didn’t say: my partner sits up and stares at my mouth and asks me to answer.

The nurse who called me back had a calm voice that made me feel immediately ashamed of my own. She asked about caffeine, alcohol, screens. She asked if I had nightmares. She asked if I ever felt paralyzed on waking.

“No,” I lied to that too. My body wasn’t paralyzed. I chose stillness. That choice felt important in a way I couldn’t explain.

She suggested basic sleep hygiene, suggested melatonin, suggested a low dose prescription if it persisted. She suggested, gently, that moving into an old house and doing renovation work could spike anxiety.

“Sometimes our brains look for patterns,” she said.

“Yeah,” I said, gripping the phone so hard my fingers went numb. “Yeah, I know.”

After I hung up, I sat at my desk and listened to the house. The fridge. The pipes. A faint draft whispering in the hall where the plaster had been opened and patched. The sound of my own breath.

I went to the kitchen drawer—the junk bowl drawer—and opened it. It held tape measures, dead batteries, a roll of twine, two mismatched screws, Mara’s spare hair ties. The packet wasn’t there.

I lifted things out one by one, ridiculous with hope. Nothing.

I went to the hall closet. Under the sink. The linen cabinet. I checked behind the cereal boxes like I was looking for a cat.

My heart pounded harder with each empty space.

Finally I went back upstairs and dropped to my knees beside the bed. I lifted the corner of the mattress and reached underneath.

My fingers closed around cloth.

Relief and dread came together, indistinguishable. I pulled the packet out. It was heavier than it should have been. The cloth was damp at the corner again, smelling faintly of saliva. The knot of twine was tighter, as if someone had retied it.

I held it in both hands and felt the urge to throw it as hard as I could. Out the window. Into the street. Into the neighbor’s yard.

Instead I carried it downstairs like a fragile thing and set it on the kitchen counter in the daylight.

It looked almost silly there, between the fruit bowl and the stack of mail. A piece of old cloth. Twine. A lump.

I stared at it until my eyes ached. Then I did what I always did when I couldn’t stand my own thoughts: I tried to make the world measurable.

I found a small plastic container—leftover takeout—and put the packet inside. The lid snapped shut with a sound that made me flinch. I slid the container into the back of the pantry, behind the bags of rice.

Then I took a piece of masking tape and wrote KEEP OUT in block letters and stuck it on the pantry door.

It was childish. It made me feel slightly safer anyway.

When Mara came home, she was quieter. The sharpness of the morning argument had cooled into something heavy. She moved around the kitchen without meeting my eyes, washed her hands, leaned against the counter.

“Sorry,” she said finally, staring at the floor. “I shouldn’t have—whatever. I shouldn’t have blown up.”

“I’m sorry too,” I said. I meant it.

She nodded once. Her gaze flicked toward the pantry door, caught on the masking tape. “What’s that.”

“Nothing,” I said too quickly.

Mara’s head lifted. The anger came back like a reflex. “Are you kidding me?”

“It’s—” I stopped. The lies were stacking up and each one felt like another nail in the house. “I put the packet away.”

Her eyes widened. “So you didn’t delete the recording. You didn’t throw out the… thing. You’re just—managing me.”

“I’m managing it,” I said, and even as I said it I knew how it sounded.

Mara’s mouth tightened. She pressed her tongue against the inside of her cheek—a tell. I saw it and felt, absurdly, grateful. A human tell. A Mara tell.

“You don’t get to decide what happens to my body,” she said, low. “If you think I’m doing something, you wake me up. You don’t sit there and—” She gestured toward the bedroom, toward the dark. “And play dead.”

I opened my mouth. Closed it. In my mind I saw her eyes in the dark again, fixed on the same place.

“If I wake you up,” I said carefully, “and you’re not… you, what then?”

Mara stared at me. For a moment she looked genuinely afraid—not of me, of something behind my words. “What do you mean,” she whispered.

I shook my head. “Forget it.”

“No.” Her voice was hard. “Don’t do that. Don’t make me beg you to tell me what’s happening.”

I wanted to tell her. I wanted to lay it all out in daylight and force it into sense. But my throat closed around the words. Because I could still hear that whisper: If you’re listening, you can stop pretending.

Because a part of me believed that naming it would invite it to sit up in daylight too.

“I don’t know,” I said, and the helplessness in it surprised me. “I just know it’s getting worse.”

Mara looked down at her hands. Her fingers were raw at the knuckles, cracked from plaster dust and washing. She flexed them like they hurt.

“I woke up with my wrist hurting yesterday,” she said quietly. “Like I’d been gripping something too hard. I didn’t tell you because I didn’t want you to—” She stopped. Swallowed. “I didn’t want you to look at me like that.”

I stared. “Show me.”

She hesitated, then held out her wrist. In the fading afternoon light, there was a faint bruise on the inside—oval, like a thumbprint. Not fresh. Just beginning.

My stomach rolled.

Mara watched my face. “So,” she said, voice thin, “what now? What do we do?”

I didn’t have an answer that wasn’t ridiculous. The clinic. Sleep hygiene. Melatonin. KEEP OUT on the pantry door.

“Tonight,” I said, and my voice sounded like it belonged to someone else, “I’m going to stay awake.”

Mara’s eyes flicked—without her meaning to—to my mouth.

Then she looked away, and in that small movement I saw how tired she was, how scared, how much she was holding herself upright by force.

“Okay,” she said. “But if you see me—if I do something—wake me up. Promise.”

I tried to say yes.

The word caught behind my teeth like it didn’t want to leave. Like it knew it would be used.

Mara waited.

I forced it out. “I promise.”

Her shoulders loosened a fraction. She nodded. “Good.”

That night, when we turned out the light, her hand found mine under the blanket and held on too tight, as if to keep herself tethered.

I lay there with my eyes open, staring at the ceiling, listening to her breath and to the house’s quiet machinery.

And when the mattress shifted, hours later, my body surged with dread—not because I was surprised.

Because I wasn’t.
step_outputs.10:
I didn’t manage to stay awake.

I told myself I would—coffee after dinner, phone in the other room, the bedside lamp left on low. I sat up against the headboard with a book I wasn’t reading and watched Mara’s face as she drifted off. I watched for the moment her features softened, for the slackening that meant she’d let go. I listened for the hitch at the end of her exhale.

Mara fell asleep with her hand on my forearm like an anchor. Even in sleep she held on, fingers tightening now and then as if the dark tried to pull her away.

The lamp threw a pool of honeyed light on the bedside table. Shadows gathered beyond it: the corner where the laundry basket sat, the doorway, the pale strip of hall beyond. The house was quiet in its nightly way, the kind of quiet that made each little sound—pipe tick, distant car—feel like a message.

At some point my eyes burned and I blinked longer than I meant to.

My head dipped. My thoughts blurred. The page of the book became a field of black marks. I jerked myself back up once, twice, like catching your chin before it hits your chest. I read the same sentence three times. The fourth time the words slid sideways and turned into something else.

When I woke, the lamp was still on and the book had slipped open on my lap. My mouth was dry. My neck ached from the awkward angle.

And Mara was sitting up.

She didn’t move like someone waking. There was no fumbling, no groan, no blink of adjustment. She was simply there, upright in the bed as if she’d been placed.

Her hand was no longer on my arm. It rested on her own thigh, palm down. Still.

Her head was turned toward me.

Her eyes were trained on my mouth.

The fact that the light was on made it worse. In the dim amber I could see too much: the slight shine in her eyes, the way her pupils didn’t quite match—one a fraction larger than the other, as if the light didn’t matter. The skin under her eyes looked stretched, as though she hadn’t blinked enough. Her lips were parted just enough to show the edge of her teeth.

My heart slammed once, hard. I kept my face slack. I let my eyes stay half-lidded, the way they would in sleep. I forced my breathing into an even rhythm and prayed she couldn’t hear the way my pulse was trying to climb out of my throat.

She watched. Patient.

Then she leaned closer.

The mattress dipped. The book shifted on my lap and slid a little. Her gaze flicked, tracking the movement, then returned to my mouth with a hunger so focused it felt like a physical thing.

“Eli,” she whispered.

My name landed in the room like a handprint. Not her voice exactly—her pitch, yes, but stripped. Too careful around the consonants. Like she was trying not to waste sound.

I didn’t answer.

Her eyes narrowed slightly, not in anger—concentration. She shifted her weight, the blanket sliding down her waist. Her bare knees emerged, pale in the lamplight.

Her hand came up and hovered near my face, two fingers poised.

“Hey,” she whispered again, softer, coaxing. “It’s okay.”

The words were Mara’s. The tone was Mara’s—the tone she used when I got a toothache and complained about it like I was dying, the tone she used when she found me sitting in the car after my dad’s funeral because I couldn’t go into the house. It was so perfect it made my throat ache.

I almost answered out of reflex. I almost let my mouth do what it does when the person you love speaks to you: respond.

Instead I swallowed.

Her gaze snapped to my throat. Her fingers pressed lightly at the corner of my mouth, not tugging this time—just resting there, sensing.

I kept my lips closed. My tongue pressed hard against the roof of my mouth until my jaw trembled.

Mara’s hand slid down to my chest and pressed flat over my sternum, feeling the rise and fall. Her palm was cool.

“You’re awake,” she whispered.

The statement was not angry. It was delighted. As if she’d been hoping.

I let my breath continue. In for four, out for six. A steady sleep-breath.

Her fingers on my chest tapped twice, a little impatient. “You can stop pretending.”

A laugh bubbled up in my chest, not humor—panic. It threatened to escape as a breathy sound. I held it in. My eyes stung.

She stared at me for a long beat. Then, very slowly, her head tilted.

The tilt wasn’t Mara. Mara didn’t do that. It was too precise, too controlled. Like a bird listening for something in the ground.

“Okay,” she whispered. “Okay.”

She shifted back. The mattress eased. For a second I thought she was going to lie down, that the moment would pass, that I’d get to wake her in the morning and pretend this was a bad stretch of sleep.

Instead she reached toward the bedside table and picked up my mug.

The mug was empty—yesterday’s tea, cold and stale at the bottom. She held it in both hands and stared into it as if it contained an answer.

Then she brought it to her mouth and tipped it as if drinking. Nothing came out. The motion made no sense. When she lowered it, a faint wet sheen glistened on her lower lip, but the mug was dry.

My skin went cold.

She set the mug down carefully. Too carefully. Then she looked at the lamp.

She stared at the lamp the way she stared at my mouth. With intent.

Slowly, deliberately, she reached out and turned it off.

Darkness flooded in.

My breath hitched. I caught it, but not in time. A tell. An admission.

Mara’s head swung back toward me immediately. In the dark, her eyes were black pools. I couldn’t see the shine anymore. I could only feel them.

“There,” she whispered. “There you are.”

Her hand found my face without fumbling. Fingers on my cheek, thumb at the corner of my mouth. This grip again—practiced. Familiar.

She pressed her thumb gently, insistently, pulling my lip down.

“Say it,” she whispered.

The words were so close to my ear that I felt the vibration of them. I smelled mint and something sour underneath, like old damp cloth.

I kept my lips closed. The pressure at the corner of my mouth increased. Her thumb dug in, not enough to hurt, enough to make my mouth want to open.

“Say,” she repeated. “Say—”

From somewhere in the house, not downstairs this time but closer, came that soft knock.

One. Two. Three.

In the hall.

My blood turned to ice. The sound was too near, too intimate. Like knuckles on the inside of our bedroom door.

Mara paused. Her hand remained on my face. Her breathing changed, shallow sips.

She turned her head toward the door, listening. Her thumb relaxed, as if the knock had distracted her.

Then she leaned down, close to my mouth again, and whispered, with a tone almost like a secret shared, “It’s waiting too.”

I couldn’t stop myself. My eyes opened.

Not fully. Just a crack.

In the darkness I saw the outline of her face inches from mine, the pale oval of it, the darkness where her eyes were. Her lips were close enough that if I spoke the air from my mouth would touch her.

And in that breath of space between us, I understood what she wanted with a clarity that made me nauseated: not my fear, not my pain.

My response.

The simple, human thing you do without thinking when someone says your name in the dark. You answer. You confirm. You make them real.

My mouth twitched.

Mara—no, whatever was using Mara—went utterly still, like a predator hearing movement.

“Mm,” it whispered, pleased. “Yes.”

I clamped my jaw shut so hard my teeth ached. I forced my eyes to close again, as if I’d been dreaming. As if I hadn’t just given it the smallest proof that I was in there.

Her hand tightened on my jaw. She turned my face a fraction toward hers, more insistently now.

“Look at me,” she whispered, and the words had a softness to them that made my stomach lurch. Mara’s softness. Mara’s care. Used like a key.

The knock came again. Two taps this time. Closer than close. As if from inside the room.

I felt an irrational urge to look at the door, to check. But that would require moving, and moving was an admission too. Everything was an admission.

Mara’s breath was in my ear. “Say it back,” she murmured. “Eli. Say—”

A sound tore itself out of me before I could stop it: a small, involuntary whimper of exhaustion and fear. Not a word. A broken animal noise.

She froze.

Then, very slowly, her head lifted. She sat back, still holding my jaw, and in the dark I could feel her attention narrowing, focusing on that sound. Like the entity had tasted it and liked it.

“Again,” she whispered. “Do it again.”

I squeezed my eyes shut so hard my temples hurt. I pressed my tongue to the roof of my mouth until it cramped. I thought of Mara in daylight, furious and frightened, telling me not to watch her like she wasn’t her. I thought of her bruise. I thought of her hand on my forearm, anchoring herself.

“Mara,” I whispered, barely audible. Not to answer it. To find her.

The moment the name left my mouth, even as a breath, the air changed.

Mara’s grip on my jaw loosened—then tightened again, not angry, satisfied.

“Yes,” she whispered. “Yes. That’s it.”

My stomach dropped. I hadn’t meant to. I hadn’t meant to give it anything. But it had taken even that—my whisper, my slip—and held it up like proof.

Her thumb pressed at the corner of my mouth again, tenderly, almost lovingly. “Say it,” she whispered. “Say you hear me.”

I didn’t. I couldn’t.

The mattress shifted abruptly. Mara’s body jerked, as if yanked by a string. Her hand fell from my face. She made a strangled sound—half gasp, half sob.

I sat up, eyes snapping open, no longer caring about pretending. “Mara!”

She convulsed once, hard. Her back arched. Then she slumped forward, hands gripping the sheet.

I fumbled for the lamp, knocking my mug over. It clattered on the bedside table and rolled, the sound absurdly loud. I twisted the switch.

Light.

Mara was on her hands and knees on the bed, hair falling over her face. Her shoulders shook. She made a low noise, like she was trying to breathe through something thick.

“Mara,” I said again, and this time it was not a whisper in the dark. It was a command. A plea. “Hey. Hey. Look at me.”

Her head lifted slowly. Her eyes blinked—rapid, human blinking, like someone coming up from underwater. She looked at me with a raw confusion that made my throat burn.

“What,” she whispered, hoarse. “What is—”

She swallowed. Her gaze flicked around the room as if trying to locate herself. Then her eyes landed on my face, on my mouth, and something like horror flashed there—not hunger, not focus.

Fear.

“I was—” she started, and then her face twisted. “I was dreaming.”

“You were sitting up,” I said, voice shaking now that I’d broken the stillness. “You—you turned the lamp off.”

Mara stared at the lamp like it was an accusation. “I—no, I didn’t.” She rubbed her face hard with both hands. “I didn’t. I swear. I was asleep. I was—”

She broke off and grabbed her own wrist, as if it hurt. She looked down, breath coming fast. “Why does my arm—”

I reached for her. She flinched away, not from me, from the idea of being touched.

“I’m sorry,” she whispered, and it was so small it didn’t sound like her. “I’m sorry. Eli, I’m sorry.”

I wanted to tell her it wasn’t her. I wanted to believe it.

But my jaw still ached where her thumb had pressed. The corner of my mouth felt bruised. When I touched it, my fingertips came away damp—saliva, not blood.

Mara looked at my hand, at my wet fingertips, and her face went white.

“Oh my god,” she whispered.

We sat there in the lamplight, both of us breathing too fast, the bed a mess around us like a storm had passed.

Somewhere in the house, in the quiet that followed, something tapped once—soft, almost polite—like knuckles on wood.

Mara’s eyes snapped toward the door.

She looked at me, pupils wide. “Did you hear that?”

I opened my mouth to answer.

Then I remembered the way it had whispered yes when I said her name, and my throat closed around the word.

I nodded instead. Just once.

Mara swallowed hard. “Eli,” she said, and her voice shook, “I don’t want to sleep here anymore.”
step_outputs.11:
We didn’t leave.

Not that night. Not the next day. Leaving would have meant deciding where to go, explaining to someone why, packing in daylight while pretending nothing was wrong. Leaving would have meant admitting—out loud, to each other, to a friend, to a landlord—that something had colonized our bedroom.

Mara called in sick. She almost never did. She sat at the kitchen table in sweatpants and an old T-shirt, a mug of coffee cooling untouched in front of her, and stared at her hands like they belonged to someone else.

I tried to keep moving because when I stopped my mind replayed the night in loops: my mouth opening, the whispered yes, the satisfaction like a lock turning. I wiped the counters that didn’t need wiping. I threw out junk mail. I reorganized a drawer and then had to put it back because Mara’s systems were different from mine and the sight of my order upset her.

Around noon, Mara lifted her head and said, “Show me.”

“What?”

“The packet,” she said, jaw tight. “The thing you hid. Show me what you’re so obsessed with.”

Her voice had that flat tone she got when she was forcing herself not to spiral. I could see the effort in her shoulders, in the way her knees bounced under the table.

I went to the pantry and pulled the plastic container out from behind the rice. The masking tape label—KEEP OUT—looked childish in daylight. I felt my face burn.

Mara watched me bring it to the table. She didn’t reach for it. She stared at the container as if it might move on its own.

“Open it,” she said.

My fingers hesitated on the lid. The snap of plastic felt suddenly too loud, too final. I could still feel the packet’s cold weight on my chest.

Mara’s eyes narrowed. “Eli.”

I popped the lid.

The smell hit first: old cloth, faint mildew, and underneath it the stale, closed-air smell I’d noticed on Mara’s breath at night. Like a cabinet that had been shut for years.

The packet sat inside, wrapped in yellowed fabric. The cloth was frayed at one edge, damp-stained at the corner as if someone had held it in their mouth. Twine bound it tight. The knot was intricate, careful.

Mara leaned forward slowly, as if approaching an injured animal. She didn’t touch it. She stared at the knot.

“That wasn’t like that,” she whispered.

“What do you mean?”

“The knot,” Mara said. She swallowed. “When I found it in the wall, it was… messy. Like whoever wrapped it didn’t care. Or like it had been undone and redone too many times.” She lifted her gaze to me. “This looks… fresh.”

I felt my stomach roll. “I didn’t retie it.”

“I know,” she said quickly, and then her face tightened as if she hated that she knew. “I know you didn’t.”

For a while we just sat there, the packet between us like a third person at the table. Outside, the neighborhood went on: a lawnmower, a child yelling, a car door slamming. The normal world pressed against the windows, ignorant.

Mara reached out and, with one finger, touched the cloth lightly. The smallest contact. She flinched as if it shocked her.

“Okay,” she said, too bright. “Okay. We’re being insane. This is—this is some old house superstition thing. Someone wrapped hair in cloth. People do weird stuff.” She laughed once, brittle. “It’s probably a dead pet or something. Christ.”

“There’s something inside,” I said.

Mara’s eyes flashed. “Don’t.”

“I’m not trying to—” I stopped. My mouth felt dangerous again. I lowered my voice. “We can open it. See what it is.”

Mara stared at me, fear and anger battling across her face. Then she shook her head hard. “No. No. Absolutely not. If it’s—if it’s anything biological, we are not opening it at our kitchen table.”

“I can take it outside. Gloves.”

Mara’s breathing quickened. “Eli, I don’t want to see what it is. I don’t want it to become real in my head.” She pressed her palms flat on the table, grounding herself. “Just—put it back. Put it somewhere. Or throw it out. Burn it. I don’t care.”

“You care,” I said quietly.

Mara’s gaze snapped up. She looked like she might deny it, then her face softened in defeat. “I care because it was in our wall,” she admitted. “It was in our house. It feels like… like someone left a tooth under our pillow and now we’re supposed to—” She broke off, shaking her head. “I don’t know. It feels like a message.”

A silence settled. In that silence, something in me loosened enough to say the part I’d been holding back.

“It wants me to answer,” I said.

Mara stared. “What.”

“At night,” I said, and my voice sounded too calm for the words, as if saying them softly could make them less absurd. “When you sit up—when it’s… happening—your eyes are on my mouth. Not my eyes. Like it’s waiting for something. It says my name. It says hey. It tells me to stop pretending. And when I—” I swallowed hard. “When I slipped and said your name, it… liked it.”

Mara’s face drained again. She looked down, pressing her tongue to the inside of her cheek. When she spoke her voice was barely there.

“I’ve been feeling watched since we moved in,” she said. “Not like paranoia. Like… like the house knows where I am. Like when you get that sensation of someone behind you, but you turn around and there’s nothing.” Her fingers curled around the edge of the table. “I didn’t tell you because I didn’t want to be the girl who thinks a house is haunted.”

My chest tightened. “Why didn’t you tell me?”

Mara gave a humorless smile. “Because you’d do exactly what you’re doing. You’d listen. You’d watch. You’d try to fix it. And then if it got worse, it would feel like it was my fault for saying it out loud.”

I had no defense. My throat hurt with the truth of it.

Mara stared at the packet again. “Maybe it’s like… a charm,” she said. “Like somebody tried to keep something out. Or keep something in.”

“Keep,” I said, remembering the whisper. The word had sounded like an instruction.

Mara nodded slowly, as if the word resonated somewhere deep. “When I found it, there was paper with it. A scrap. I didn’t tell you that either.”

My stomach dropped. “There was a note?”

Mara stood abruptly and went to the hall closet. She rummaged through the box where we’d tossed random renovation debris—old outlet covers, loose nails, the cracked plaster chunks. She pulled out a folded scrap of paper sealed in a plastic sandwich bag.

“I put it in here because it was gross,” she said, as if that explained everything.

She brought it back and slid it across the table to me. The paper inside was browned and brittle, edges crumbling. Ink had bled into it like spilled tea, leaving only faint strokes.

I opened the bag carefully and unfolded the scrap with trembling fingers.

Most of it was illegible. But in the center, three words repeated, written heavier as if the writer had pressed hard in desperation.

KEEP.

DO NOT ANSWER.

SLEEP.

My mouth went dry. The words looked like they’d been written by someone whose hand shook. The letters were cramped, angular. At one corner there was a smudge that might have been a fingerprint—or a tear stain.

Mara leaned in, reading over my shoulder. Her breath caught. “Oh my god.”

I stared at the words until they blurred. My brain tried to fit them into a rational shape: a prank, a warning from a previous tenant, a note from someone with a sleep disorder. But the simplicity of it felt older than that. Like someone had learned a rule the hard way and was begging whoever came after not to repeat their mistake.

Mara sat back slowly, hand rising to cover her mouth. For a second her eyes—normal eyes—were fixed on my lips. She lowered her hand quickly as if she’d caught herself.

“So,” she said, voice unsteady, “we… we just keep sleeping? That’s—”

“That’s the opposite of what we’ve been doing,” I said. “I’ve been staying awake. Pretending.”

Mara’s gaze dropped to the paper again. “Do not answer,” she whispered, and the words sounded like a prayer. “Like—like it’s a rule.”

My throat tightened. “I already did. I said your name.”

Mara’s eyes filled, but no tears fell. “You said my name,” she repeated, as if tasting how terrible and ordinary it was. “That’s not—Eli, that’s not a sin.”

“It felt like one,” I said.

We sat there with the packet and the paper between us. Outside, the afternoon light shifted. Dust motes drifted in the sunbeam like slow snow.

Mara suddenly shoved her chair back. “We’re not sleeping in the same room tonight,” she said, voice hardening as she grabbed at control. “No. I’m not letting it have—whatever this is—both of us. One of us has to be awake enough to—”

“To answer?” I said, and my voice came out sharper than I meant. “That’s what it wants.”

Mara froze. Her jaw clenched.

“Fine,” she snapped, and then her voice broke. “Then what do you want? Tell me what you want.”

I didn’t know. I wanted to go back to three weeks ago when our biggest problem was a cracked window latch. I wanted to pick the packet up with tongs and throw it into a river. I wanted to sleep for a year.

“I want you to be safe,” I said.

Mara laughed, bitter. “Safe from me?”

“Safe from whatever’s using you,” I said, and immediately regretted the wording when her face hardened.

“I’m right here,” she said. “I’m not—Eli, I’m not a costume.”

“I know,” I said, too quickly, too desperately. “I know you’re not.”

Mara turned away, pressing both hands to her eyes. When she spoke again her voice was muffled. “Okay. Okay. Tonight we do it different. We keep the light on. We keep the door open. We—” She lowered her hands and looked at the packet with disgust and fear. “We put that thing back where it came from.”

“In the wall,” I said.

Mara nodded. “In the wall. We seal it up. We patch it. We—keep it.”

The word keep sat in the air between us. It tasted wrong.

“Okay,” I said. My voice sounded like someone agreeing to bury something.

That evening, we went into the hallway with a putty knife and a flashlight. The patch where we’d opened the plaster was half-finished. Mara pried at the edge with shaking hands until the piece of drywall flexed and came free, revealing the dark cavity behind it.

Cold air breathed out.

Mara held the packet at arm’s length as if it might bite. “I hate this,” she whispered.

“I know,” I said, and meant it in a way I hadn’t meant most of my apologies lately.

She slid the packet into the cavity. It disappeared into darkness like it had never existed. For a second her hand hovered there, as if reluctant to let go. Then she yanked her hand back fast, wiping it on her pants.

I put the scrap of paper in too, after a hesitation. It felt wrong to keep it. It also felt wrong to destroy it. The only thing that made sense was returning it to the place it had been kept.

Mara watched. “Do you think whoever wrote that… lived here?” she asked.

“I don’t know,” I said. “But they knew.”

We patched the hole. Mara smeared spackle with more force than necessary. The smell of wet plaster filled the hall.

When it was done, we stood in the hallway staring at the smooth white patch like it was a bandage over an artery.

Mara exhaled shakily. “Okay,” she said. “Okay. We did something.”

That night, when we went to bed, she insisted the lamp stay on. She insisted the bedroom door stay open. She insisted we sleep with our backs to the wall, as if making ourselves smaller could make us safer.

Her hand found mine under the blanket. It was clammy. “Don’t answer,” she whispered in the dark, and her voice shook on the word answer like it was sharp.

“I won’t,” I whispered back.

We lay there, trying to let sleep take us the way you let anesthesia take you—by surrendering even though you’re afraid of what happens when you’re gone.

Hours passed. Or minutes. My sense of time dissolved in exhaustion.

Then I felt the mattress shift.

And in the open doorway, from the hallway we’d patched, the air grew colder as if the house itself had leaned in to listen.
step_outputs.12:
We tried to live around it.

The day after we sealed the packet back into the wall, Mara went to work like a person who had decided that normal behavior was a shield. She put on mascara. She wore jeans instead of sweatpants. She kissed me goodbye at the door with a firmness that felt like an oath.

“Don’t do anything,” she said.

“I won’t,” I lied, because doing nothing had become impossible.

The patch in the hallway dried smooth and pale. I sanded it lightly because I couldn’t stand the raised edge. The sanding released fine white dust that drifted into the air and settled on the baseboards like ash. I vacuumed it up. I kept vacuuming. I kept the house clean the way you keep a wound clean: to prevent infection, to pretend you can control what you can’t see.

That night we didn’t share a bed.

It was Mara’s idea at first, said briskly over dinner as if it were a sleep experiment: “One of us should get real rest. We’ll rotate. I take the bedroom, you take the couch.”

“I should take the couch,” I said. My voice came out too eager. I wanted distance from the room, from the place where the staring lived.

Mara’s jaw tightened. “No. I’m not banishing you like a dog. And I’m not sleeping alone in there if I’m the—” She stopped, swallowed, forced the word. “If I’m the problem.”

“You’re not the problem,” I said automatically.

Mara gave me a look that said stop trying to fix my feelings. “Then don’t treat me like I am,” she said.

We compromised in the way exhausted people compromise: badly. Mara took the bedroom with the lamp on and the door open. I took the couch with the TV muted to a blue glow because the dark felt too thick. We both pretended this arrangement was temporary.

At midnight Mara texted from the bedroom: *u awake?*

I stared at my phone. The answer was obvious. I didn’t want to put the words into the air, even through a screen.

*yeah* I typed, then deleted it. My thumb hovered.

Another text came: *i’m scared. don’t laugh.*

*I’m not laughing,* I typed back. *I’m here.*

A minute later: *don’t answer her if it happens.*

Her.

My stomach clenched. I stared at that word like it might change if I blinked.

The house settled. The pipes ticked. The fridge hummed. And under those familiar sounds there was something else now that I couldn’t locate—a faint, rhythmic thump, like a heartbeat in a wall.

I lay on the couch and listened until my eyes burned.

At some point I must have dozed, because I woke with my cheek stuck to the fabric and my arm asleep. The TV had gone to a black screen with a faint reflection of the room. My phone lay on my chest, the screen dark.

I sat up, heart pounding, trying to orient. The house was quiet in that deep-night way. The kind of quiet that makes you feel like you’re the only person left.

Then I saw her.

Mara stood in the doorway between the living room and the hall, bare feet on the wood, hair loose down her shoulders. She wore the T-shirt she slept in. In the dim light from the street outside, her face was a pale oval.

She was staring at me.

Not at my eyes.

At my mouth.

My breath caught. I tasted panic, metallic and immediate.

I held still. A ridiculous instinct, since I was already sitting up. But my body had learned stillness as a form of camouflage. If I didn’t move, maybe it would think I wasn’t fully awake. If I didn’t speak, maybe it would get bored.

Mara’s lips parted.

“You’re awake,” she said.

The words were not accusation. They were almost conversational, like she’d walked in to ask if I wanted water.

My throat closed.

She took a step into the room. The wood didn’t creak. We’d oiled the floorboards in here too, Mara’s insistence. Quiet. Always quiet.

She tilted her head. That precise tilt again, the one that made my skin crawl.

“You can hear me,” she said. “Don’t pretend.”

I stared at her, terrified of my own face. Terrified that my eyes would widen, that my mouth would soften, that my body would answer even if I didn’t.

“Mara,” I said.

The name slipped out before I could stop it, the way you say someone’s name when you’re trying to pull them back from the edge of something. It wasn’t a whisper. It wasn’t soft. It was sharp, urgent.

For a fraction of a second, Mara blinked rapidly. Human blinking. Confusion flickered in her features like a match flaring.

Then her face smoothed.

Her mouth curved, just slightly, in a smile that didn’t belong to relief or affection. It belonged to recognition.

“Yes,” she said, and the word was so satisfied it made me nauseated. “There.”

I tried to clamp down, but it was too late. The air in the room felt different, thicker, as if my voice had added weight to it.

Mara stepped closer. The light from the street caught her eyes; one pupil was a little larger than the other. She didn’t seem to notice.

My body wanted to back away. The couch pinned me. I could have stood, could have run, but running felt like giving it what it wanted too: reaction, confirmation, a dance partner.

Mara’s gaze stayed on my mouth like it was a magnet.

“Say it again,” she whispered.

I pressed my lips together. My tongue pressed hard against my teeth.

“Mara,” I said again, because some primitive part of me believed repetition could carve a path back to her. Because I was angry now, and fear makes anger feel like oxygen.

Her smile widened a fraction. “Good,” she said. “Good.”

Something inside me broke in a small way. Not courage. Restraint.

I stood up abruptly, the couch springs squealing. My movement was loud in the quiet house. Mara didn’t flinch. She watched my mouth.

“Stop,” I said, and the word came out too loud. “Stop it.”

Mara’s head tilted again. “Stop what.”

“You know what,” I said. My hands balled into fists at my sides, nails digging into my palms. “You keep—at night—you—”

She took one more step. We were close enough that I smelled her breath: mint, and beneath it that stale cabinet air. Her eyes flicked, not to my eyes—never to my eyes—but to the shape of my lips as they formed words.

“You say a lot when you’re afraid,” she whispered, as if commenting on the weather.

My stomach lurched. “Where’s Mara,” I demanded.

For a moment, something like annoyance passed across her face, quick and sharp. “Right here,” she said. The pitch was Mara’s. The impatience was Mara’s. It was a better imitation than it had been days ago.

I felt sick. It was learning.

My phone buzzed in my hand. I hadn’t realized I was holding it until then. A text from Mara—real Mara, presumably, from the bedroom.

*eli?*

My blood went cold. Two Maras. One in the hallway, one in my phone.

I looked down at the message, then back up at the figure in front of me, and my mouth went dry with the terrible clarity of it: if Mara was texting me from the bedroom, then the thing in the doorway was not her.

The thing watched my mouth open on a breath.

“Don’t,” it said, and there was a new edge to the word. “Don’t look away.”

I forced my eyes to stay on it. I forced my mouth closed. I typed with shaking thumbs.

*I’m in living room. stay in bedroom. lock door.*

A beat.

*what? why*

I didn’t answer. I couldn’t. The last thing I wanted to do was send more words into the dark.

The thing’s hand rose slowly, palm open, and hovered near my face. Not touching. Waiting for my flinch.

“You called me,” it whispered. “You did.”

“I called her,” I said, and the moment the words left my mouth I hated myself. More sound. More confirmation. More of what it wanted.

It smiled again. “Same mouth,” it said softly. “Same.”

A cold wave passed over me. The phrase wasn’t poetic. It was clinical. Like an assessment.

Behind it, in the hallway, something knocked. Not three taps. Just one. Like a signal.

The thing’s attention flicked toward the hall, then back to me. It seemed pleased with the response it got—its smile deepened.

“You did good,” it whispered. “Now you can sleep.”

The word sleep crawled over my skin. I thought of the note in the wall: KEEP. DO NOT ANSWER. SLEEP. I thought of the way it had told me, You can stop pretending.

My jaw clenched until it hurt.

From upstairs came a sound: the bedroom door handle rattling.

“Mara?” I called, but the word came out before I could stop it. Mara—real Mara—must have heard me, must have gotten scared. She was trying to come out.

The thing in front of me turned its head toward the stairs. The impatience returned, sharper.

“No,” it said. “Not yet.”

I moved without thinking. I lunged toward the stairs, intending to intercept Mara before she came down, before she saw this, before she spoke, before she—

The thing moved faster.

It stepped sideways into my path with a smoothness that wasn’t human sleepwalking. It didn’t grab me. It simply stood where I needed to be, and the way it stood—upright, still—made my body hesitate like hitting an invisible barrier.

“Eli!” Mara’s voice called from the top of the stairs, awake and frightened. “What’s going on?”

My chest tightened painfully.

The thing turned back to me. Its eyes were bright in the dim light. It stared at my mouth like it was waiting for the next offering.

“You can tell her,” it whispered. “Use your words.”

I could hear Mara’s bare feet on the landing now, approaching the stairs. I imagined her coming down and seeing her own body in the hall, her own face turned wrong. I imagined her screaming. I imagined her saying my name. I imagined the thing drinking it in.

I made an ugly choice.

I ran up the stairs two at a time, not looking at the thing as I passed, not giving it my face. I reached the landing and met Mara in the doorway.

She was pale, eyes wide, hair a mess. She had her phone in her hand like a weapon.

“What—” she started.

I clapped a hand over her mouth.

Her eyes went huge with shock and betrayal. She tried to pull away, making a muffled noise under my palm.

“Don’t,” I hissed in her ear. “Don’t talk. Just—just listen.”

Her breath came hot against my fingers. Tears sprang instantly, angry and scared. She shook her head hard, trying to jerk free.

I’d never covered her mouth before. The act felt obscene. It felt like becoming the thing I was afraid of.

Downstairs, in the hall, the other Mara laughed softly. A sound like amusement. Like approval.

Mara bit my palm.

Pain flared. I yanked my hand back with a curse. Mara gasped and immediately said my name—“Eli”—as if the syllables were the only thing keeping her upright.

The laughter downstairs stopped.

The house went very quiet.

Mara stared past me, down the stairs, eyes narrowing as she tried to see.

“What is that,” she whispered.

I opened my mouth to tell her to stay back.

And in the hollow between my words, from downstairs, came a soft, patient whisper—Mara’s voice wearing something else:

“Say it again.”
step_outputs.13:
Mara’s hand clamped around my wrist, hard enough to hurt.

“Move,” she whispered, not looking at me. Her eyes were fixed down the stairs. Her mouth tightened the way it did when she was about to walk into an argument at work and win it. “Get out of the doorway.”

“I’m not letting you go down there,” I whispered back, and my voice sounded small next to hers.

Mara’s nostrils flared. “That’s me,” she said, and the word me was a blade. “That is my body.”

Downstairs, in the hall, the other Mara—my Mara’s face—tilted toward the stairs as if it could hear our breathing. It didn’t move. It didn’t need to. Its stillness was a kind of confidence.

The lamp in the bedroom threw light onto the landing. Beyond it the stairwell descended into a dim gray strip of hallway and deeper shadow. The house felt huge and close at once.

Mara stepped forward. I grabbed her arm. She jerked away.

“Don’t touch me,” she hissed, and then softer, furious and frightened, “Don’t you ever do that again.”

My palm still throbbed where she’d bitten me. The bite wasn’t deep. The shame was.

“I’m sorry,” I said.

Mara didn’t look at me. “Don’t,” she said. “Not right now.”

From below, the other Mara spoke. The voice carried up the stairs perfectly, as if the house were built for it.

“Eli,” it called gently, with false warmth. “You can stop pretending.”

Mara flinched at the sound of her own voice coming from the wrong place. Her hand flew to the stair rail. She gripped it, knuckles whitening.

“That’s not me,” she whispered, and hearing her say it out loud—finally, without anger, without denial—did something to me. It made my stomach drop. It made the situation real in a way it hadn’t been when it was just me in the dark.

The other Mara laughed softly. “Yes,” it said. “It is. Same mouth.”

Mara’s eyes snapped to my face, to my mouth, as if she couldn’t help checking whether I would answer. Then she caught herself and looked away, swallowing hard.

“Don’t talk to it,” she whispered to me. “Don’t. That note—”

“Do not answer,” I breathed.

The other Mara’s head tilted, listening. “You read,” it said, pleased. “Good. You learn.”

Mara’s grip tightened on the rail until the wood creaked. Her voice went sharp, protective, the way it got when someone cut in line ahead of a kid. “Leave him alone.”

The other Mara smiled in the darkness. “You can tell me,” it said, and the tone was coaxing, intimate. “You’re awake now. You can help. Say it. Say you see me.”

Mara’s breath hitched. Her gaze snapped down, as if the words had snagged her by the throat. She opened her mouth.

I moved fast. I stepped in front of her so she had to look at my back instead of the stairs. I shook my head once, hard. No.

Mara’s mouth closed. Her jaw worked. Rage flashed through her features—at me, at it, at herself.

“Okay,” she said, almost soundless. “Okay. So what do we do.”

I didn’t have an answer that didn’t feel like a gamble. The note in the wall said SLEEP. My experience said sleeping was exactly what gave it room. Pretending was what it liked most: a body present, a mind withheld, a mouth on the verge.

I thought of the packet sealed behind plaster. Keep.

I realized, with a cold certainty, that we’d put the lock back on a door without checking what side we were on.

“It’s in the hall,” I whispered. “It’s between us and—everything. It’s not just you.”

Mara’s eyes narrowed. “You think it’s the house.”

“I think the house is… where it waits,” I said. “And you—” My voice cracked. “You’re how it talks.”

The other Mara spoke again, interrupting as if it had heard our conclusion and wanted to correct it. “I’m here,” it said. “You keep me. You read my note. You breathe my air. Say it.”

Mara’s fingers dug into the rail. “Stop,” she whispered, and the word trembled. “Stop. Please.”

The other Mara’s smile softened into something like pity. “Look at me,” it said. “Then you can sleep.”

Mara’s eyes flicked toward the stairs despite herself.

I made another ugly choice. I reached behind me and turned the bedroom lamp off.

Darkness swallowed the landing. Mara made a small sound of protest, instinctive.

“It wants light,” I whispered into the black. “It wants your eyes on it. It wants your mouth.”

From below came a pause. Then, faintly amused: “You think darkness helps.”

In the black, Mara’s hand found my arm. Not tender. Desperate. “Eli,” she whispered, “I can’t—”

“I know,” I whispered back. “Stay here.”

I moved by memory, along the wall, toward the hall where we’d patched the plaster. My bare feet found the edge of the runner rug, the dip where the board was loose. The house smelled like warm wood and dried plaster and our sweat.

Behind me, Mara whispered my name again, quieter this time, as if saying it could keep me anchored. “Eli.”

The sound made my stomach twist. Names were dangerous now.

I didn’t answer. I just squeezed her hand once, then let go.

I went down the stairs.

Halfway down, the air cooled. Not just the temperature—something in the air texture changed, like stepping into a room with different pressure. My skin prickled.

At the bottom of the stairs, in the hallway, the other Mara stood with her head angled up toward me. In the dim light from the kitchen window, I could see her eyes shining.

She— it—smiled. “There you are.”

I kept my mouth shut. I forced my breathing through my nose. In and out, slow.

It took a step toward the stairs. I didn’t move back. I couldn’t. The hall behind me held the patched wall like a scar.

The other Mara’s gaze dropped instantly to my lips, as if drawn.

“Say it,” it whispered.

I clenched my jaw until it hurt.

It moved closer, slow, confident. It lifted its hand. Two fingers hovered near my mouth, the familiar ritual.

I swallowed. The swallow was loud in the silence.

The fingers paused, then lowered—almost tender. “I know you,” it murmured. “You can’t stop talking forever.”

My blood sang in my ears. I wanted to scream. Screaming was words too.

Behind me, upstairs, a floorboard creaked. Mara shifting her weight. Listening. Holding herself still.

The other Mara turned her head slightly, as if sensing Mara’s presence above. “She’s awake,” it said, pleased. “Good. You brought her.”

My stomach dropped. It wanted both of us here. It wanted witnesses.

I backed one step, just one, placing myself closer to the patched wall. My shoulder brushed plaster. Dry, smooth.

Keep.

My hands found the edge of the patch by feel. The spackle was sanded but not painted. A faint ridge remained if you knew where to press. I dug my fingers under it.

The other Mara watched my mouth, not my hands. That gave me a second.

I pulled.

The patch flexed. A crack whispered through the plaster. Fine dust puffed out, gritty in my nose. The smell of wet spackle—faint, chalky—rose.

The other Mara’s eyes flicked, briefly, to the wall. Annoyance sharpened her face. “No,” it said, the first hard word I’d heard from it. “Keep it.”

I tore the patch open.

The cavity behind was black and cold. Air breathed out like a sigh from a throat.

I reached in.

My fingers hit cloth.

The packet was tucked deeper than before, as if pushed back. I yanked it out. The twine bit my skin. The packet was colder than it should have been, as if it had been sitting in a freezer.

The other Mara moved fast—too fast. She grabbed my wrist. Her grip was iron.

Her face was inches from mine. Her eyes were bright, hungry, and still aimed at my mouth even as she held my hand.

“Put it back,” it whispered, and now the whisper wasn’t coaxing. It was furious. “Keep.”

I shook my head once. No.

It tightened its grip until pain flared up my arm. My hand spasmed. The packet slipped in my fingers.

The other Mara’s thumb rose toward the corner of my mouth, familiar and intimate. “Say you see me,” it whispered. “Say it. Then she stops hurting.”

Upstairs, Mara made a sound—a sob, or a choked gasp—as if my pain traveled up the stairs through the rail. “Eli,” she called, voice breaking. “Eli!”

Hearing her voice above me, real and terrified, did something simple and violent to my restraint: it made me want to answer her. To reassure her. To say I’m here.

The other Mara’s eyes widened slightly, as if she felt the shift. Her thumb pressed at the corner of my mouth.

“Say,” it whispered.

I couldn’t afford to speak. Not her name, not my own, not anything. Speech was the door.

So I did the only thing left that was not words.

I opened my mouth and bit down hard on the inside of my cheek.

Pain flared, hot and immediate. Blood filled my mouth. The copper taste shocked me fully awake. I clamped my jaw shut, not on air, not on a syllable—on flesh. It kept my mouth busy. It kept it closed.

The other Mara blinked, startled by the sudden blood. Her grip loosened for half a heartbeat.

I used that heartbeat.

I shoved the packet back into the wall cavity, deep, as far as my arm would go, forcing it into the darkest corner like stuffing something back into a wound. I pushed until my fingers scraped wood.

Then I slammed the broken patch back into place with my palm, smearing spackle dust. A stupid gesture. A human one. As if physical force could reseal a breach.

The other Mara hissed—an ugly sound, not a word. Her face contorted with something like disgust.

The hallway air shifted. The pressure eased slightly, like a hand lifting off a mouth.

The other Mara’s posture sagged for a fraction of a second. Her eyes—still too bright—flicked upward toward the stairs, toward Mara’s breathing above.

“You can’t keep forever,” it whispered, and the tone had changed again—less angry, more amused. “You sleep. You blink. You answer.”

Then Mara—real Mara—stumbled down the stairs, half-running, face wet, hands clutching the banister.

When she reached the bottom, she stopped short as if she’d hit an invisible wall. She stared at herself standing in the hall, at her own body angled wrong, at her own mouth.

The other Mara turned slowly to look at her.

Mara made a small, broken sound. “Oh my—”

I lunged between them, not touching Mara’s mouth this time, just grabbing her shoulders hard enough to bruise. I pulled her backward, toward the stairs.

“Don’t,” I rasped, blood thick in my mouth. The word came out garbled, half-swallowed. I hated that I’d made a sound. But it wasn’t her name. It wasn’t recognition. It was a warning.

Mara’s eyes snapped to mine. In the dim light she saw the blood at the corner of my mouth, the spackle dust on my hands, the raw fear.

She swallowed the words she’d been about to say. Her lips pressed tight. Her eyes filled again.

The other Mara watched us with a small, satisfied smile, as if even our silence was data.

Mara backed up the stairs with me, one step at a time, never taking her eyes off her own face in the hall. I could feel her shaking through my grip.

Upstairs, in the bedroom, we shut the door and turned the lock. The click sounded obscene, like locking someone out of their own house.

We sat on the floor with our backs against the bed, breathing hard. My mouth tasted of blood and plaster.

Mara’s hands fluttered over my face without touching, like she wanted to wipe the blood away but was afraid to put her fingers near my mouth. “Eli,” she whispered, and then stopped, as if even saying my name was dangerous now.

“It’s okay,” I said, thickly, and the lie hurt.

Mara shook her head violently. “No,” she mouthed. She didn’t voice it.

We stayed like that until dawn, not sleeping, not speaking, listening to the house settle around us like a body going back to rest.

When morning came, the light didn’t make it better. It only made everything look exhausted.

We went into the hall together. The patch in the wall was cracked, spackle smeared. Fine white dust coated the baseboard. It looked like we’d made a mess. It looked like we were the problem.

Mara stared at the patch. Her voice, when she finally used it, was hoarse. “So we keep it.”

I nodded. My mouth ached from the bite. I didn’t trust it.

We didn’t talk about moving. We didn’t talk about calling anyone. We didn’t talk about the version of Mara that had stood in our hall and demanded words like a debt.
