In Rowan’s room, Mara grabbed the backpack and zipped it without looking. “Inhaler,” she whispered to Rowan.

Rowan pointed with two fingers, quick. “Bathroom drawer. Mom said—”

Mara left him there and slid into the bathroom. Her eyes went to the drawer Dana had pointed out earlier. She yanked it open. A jumbled mess: hair ties, band-aids, little hotel soaps. Then the inhaler, blue plastic, and a spacer with cartoon stickers.

Good.

Downstairs: another knock.

Not angry yet. That was what made Mara’s stomach tighten. Anger could be sloppy. This felt planned.

She herded the kids into the hallway closet opposite the bathroom. It was a linen closet, tall shelves, towels stacked in crisp, folded columns. Dana’s version of chaos control.

Rowan stepped in first, then Lily. Mara pushed extra towels forward, making a soft cave behind them. She gave Rowan the inhaler and spacer like handing him a fragile weapon.

“Okay,” Mara whispered. “New rule. If I say ‘blue,’ you run. Down the stairs, out the back. Do you know the back door?”

Rowan nodded, eyes too bright.

“Good. You hold Lily’s hand. You don’t let go.”

Lily’s mouth trembled. “Blue is my inhaler.”

Mara almost smiled. Almost. “Exactly. So you’ll remember.”

She closed the closet door until it was barely latched, enough to look like it belonged shut. Then she stood in the hallway and listened again, forcing herself to breathe slow. She pictured the oxygen in her own lungs like a measured supply. Panic was a fire; it ate air.

At the top of the stairs she could see the foyer. The front windows turned the rain outside into moving glass. The front door was a dark rectangle waiting.

Knock.

Knock.

This time, the door handle jiggled.

Mara didn’t move.

The jiggle stopped. A pause. Then came the sound of something thin sliding against metal—credit card or lock pick, probing.

Smart locks didn’t care about cards, but the chain and latch did. Mara watched the handle, every muscle drawn tight.

A new sound: a faint, high whine, like a tool spinning up.

Mara’s brain offered her a list. Drill. Saw. Something meant for a job done quickly.

She backed up one step, then another, so she wasn’t directly in the line of the door if it came open. Her heel brushed the edge of the console table. The family photo rattled softly.

She caught it before it could fall, fingers closing on the frame. The glass was cold.

The tool noise rose. Metal complained. A smell—hot plastic, hot metal—reached her even from here.

Then the porch light flickered again, and the whole house dipped into darkness for a half second and came back. Not enough to reset anything, but enough to make her skin prickle.

Downstairs, the smart lock keypad’s little blue light died and stayed dead.

Mara stared at it.

Power out. At least partially.

The tool noise stopped.

A single, quiet click.

The front door swung inward.

Three men stepped into the Hale house like they had been invited.

They wore dark hoodies and cheap black gloves. Not ski masks—just caps pulled low and cloth gaiters over their mouths, like they were going to a protest or a cold football game. Their eyes were visible, and that made them worse.

The first one—tall, lean—paused just inside and scanned the foyer with a slow, practiced motion. His gaze flicked to the living room, the stairs, the kitchen entry. He didn’t look surprised by the darkness. He expected it. He owned it.

The second was slighter, twitchy. He glanced back at the door like he was already measuring escape routes.

The third moved like he wanted to touch everything. His attention went to the family photo, to the wreath, to the neat shoes lined up by the wall.

Mara stayed still on the stairs. From this angle, she was in shadow. The men hadn’t looked up yet. People often didn’t look up until the thing above them fell.

“Door’s easy,” the twitchy one muttered. His voice was young, strained. “Told you.”

“Shut up,” the lean one said, calm. He spoke as if he had all the air in the house and could afford to waste some.

The third man picked up one of the shoes by the wall—a tiny sneaker, Lily’s—and squeezed it. He made a low noise through his gaiter, like amusement.

“Hey,” Mara said, voice low and level, and watched all three heads snap up.

The twitchy one startled hard, stepping back like she’d struck him. The third—shoe in hand—grinned behind his covering, eyes narrowing. The lean one just looked at her, eyes steady.

“There’s a woman,” the third said, delighted, like he’d found an unexpected toy.

Mara didn’t descend the stairs. She didn’t offer more of herself. “You’re in the wrong house.”

Lean man’s gaze traveled over her. Hoodie. Jeans. Damp hair. No uniform. No visible weapon. His eyes assessed and dismissed in the same breath.

“No,” he said. “We’re in the right one.”

Mara kept her hands visible. Not surrender, just control. “Whatever you want, take it and go. Kids are asleep. You don’t need to make noise.”

Twitchy one laughed, too high. “Kids are—”

“Denny,” lean man said, and the twitchy one shut up.

The third man leaned on the banister like he lived here. “She talking to us like she’s the homeowner.”

Mara felt the line he was baiting and refused to step over it. “I’m the adult here tonight.”

The third man’s eyes lit up. “She said adult.”

Lean man didn’t react. He pointed toward the hallway that led to Mark’s office. “Where’s the office?”

Mara held his gaze. “Locked.”

“Everything’s locked,” the third man said. He set Lily’s sneaker back down with exaggerated care. “That’s why we’re here.”

The lean man took two steps forward, and the air changed. He didn’t hurry. He didn’t need to.

Mara stepped down one stair, then another, matching his movement without giving up her position too fast. “Listen,” she said. “You don’t want this to get complicated.”

The third man chuckled. “It’s just the sitter,” he said casually, like he was ordering food. “She gonna call the cops.”

Mara’s phone was in her hoodie pocket. She could feel its weight like a stone. She’d already tried it once at the top of the stairs—no bars. She hadn’t told herself that yet, but her body knew.

Lean man’s eyes flicked to her pocket. “No need.”

Mara’s pulse lifted. “What are you here for?”

Lean man’s gaze moved past her, up the stairs toward the dark hallway. “Not you.”

That was supposed to soothe her. It didn’t. It made her skin crawl.

Mara forced her voice to stay even. “If you’re not here for me, then leave me alone.”

The third man—Cal, Mara decided, because he had the face of a Cal—tilted his head. “You got a name?”

Mara didn’t answer.

Cal took a step toward the stairs, eyes bright. “I’m gonna go say hi to the kids.”

“No,” Mara said, and the word came out sharper than she intended.

Lean man lifted his hand, a small stop gesture. Not for Mara. For Cal. “Stay on task.”

Cal’s grin faltered. His eyes went to lean man with resentment, then back to Mara. “Task. Right. Task is take what we came for. But sometimes you get extras.”

Mara’s stomach went cold. She backed down another stair, putting herself between them and the hallway. “There’s nothing in this house worth that.”

Lean man watched her, then nodded once, as if confirming a suspicion. “There is,” he said.

He reached into his pocket and pulled out something small and black—a device. He pressed a button.

A soft pop sounded somewhere in the house. Like a fuse blowing.

The baby monitor’s green light in the kitchen winked off.

Mara’s mouth went dry. He wasn’t just cutting power. He was cutting senses.

Denny, the twitchy one, glanced toward the kitchen. “That work?”

“It will,” lean man said. His voice didn’t change.

Mara’s mind moved fast, flipping through options like drawers.

Back door: side gate open. Rain. Dark. Kids in closet upstairs.

Hide until they leave: they knew about the office. They would search. They would find.

Call for help: no service. Landline? Did they even have one? If they did, these men would have cut it.

Mara’s fingers flexed. She kept her face still.

Cal started up the stairs, slow, savoring it. Lean man didn’t stop him this time. Maybe the plan allowed it. Maybe he didn’t care. Maybe he wanted Mara to run.

Mara moved.

She didn’t launch herself at Cal on the stairs. That was what people expected from heroes in movies. Mara stepped backward into the kitchen, keeping her eyes on them, and grabbed the first thing her hand landed on: a heavy ceramic utensil crock full of metal spoons, ladles, and one long carving knife.

She lifted it with both hands.

Cal laughed. “Oh, she’s got a pot.”

Mara swung the crock down hard onto the edge of the island.

Ceramic shattered. Metal clattered across the countertop and floor like sudden rain.

Cal’s laugh stopped.

Mara snatched the long knife—not for stabbing, not yet, but because holding something changed how close people wanted to get. She backed toward the pantry door, eyes never leaving the men.

Lean man’s gaze flicked to the knife, then to the stairs, then back. “Put it down,” he said, like he was giving her a chance to be reasonable.

Mara’s voice was quiet. “No.”

Denny’s eyes were wide. “Man, come on. We don’t—”

Cal turned and looked at Denny as if seeing him for the first time. “Shut up.”

Mara’s grip tightened until her knuckles ached. She wanted to be calm. She wanted to be the woman who read bedtime stories and counted breaths. But the house had shifted. It wasn’t a home tonight. It was a place where predators came to do work.

Lean man stepped into the kitchen. “We came for something,” he said. “You can make this easy. Tell me the office.”

Mara shook her head once.

Cal, on the stairs, called down softly, “I can smell them.”

Mara’s stomach lurched. The closet. The towels. Lily’s shampoo. Their warmth in a narrow space.

She swallowed. “If you go upstairs,” she said, and her voice changed despite her effort, hardening, “you’ll have to go through me.”

Cal’s eyes shone. “Just the sitter,” he repeated, like a prayer.

The lean man stared at Mara for a long beat. Then he nodded toward the hallway. “Fine,” he said. “We’ll do it ourselves.”

He moved toward the office hall, slow, careful.

Cal stayed on the stairs, watching Mara like she was entertainment.

Denny hovered by the door, shifting his weight, breathing fast through his covering. He kept looking from Mara to the stairs to the rain outside as if waiting for the world to intervene.

It didn’t.

Mara took one step toward the hallway, then stopped. If she chased lean man, Cal would go up. If she went up, lean man would reach the office. If she froze, they’d pick the house apart like a carcass.

She made a decision that tasted like metal.

She turned her head slightly, eyes still on Cal, and raised her voice just enough for the closet to hear.

“Blue,” she said.

It was a soft word. Almost nothing.

Upstairs, a floorboard creaked. Then another.

Cal’s head snapped toward the sound. His grin widened.

Mara moved before he could. She hurled the knife—not at his body, but at the stair rail beside his hand. The blade struck wood with a thunk and stuck, vibrating.

Cal jerked his hand back, more surprised than hurt.

Mara was already running up the stairs.

Not to fight him.

To get to the kids before he did.
step_outputs.8:
## Chapter 3 — The Thin Parts of Walls

Mara hit the top landing and nearly collided with Rowan.

Rowan had Lily’s hand in a death grip. Lily’s cheeks were wet, but she wasn’t making a sound; her mouth was pressed into Pickles’ fur like she could swallow her fear.

“Good,” Mara whispered, not praise exactly—an assessment. “Back. Bathroom. Now.”

Rowan started to protest, then saw Mara’s face and didn’t. They slid into the bathroom, and Mara shut the door most of the way, leaving it open a crack to listen. She could hear the men below—shoes on wood, a low voice, the soft laugh that didn’t belong.

Cal’s laugh.

Mara’s mind tried to build a map of the house with moving pieces. Lean man—Rook—heading toward the office hall. Denny near the door. Cal coming up, because of course he was. Cal wanted to take the stairs like a stage.

Mara grabbed the baby monitor unit off the bathroom counter. It was a parent’s comforting lie until it wasn’t. She thumbed it on. Static, then a hiss, then nothing—dead. Whatever device Rook had used downstairs had killed it.

“Okay,” she whispered, more to herself than to the kids. “Old school.”

She pushed Rowan and Lily into the shower stall, behind the curtain. She yanked the curtain closed and pointed at the floor. “Sit. Both of you. Low.”

Rowan obeyed, jaw tight. Lily’s eyes were huge.

Mara leaned close. “No talking. If you have to cough, you cough into Pickles. If you have to cry, you cry into Pickles. Pickles is brave.”

Lily nodded, pressing the rabbit’s head over her mouth like a mask.

Mara backed out of the bathroom and pulled the door mostly shut again. She took three silent steps down the hall toward the linen closet and the attic access panel above it. At the corner, she paused and listened.

Footsteps on the stairs. Slow, deliberate. A hand sliding along the banister like it was a pet.

Cal’s voice floated up. “Maraaa,” he called, sing-song and intimate. “Where’d you go?”

Mara’s skin crawled. He had asked her name and she hadn’t answered. He shouldn’t have it. But people like Cal didn’t need information; they used whatever stuck.

She reached up and eased open the attic panel. Dust fell in a soft puff. She pulled down the ladder a few inches, just enough to creak once—then stopped, cursing herself silently.

The footsteps paused.

Mara froze, hand on the ladder, breath held. Silence expanded, full and thick.

Then Cal laughed again, quieter. “I hear you,” he said. “House talks.”

Mara lowered the ladder all the way anyway. Sometimes you couldn’t undo a sound; you just had to make it part of a different story.

She climbed quickly into the attic, not caring about grace. The space above was unfinished—pink insulation, dark beams, the smell of old wood and mouse droppings. She moved on her knees, palms pressing into gritty dust. Her cuticles stung.

She found the thin strip of plywood that served as a walkway over the joists and crawled toward the center of the house, toward where the attic would be above Mark’s office.

Below, Cal’s footsteps reached the top landing. A pause. A door clicked open—Rowan’s room, maybe. Drawers opened. Something tossed. Cal was making a point of rummaging. Of owning.

Mara inched forward until she found a vent opening—a gap around a duct where she could see down into the hallway through a sliver between ceiling and wall. The house had thin parts. You could watch through them if you knew where to look.

Cal stood in the hallway beneath her, head tilted, listening. His hood was up, but she could see the shape of his face, the gleam of his eyes when he glanced toward the bathroom door.

He stepped closer to it, slow. Mara’s muscles coiled.

Downstairs, another voice—Rook—called up once, a short command. “Cal. Don’t.”

Cal didn’t answer. He reached for the bathroom doorknob with exaggerated gentleness.

Mara had no weapon now. The knife was downstairs, stuck in the banister. She scanned the attic around her, frantic and focused. There: an old metal hanger, twisted, abandoned. A length of thin rope used for holiday lights. A box with “XMAS” written in Sharpie, lid half open.

Her hand closed on the hanger. She straightened it as much as she could, metal biting into her palms. Not lethal. Not impressive. But metal was metal, and surprise was leverage.

Below, Cal turned the knob.

Mara couldn’t drop down on him from the attic like an action hero. She could do something else. She could make noise somewhere else. Make him turn away from the bathroom.

She found the attic access over Rowan’s room—another panel farther down—and shoved it open hard.

The panel fell with a clap, and the folded ladder inside thunked against its frame.

Cal jerked back from the bathroom door, head snapping toward the sound. “What the—”

Mara shoved the ladder down another inch to make it creak louder, then froze.

Cal moved toward Rowan’s room, drawn to the disturbance.

Mara crawled back along the plywood, heart hammering. She had bought a few seconds. Seconds were currency.

She dropped down the hallway ladder quietly—barely. The rung squeaked once. She winced, but Cal was farther away now, moving toward Rowan’s room.

Mara landed on the carpet in the linen closet area and closed the attic panel softly, palms shaking.

She padded to the bathroom door and slipped inside.

Rowan and Lily sat in the shower like she’d left them, knees tucked, Pickles between them. Lily’s chest rose too fast.

Mara crouched and put two fingers to Lily’s wrist. Tiny pulse, fast and fluttery. “Breathe with me,” she whispered. “In through your nose. Out through your mouth.”

Lily tried, but her inhale hit a hitch. A faint whistle.

Rowan’s eyes darted to the inhaler in his hand. “She’s—”

“I know,” Mara whispered. She took the inhaler and spacer and fitted them quickly. Hands steady, brain shouting. “Lily. Lips on. Good. One puff.”

She pressed. Lily sucked in, eyes squeezed shut, a tiny panic tremor running through her.

“Good,” Mara said. “Again, slow.”

Footsteps in the hall—Cal, returning. The boards creaked in a rhythm that made Mara want to crawl out of her own skin.

Mara glanced at Rowan. “Hold this,” she whispered, handing him the spacer. “Count for her. One, two, three…”

Rowan’s lips moved silently as he counted, eyes fixed on Lily like she was the only real thing left.

Mara stood and listened at the bathroom door crack.

Cal’s voice drifted through, lower now. “They moved,” he murmured. “She moved them. That’s cute.”

Rook’s voice from downstairs, impatient. “Stop playing. Find the office. You’re wasting time.”

Cal made a soft clicking sound with his tongue. “Time’s the only thing we got.”

Mara’s fingers closed on the bathroom towel rack. It was bolted into the wall. Metal bar, sturdy. Not a weapon, but something to hold onto.

She thought: back door. Rain. Side gate open. If they ran, where would they go? Neighbor houses lined up like sleeping animals. Lights off. Locked doors. People who might not answer.

She heard a new sound downstairs—metal on metal, heavier. Rook working on something. The office door.

Mara’s stomach sank. Rook wasn’t upstairs. He was doing the job.

Cal, though, was here. Guard dog and predator.

Mara opened the bathroom door a crack wider and looked out.

Cal stood at the top of the stairs, one hand on the banister where the knife still trembled in wood. He looked up the hall, sniffing like a dog.

He turned his head sharply and locked eyes with Mara through the crack.

For a split second, neither of them moved. Then Cal’s eyes crinkled with pleasure.

“There you are,” he said softly.

Mara’s mouth went dry. She stepped out into the hallway, leaving the bathroom door mostly shut behind her. She put herself between Cal and the kids, body angled, hands visible.

Cal took a step toward her. “You’re brave,” he said, voice almost admiring. “Or stupid. Sometimes it’s the same thing.”

“Go downstairs,” Mara said. Her voice sounded too calm. She hated that it sounded like a command, because commands invited defiance.

Cal’s gaze flicked to the bathroom door. “Kids in there?”

“No,” Mara lied.

Cal smiled. “You’re a terrible liar.”

Behind Mara, a tiny cough—Lily, failing at being quiet.

Cal’s head snapped toward the sound. His grin widened. “Oh.”

Mara moved without thinking. She swung the towel rack like a baton, ripping it off the wall. Screws tore out with a crack. The sudden noise echoed down the hallway like a gunshot in a church.

Cal flinched, more surprised than hurt, and Mara used the moment to shove him—hard—back toward the stairwell.

Cal stumbled one step, then caught himself, laughing, exhilarated. “That’s it,” he breathed. “That’s the good part.”

Mara’s hand throbbed where the metal had bitten into her palm. She backed toward the bathroom door, keeping her body between Cal and it.

Cal advanced again, slow. “You can’t win,” he said, as if explaining a rule.

Mara didn’t answer because she didn’t have a speech. She had two kids behind her and a house that was turning into a trap.

Downstairs, Rook’s voice rose, sharp. “Denny! Phone. Now.”

Denny’s voice answered, panicked. “It’s dead, man—there’s no—”

Rook cursed.

Cal paused, distracted for half a second by the change in tone. Mara took it. She slammed the bathroom door shut and threw the little lock on it.

Rowan looked up at her, eyes wide. Lily’s breathing was still tight, but the inhaler was starting to do its work.

“We’re not staying here,” Mara whispered. Her voice finally cracked at the edges. “We’re moving. Quiet feet.”

Rowan swallowed. “Where?”

Mara’s eyes flicked to the attic access again. Then to the window at the end of the hall—the one that looked out over the roof of the porch.

“Anywhere that isn’t predictable,” she whispered.

From the other side of the bathroom door, Cal’s knuckles rapped gently.

Tap. Tap. Tap.

Like he was being polite.

“Open up,” he said, almost kindly. “We can do this easy.”

Mara stared at the door, then at the children. Her brain, exhausted and furious, offered her a single clear thought:

*He thinks you’re just the sitter.*

She grabbed the inhaler, shoved it into Rowan’s hands, and lifted the bathroom window lock.

Outside, the rain waited.
step_outputs.9:
## Chapter 4 — The Office Door

The bathroom window stuck the way cheap promises did.

Mara dug her fingers under the sash and shoved. The wood resisted, swollen with rain, then gave with a wet squeal that felt impossibly loud. Cold air punched in, carrying the metallic smell of storm and the dark, green stink of wet leaves.

Rowan climbed onto the toilet seat without being told. Lily followed, knees trembling. Mara went last, one hand on the window frame, one on Lily’s waistband like a leash.

Behind them, the bathroom door shuddered once. Cal didn’t kick it yet. He didn’t need to. He was enjoying the thought of time.

“Mouse quiet,” Mara whispered.

Lily nodded and pressed Pickles to her face. Rowan’s jaw worked.

They edged out onto the small, slick roof over the porch. Shingles glistened. Water streamed toward the gutters in narrow rivers. The drop to the ground was only eight feet, but it looked like a different world down there—open, exposed, lit by the neighbor’s motion light that kept flaring on and off in the rain.

Mara crouched, keeping her center low. “Stay on your hands and knees,” she whispered. “Crawl. Don’t stand.”

Rowan did as told, but Lily’s palms slid on the wet shingles. She made a small, terrified sound.

Mara caught her elbow. “Look at me,” she breathed. “You’re doing it.”

Lily’s eyes found Mara’s. For a moment, the panic receded—replaced by the fierce, stubborn focus of a child trying to win a game.

They crawled along the roofline toward the side where the gutters met the tree. If they could get to the thick branch that hung close, they could drop from there into the shrubbery and run.

Mara’s brain kept counting: breaths, steps, angles. She could hear muffled voices through the house—Rook downstairs, Cal at the bathroom door.

Then Cal’s voice rose suddenly, delighted. “She went out the window!”

A crash as the bathroom door gave way—wood splintering, a triumphant laugh.

Mara’s blood ran cold. “Faster,” she whispered, and immediately hated the word. Faster meant slipping. Faster meant Lily falling.

Rowan scooted ahead, surprisingly steady. He reached the tree branch first and looked back, rain plastered to his hair.

“I can—” he started.

“No,” Mara whispered. “You go first.”

Rowan hesitated, and in that hesitation Mara saw what he was really doing: waiting for permission to leave the house. Waiting for an adult to say it was okay to run.

Mara met his eyes. “Go,” she said, firm. “Now.”

Rowan swung one leg over the branch, then the other, and lowered himself down. His sneaker slipped, and his breath hitched, but he caught himself and dropped into the bushes with a soft whoof.

Lily crawled to the branch and froze, staring at the drop like it was an ocean. Her small fingers clenched around Pickles.

“I can’t,” she whispered, voice thick with tears.

“Yes you can,” Mara whispered back, close enough for Lily to feel her breath. “I’m right here. Put Pickles on your back. Use both hands.”

Lily obeyed, fumbling. Mara guided her fingers around the branch, feeling how cold and slick it was.

From inside, a window slid open hard. Cal’s silhouette appeared in the bathroom window, black against the interior light. He leaned out, rain hitting his hood, and grinned like this was a carnival ride.

“Hi!” he called, too loud, waving. “Don’t fall!”

Mara’s body flooded with something hot and savage. She wanted to throw herself at him just to make him stop talking.

Instead she stayed with Lily. “Look at me,” she whispered. “Not him. Look at me.”

Lily’s eyes, watery and wide, found Mara again. Mara guided her down, inch by inch. Lily’s feet searched for purchase, found air.

Rowan’s voice rose from below, cracked. “Lily! I got you!”

Lily let go and dropped. Rowan caught her awkwardly, both of them falling into the bushes. A small yelp—then silence.

Mara swung her leg over the branch and started down.

A hand shot out from the bathroom window—Cal’s. He grabbed a fistful of Mara’s hoodie and yanked.

The fabric tightened around her throat. Mara’s breath cut off, a harsh choke. Her hands scrabbled for purchase on the branch.

Cal laughed, close now, rain in his teeth. “Gotcha.”

Mara’s fingers found the hair tie on her wrist. She ripped it off and looped it around her fingers for grip, then twisted her torso, using her body weight like a lever. She couldn’t overpower him. She could make it inconvenient to hold on.

She drove her elbow backward, hard, into his wrist. Bone. Tendon. The kind of pain she knew intimately.

Cal hissed and loosened his grip just enough.

Mara tore free, losing balance. For a split second she was weightless, rain cold on her face, the world tilted wrong.

Then she dropped.

She hit the bushes, shoulder-first, pain exploding bright. A sharp sting in her palm where something thorny tore skin. She rolled onto wet grass, gasping like she’d been underwater.

Rowan was crouched over Lily, arms around her. Lily was crying silently, shaking. Pickles lay facedown in the mud.

Mara pushed herself up, shoulder screaming. “Move,” she whispered. “Now.”

They ran along the side of the house, staying low, cutting through wet hedges. The side gate stood open, swinging slightly in the wind like a beckoning hand.

Mara’s mind flashed back to the latch hanging open earlier. Not a mistake. An invitation.

They slipped through and into the backyard.

Motion lights snapped on, bleaching the rain-slick yard in harsh white. Mara froze for a fraction of a second, feeling exposed. Then she forced herself forward. Light didn’t mean seen. It meant time.

Rowan tugged Lily along. Lily wheezed once—high, tight.

Mara’s stomach clenched. “Inhaler,” she whispered without looking.

Rowan shoved it into Mara’s hand. Mara shoved it back. “You do it,” she whispered. “You know how.”

Rowan’s eyes widened. “I—”

“You do,” Mara said. “Now.”

Rowan fit the spacer to the inhaler with shaking hands. Lily took it, breathing hard, and Rowan counted under his breath like he’d been born to it. One, two, three.

Behind them, the back door of the house burst open.

Cal stepped into the yard, rain plastering his clothes. He lifted his arms wide like he was greeting an audience. “There they are.”

Mara’s heart thudded. She scanned the fence line. Neighbor’s yard. Another fence. A shed. A slippery trampoline. Nothing that looked like safety.

Cal took one step, then another. He wasn’t rushing. He wanted them to run themselves into exhaustion.

Mara’s gaze flicked to the house, to the dark rectangle of Mark’s office window on the first floor. Somewhere inside, Rook was still doing the job. The office. The safe.

If they ran far enough, the men would get what they came for and vanish. They’d leave the kids alive, maybe—if that was true. If Cal didn’t get bored.

Mara tasted metal in her mouth. She’d seen what “maybe” did to people. Maybe was a coin flip with children’s lives on it.

She took Rowan’s shoulder. “Stay here,” she whispered. “Behind the shed. Keep her breathing. If I say blue, you go.”

Rowan’s face twisted. “Where are you going?”

Mara didn’t have time to make it gentle. “Back in.”

Rowan grabbed her sleeve, desperate. “No—Mara, don’t—”

Mara leaned close, voice fierce and quiet. “Listen to me. I’m not leaving you. I’m stopping them.”

Rowan’s grip loosened. He looked like he wanted to argue, but something in Mara’s tone—something that wasn’t negotiation—made him let go.

Mara turned and sprinted toward the house, low and fast.

Cal saw her pivot and laughed, delighted. “Oh, she’s coming back.”

He started toward her.

Mara cut left toward the patio and grabbed the long-handled grill tongs off a hook as she ran. Not a weapon, not really, but her hands wanted something. She yanked open the basement bulkhead door—unlocked, of course, because nothing in this house was meant for real danger—and plunged into darkness.

The basement smelled of cardboard and laundry detergent. Water dripped steadily into a floor drain. She moved by memory and feel, fingers sliding along walls, heart hammering.

Above, footsteps thudded on the patio. Cal’s voice called down, playful. “Basement? Really?”

Mara didn’t answer. She found the stairs up from the basement to the kitchen and climbed them quietly.

In the kitchen, the shattered ceramic still littered the floor. The knife was gone from the banister—pulled free, or fallen, or taken. Mara’s stomach tightened.

Voices came from the hallway—Rook and Denny. Low, urgent.

Mara crouched and peeked around the kitchen corner.

Rook stood near the office door, shoulder braced against it while Denny fiddled with something at the lock—tools, maybe. Rook’s posture was all business. Denny’s hands shook.

“This is taking too long,” Rook snapped.

“I’m trying,” Denny hissed. “It’s— it’s not like my lock at home, man.”

Rook made a small, sharp sound of disgust. “Crowley said tonight. He said—”

Mara’s mind snagged on the name. Crowley. The way Rook said it—like it was an order, not a person.

A creak upstairs. Then Cal’s voice floated down the stairwell, sing-song. “Mara? You in the walls? Come on out. Let me see you.”

Rook’s head turned slightly toward the stairs. Annoyance flickered across his eyes. “Cal,” he called, controlled. “Stop. Watch the door.”

Cal didn’t answer.

Denny’s tool slipped, metal scraping. He swore under his breath, voice small. “This is—this is stupid.”

Mara’s gaze went to the office door. Solid wood. A keypad lock. A deadbolt. Mark’s insistence: you won’t need to go in there.

There was a narrow transom window above it—decorative, frosted. Too small for a man, but big enough for a hand. Big enough for a tool.

Mara’s pulse beat behind her eyes. She looked down at the grill tongs in her hand. Useless. Her shoulder throbbed from the fall. Her palm stung where thorns had torn skin.

She saw the hall closet door slightly ajar. Inside: a step ladder.

Mara moved without thinking, crossing the kitchen in two silent steps, sliding into the closet. She pulled the ladder out an inch at a time, careful to keep the hinges from squealing. She set it in the hallway just out of sight from Rook’s angle.

Then she waited, breathing shallow, listening for the next mistake.

The house was full of thin parts.

One of them was the space between Rook’s certainty and his impatience.

Above, Cal’s footsteps started down the stairs, slow.

Mara’s throat went tight. She had seconds.

She stepped into the hallway and spoke, loud enough to carry, keeping her voice flat. “Hey.”

Rook and Denny both snapped around.

Mara lifted the tongs like they were a gun. It was almost laughable. Denny’s eyes went huge anyway.

“Just the sitter,” Cal said from halfway down the stairs, amused.

Rook stared at Mara with irritation, as if she was a delay in a schedule. “Where are the kids?”

Mara didn’t answer. She pointed the tongs at Denny’s hands. “Drop it.”

Denny hesitated, looking to Rook for permission. His breathing was fast, panicked. Mara saw the tremor in his fingers. She saw how close he was to doing something stupid.

Rook’s eyes narrowed. “You think you’re in control?” he said.

Mara’s voice stayed even, because if it cracked she’d lose them. “I think you have a job,” she said. “And I think your friend upstairs is making it messy.”

Rook’s gaze flicked toward the stairs where Cal was descending, smiling.

“Cal,” Rook said sharply.

Cal’s smile widened. “What?”

Rook’s jaw clenched. The smallest fracture.

Mara moved.

She kicked the ladder forward with her foot, sending it scraping across the hall, and in the same motion she lunged toward the office door and rammed the tongs up into the transom window frame.

The tongs bit into the soft wood around the frosted glass.

She twisted hard.

Glass cracked with a brittle scream.

Denny shouted, startled. Cal laughed. Rook swore and surged toward her.

Mara jerked the tongs down, ripping the broken glass out and reaching through the jagged opening with her injured hand. Pain lanced up her arm as shards bit her skin.

Her fingers found the interior thumbturn of the deadbolt.

She turned it.

The lock clicked.

The office door swung inward.

Rook grabbed her hoodie from behind and yanked her back, hard, slamming her into the hallway wall. Her shoulder exploded with pain. Stars burst behind her eyes.

But the door was open.

Mara tasted blood where she’d bitten her cheek.

Inside the office, a small green light blinked on a safe in the closet.

And the house, suddenly, felt like it had handed her its throat.
step_outputs.10:
## Chapter 5 — Oxygen

The office smelled like toner and expensive cologne trying to be harmless.

Mara hit the wall, slid half a step, then forced her legs to hold. Rook’s fist twisted in her hoodie, bunching fabric tight under her collarbone. His other hand came up with something dark—gun, she realized, stupidly late. Not raised yet. Just present. A fact.

Cal leaned in the doorway behind them, rain dripping from his hood onto the hardwood. He looked delighted, as if Mara had opened the office like a gift for him.

Denny stood a few feet back, breathing fast, staring at the broken transom window with horror. “Jesus—”

Mara’s right hand—cut and stinging—was still inside the jagged glass frame. Blood ran down her wrist, warm against cold skin.

Rook’s eyes flicked from her hand to the safe light. “You opened it,” he said, almost accusing. “You shouldn’t have been able to open it.”

Mara swallowed. Her mouth tasted like copper. “Neither should you.”

Rook stared at her with a tight, controlled fury. “Where are the kids?”

Mara didn’t answer. She couldn’t risk her voice slipping toward the shed. She couldn’t risk Cal hearing a lie and deciding to test it.

Cal stepped closer, peering into the office with a child’s fascination. “Oh, this is the special room,” he murmured. “Daddy’s secrets.”

Rook’s grip tightened. “Move,” he said to Mara. He shoved her forward through the doorway.

Mara stumbled into the office and caught herself on the edge of the desk. Pain flared through her shoulder like an electric arc. She fought the urge to gasp. Noise was weakness, and weakness was invitation.

Rook followed, gun now visible at his side. Denny hovered in the hall. Cal stayed in the doorway like a bouncer to a nightclub.

“Open it,” Rook said, nodding at the closet safe.

Mara’s mind ran through time and distance: upstairs to the kids, down to the back door, the yard lit by motion lights, Rowan’s small hands fitting the spacer to the inhaler. Lily’s wheeze. The shed’s dark corner.

Her lungs felt suddenly too small. She forced herself to inhale slowly.

“I don’t know the code,” she said.

Rook’s gaze bored into her. “Try,” he said.

She walked to the closet. The safe was waist-high, bolted to the floor. A keypad with numbers worn slightly from use. Mark’s fingers, tapping without thinking. Rowan’s face when he’d confessed. *Dad taps his ring… three quick… then pause…*

Mara’s hands shook as she reached for the keypad. Blood smeared the plastic.

Rook’s voice came close to her ear, low. “You know what happens if you waste time.”

Mara’s fingers hovered. She didn’t have a plan. She had a pattern and a kid’s half-remembered shame.

She tapped: three quick numbers, then a pause. Another set. The safe beeped—angry.

Rook shifted, impatience thickening the air. “Again.”

Mara tried another permutation, heart pounding. Beep.

Cal sighed theatrically. “Let me,” he said, stepping forward.

Rook snapped his gaze at him. “No.”

Cal’s eyes narrowed, offended. He backed up a half-step, but his smile was gone now. “Fine,” he said softly. “Just saying.”

Mara’s fingers hovered again. She could hear, faintly, through the house—the sound of something upstairs. A floorboard. Or her own imagination. She couldn’t tell anymore.

She tapped again. This time, when she hit the last digit, the safe gave a softer tone. A click.

The door swung slightly open under its own weight.

For a fraction of a second, nobody moved.

Then Rook shoved Mara aside and yanked the safe door wide.

Inside: stacks of cash bound with rubber bands. A small flash drive in a plastic sleeve. And, tucked beneath them, a thin black notebook with a rubber band around it. Not a corporate binder. A cheap thing you’d buy at a pharmacy. The kind people used for grocery lists, phone numbers, sins.

Rook’s hand went in fast for the drive.

Mara’s eyes went to the notebook instead. A strip of paper stuck out between pages, and on it, in hurried handwriting: names. Amounts. Dates.

And under the notebook, wrapped in a dish towel as if it was fragile, something metallic glinted.

A class ring.

Mara recognized it without knowing why. Because small towns posted these things. Because missing kids became posters, then memories, then unspoken facts. A high school emblem. A year. Initials scratched into the band.

She saw the ring and felt her stomach drop through the floor.

“This is it,” Rook said, voice sharp with relief. He pocketed the flash drive. He grabbed the notebook with his gloved hand.

Cal leaned in, eyes bright. “What is it?”

“Not yours,” Rook snapped.

Cal’s gaze flicked to Mara. “She saw it,” he said, almost happily. “She saw the thing.”

Rook’s eyes hardened. He looked at Mara the way he’d looked at the foyer: as an obstacle, a noise.

He lifted the gun.

Mara’s body moved before her mind could. She grabbed the dish towel and the ring and threw both at Cal’s face.

Cal yelped and flinched, hands coming up. The ring struck his cheekbone with a dull smack. He swore, more angry than hurt.

Mara lunged for the notebook.

Rook barked something—half curse, half command—and grabbed at her. His fingers caught her hoodie again, but she twisted, using the slick blood on her wrist to slip free. Her nails raked his glove, useless.

She snatched the notebook, yanked it out of his hand, and ran.

Not upstairs. Not toward the kids. Toward the back of the house, because the front door was where Denny hovered and the back door led to rain and motion light and a yard she’d already mapped.

Behind her, Cal roared, rage finally flooding his play. “Bitch!”

Footsteps pounded. Rook’s voice, controlled even now: “Don’t kill her in the house.”

Mara hit the kitchen. Her foot slid on ceramic shards, and she caught herself on the island, knocking a chair over. The crash sounded like surrender.

She pushed through the back door and into the yard.

The motion light snapped on, bleaching everything. Rain hammered her face. The notebook stuck to her palm with blood and water, paper swelling.

She sprinted to the shed.

Rowan and Lily were crouched behind it as instructed, Rowan’s arms around Lily. Lily’s breath was a ragged whistle, the inhaler clutched in her hand like a toy.

Rowan looked up at Mara, eyes wide, face streaked with rain. Relief flashed—then fear as he saw her bleeding.

“Mara—”

“Blue,” Mara said immediately. The code word was a shove. “Run. Out the gate. To the neighbor’s porch. Bang until someone opens. Go.”

Rowan’s mouth opened. “But—”

“Now,” Mara said, and the command in her voice surprised even her.

Rowan grabbed Lily’s hand. Lily stumbled, wheezing, but she moved because Rowan pulled and Mara’s tone left no space for argument.

Mara turned back toward the house.

Cal burst out the back door, wild now, no mask in his eyes. Rook followed, gun low, scanning.

Denny appeared behind them, hanging back like he wanted to be anywhere else.

Mara backed toward the fence line, drawing them away from the kids’ path. The notebook was tucked into the waistband of her jeans. The class ring was in her hoodie pocket, heavy and wrong.

Cal charged. Mara grabbed the grill lid from the patio—heavy metal—and swung it like a shield.

Cal’s shoulder hit it. The impact rattled her teeth. She staggered back, arms shaking, but it bought her two seconds and two steps.

Rook’s voice cut through the rain. “Cal! Enough!”

Cal ignored him. He grabbed the lid and yanked it away with brute strength. “You think you can—” he started, and then his foot hit the slick edge of the patio step.

He went down hard.

His head cracked against the concrete with a sound like dropping a melon.

For a second, everything slowed.

Cal lay sprawled, rain pooling around his hood. His arms twitched once.

Mara stared at him. She hadn’t aimed for that. She hadn’t wanted that. But the world didn’t care about intent.

Rook swore, sharp. He looked at Cal with irritation—then at Mara with something colder. “Get up,” he snapped at Cal, but Cal didn’t.

Denny made a strangled noise. “Oh my God.”

Rook’s eyes flicked to Denny. “Shut up.”

Then Rook’s head lifted, listening.

From beyond the fence, a neighbor’s porch light snapped on. Then another. Rowan’s small fists pounding on a door. A muffled shout: “Help!”

Rook’s mouth tightened. He took two steps toward Mara, gun raising now, not as a threat but as a decision.

Mara’s body tensed, every nerve screaming. She had no cover. Just rain and fence and the knowledge that if she went down, the kids were next.

A new sound cut through the yard.

A siren.

Then another.

Red and blue light flickered through the rain like a distant heartbeat.

For a dizzy half second, Mara felt relief surge up so hard it made her nauseous.

Then she saw Rook’s expression.

He didn’t look surprised.

He looked annoyed.

He lowered the gun slightly and stepped back, eyes never leaving Mara. “We’re done,” he said.

Denny stared at him. “We’re—what? But—”

“We’re done,” Rook repeated. He grabbed Denny’s sleeve and yanked him toward the side gate.

Mara watched them vanish into the rain between fences like shadows swallowed by darker shadow.

She turned toward the street, heart hammering, and saw a police cruiser roll up slow, tires hissing on wet asphalt.

The passenger window was down.

The officer behind the wheel looked up at her.

“Mara Vance,” he said, like he’d been expecting her. “Step away from the house. Hands where I can see them.”

He opened the door.

Not in a hurry.

Not in fear.

In control.

Mara’s skin went cold all over again.

Behind her, Rowan’s voice, thin and terrified, carried from the neighbor’s porch: “She’s with us! She’s—she’s the babysitter!”

The officer’s gaze didn’t flick toward the children. It stayed on Mara.

His hand rested on his holster with casual familiarity.

“Ma’am,” he said, voice pleasant. “Do not make this harder than it has to be.”

Mara lifted her hands slowly.

In her hoodie pocket, the class ring felt like a stone dragging her down.
step_outputs.11:
## Chapter 6 — The Ledger Has Names

Mara didn’t run.

Not because she couldn’t. Because if she ran, the story became simple for them: babysitter flees from police, suspicious, probably guilty. If she stayed, maybe the story stayed messy enough to be true.

She held her hands up, palms out, rain washing blood down her fingers in thin red streams.

“Officer,” she said, keeping her voice level, “there are men in the house. One is injured. The kids—”

“I said step away,” the officer interrupted. Still pleasant. Still controlled. “Hands higher.”

Mara lifted her hands higher. Her shoulder screamed in protest. She didn’t let her face show it.

The officer walked toward her with unhurried steps. He was middle-aged, thick through the middle, uniform dark and immaculate despite the rain. A name tag gleamed on his chest: HARRIS.

He stopped close enough that Mara could smell his aftershave over the wet air. He glanced at her hands, at her torn hoodie, at the cut on her wrist.

Then he smiled.

It wasn’t friendly. It was the smile you gave a dog you were about to leash.

“You’re bleeding,” Harris observed.

“Yeah,” Mara said.

“Where’s your weapon?” he asked.

Mara stared at him. “My weapon?”

Harris’s eyes flicked past her to the patio, where the grill lid lay in the grass and Cal still sprawled, half-hidden by rain. “Whatever you used.”

Mara swallowed. “I used a grill lid.”

Harris chuckled softly, like she’d made a joke. “Okay,” he said. “Turn around.”

Mara’s mind raced. If he cuffed her, he’d separate her from the kids. He’d control the scene. Whatever else happened next would happen to Rowan and Lily without her.

From the neighbor’s porch, Rowan’s voice rose again, breaking: “Please! She didn’t—she—”

A woman’s voice—older, startled—hushed him. A door slammed.

Mara turned her head slightly. She could see Rowan’s small shape at the edge of the porch light, Lily pressed against him, both of them soaked.

She made a decision the way she made them in an ambulance: quick, ugly, necessary.

“Officer Harris,” she said, “Lily Hale is having an asthma exacerbation. She needs—”

“Turn. Around,” Harris repeated, and his pleasantness thinned. His hand came up, not quite touching her shoulder, but close.

Mara twisted her torso abruptly, making it look like compliance, and in the same motion she drove her elbow backward into his forearm—not his face, not something dramatic, just enough to jar him. He swore, startled.

Mara spun and sprinted.

Pain shot through her shoulder and down her ribs. Her lungs burned cold. She ran anyway, boots slipping on wet grass. She cleared the side gate and cut along the neighbor’s hedge, staying low, heading for the porch light.

“Rowan!” she hissed.

Rowan saw her and stumbled forward, dragging Lily by the hand. Lily’s breathing was a tight, whistling rasp.

Mara scooped Lily up—too heavy for comfort, but adrenaline ignored comfort—and ran. Rowan ran beside her, panting, face white.

Behind them, Harris shouted. “Stop! Stop right now!”

Mara didn’t look back. She could feel him on the street, the cruiser radio crackling, other voices waking up.

She aimed for the only place she could think of that was open and bright and full of witnesses: the all-night pharmacy on Maple, the one with the fluorescent lights that made everyone look like they were confessing.

They reached the sidewalk, then the street. Cars were sparse, but one passed and slowed, headlights catching them—woman carrying a child, boy running, blood on hands—and then accelerated away like it didn’t want to know.

Lily wheezed hard and clawed weakly at Mara’s hoodie. “I can’t,” she rasped.

“Yes you can,” Mara said, voice tight. “Look at me. In. Out.”

Rowan’s breath hitched. “Her inhaler—”

“In your pocket,” Mara said. “Get it. Spacer.”

Rowan fumbled in the rain, pulled the inhaler and spacer out with shaking hands. Mara stopped under the awning of a closed nail salon and set Lily down against the wall, shielding her with her body.

Rowan fit the spacer, pressed the inhaler. Lily sucked in, eyes squeezed shut, tears mixing with rain.

“Count,” Mara ordered, and Rowan counted, voice breaking. “One. Two. Three. Four…”

Mara watched Lily’s chest, watched the muscles in her neck strain, watched the panic in her eyes. Asthma was a thief; it stole time and made every second feel like punishment.

Her own hands trembled. Blood ran down her fingers again, diluted by rain.

Somewhere behind them, a siren whooped closer.

Mara made herself look up the street. The pharmacy sign glowed green through the rain.

“We’re going,” she said.

They moved in short bursts, stopping when Lily needed another puff. Mara’s breath came in harsh pulls. Her shoulder felt like it was full of broken glass.

At the pharmacy doors, they hit the automatic sensor and the doors slid open with a soft, indifferent sigh.

Warm air hit them. The smell of floor cleaner and plastic.

A teenage cashier looked up from her phone, eyes widening. “Oh my—”

“Help,” Mara said, and her voice finally cracked. “Asthma. She needs a neb. Call 911. Tell them—” She stopped. Tell them what? That the police were part of it? That the babysitter was bleeding and running?

The cashier’s hands hovered, useless. “We—we don’t—like, we have—”

“We have a first aid kit,” a man in a puffy jacket called from aisle five, peering over a shelf.

“I need a nebulizer,” Mara said, too sharply. She forced herself to breathe. She softened her voice because screaming wouldn’t open airways. “Is there a clinic near here? Urgent care?”

The cashier pointed shakily. “Across the street. The—um—Maple Urgent. But they close at—”

“Open,” Mara said, already moving. “Come on.”

Outside again. Across the street. Cars slowed now, watching.

Maple Urgent Care’s lights were still on. The door was locked, but a nurse behind the glass saw them and rushed to open it.

The nurse was middle-aged, tired, hair in a bun. She took one look at Lily’s face and moved like muscle memory. “Honey, come with me,” she said, guiding Lily and Mara inside. “We’ve got you. Sit. Sit.”

Mara sat Lily on a chair and held her upright while the nurse hurried away. Rowan hovered, hands clenched, eyes darting to the door.

Mara felt the class ring in her hoodie pocket like a weight. The notebook in her waistband pressed into her skin, damp paper softening.

The nurse returned with a nebulizer mask and a small machine. “Okay,” she said, brisk. “Breathe for me. Just breathe.”

Lily’s eyes went to Mara. Mara nodded. Lily let the mask be placed over her nose and mouth. The mist began, soft and steady.

Rowan sagged against the wall, trembling. He started to cry silently, shoulders shaking, fury and terror and relief all tangled.

Mara’s own knees threatened to give. She forced herself to stay upright, because if she fell apart now, Rowan would too.

The front door opened.

Mara’s head snapped up.

A woman stepped in wearing a damp jacket over her scrubs. Her hair was braided down her back, and rain dotted her freckles like ink.

Tess Donnelly.

She scanned the room, eyes sharp, then locked onto Mara. For a fraction of a second her face softened with relief—then hardened again when she saw the blood.

“Mara,” Tess said, and it came out like a swear. “What the hell?”

Mara’s throat closed. She hadn’t realized how much she’d been holding until Tess walked in and proved she wasn’t alone.

“Tess,” Mara managed. “I—”

Tess held up a hand. “Later,” she said. “Tell me what you need right now.”

Mara swallowed and forced herself into triage mode. “Lily’s asthma. She needs to finish the neb. Then we need to disappear. Police—” She hesitated. Saying it out loud felt like stepping off a ledge. “Police aren’t safe.”

Tess’s eyes narrowed. “Who’s not safe?”

Mara pulled the notebook out from her waistband with two fingers, as if it might bite. It was rain-swollen, the rubber band cutting into the cover. She handed it to Tess.

Tess took it, flipping it open. Her eyes moved over names, amounts. She went still.

Then she turned a page and saw the class ring Mara pulled from her hoodie pocket and set on the plastic chair between them. The initials glinted under fluorescent light.

Tess exhaled once, slow. “Jesus,” she whispered.

Mara’s voice went flat again, because flat was how you kept from screaming. “They came for this. Rook said Crowley. The cop knew my name.”

Tess looked up. “Crowley?” she repeated. The name hit her like a bad smell. “Bennett Crowley?”

Mara nodded.

Tess’s jaw clenched. “Okay,” she said. “Okay. We’re not calling anyone local. Not yet.”

Mara stared at her. “We can’t—”

“We can,” Tess cut in. “We just have to do it smart. You and I, we can’t out-law the law. But we can keep you alive long enough to put this in the right hands.”

Mara’s hands shook. “He’ll come for them,” she whispered, eyes flicking to Lily’s small body under the neb mask. “He’ll come for Rowan and Lily.”

Tess’s gaze softened, just a fraction. “Then we move them,” she said. “We keep them breathing. And we make sure Crowley can’t pretend this never happened.”

From outside, a siren wailed closer again, then faded as it passed. Or turned.

Rowan wiped his face with his sleeve and looked at Tess like she was a lifeline. “Are you… are you a nurse?” he asked hoarsely.

“Paramedic,” Tess said, glancing at him. “Which means I’m the person who shows up when people lie about how bad it is.”

Rowan’s mouth twitched, almost a smile, then collapsed. “They were in our house.”

“I know,” Tess said, and her voice went hard. “And they shouldn’t have been.”

Mara looked down at her cut hand, the blood smearing the plastic chair arm. She thought of Rook’s calm eyes, Cal’s laugh, the officer’s pleasant voice calling her by name.

Her mind finally stopped spinning long enough for one clear thought to land:

This wasn’t over. It had never been just a break-in. It was a leash tightening.

Mara lifted her eyes to Tess. “I can’t give this to the police,” she said, and it wasn’t a question. It was surrender to reality.

Tess closed the notebook carefully, as if it were a wound. “No,” she agreed. “You can’t.”

Lily’s breathing eased slightly under the mist. The whistle softened.

Mara watched her chest rise and fall, slow enough to count again.

Outside, the rain kept coming, steady and indifferent, washing the street clean of footprints.

Inside, under the fluorescent lights, Mara felt the moment turn—not toward safety, but toward something else.

Toward purpose.

## Chapter 7 — Crowley’s Voice

Tess didn’t take them to her house.

“Too obvious,” she’d said, already moving, pulling a spare hoodie out of her car and tossing it to Mara. “And my neighbors are nosy in the way that gets people killed.”

Instead she drove them behind the ambulance depot—an ugly block building that smelled like diesel and old coffee—and ushered them through a side door that didn’t have a sign. A supply room, half-lit, stacked with cardboard boxes labeled GAUZE, SALINE, CHILD MASKS. The kind of room nobody thought about until the world started bleeding.

“Sit,” Tess ordered. “Both of you.”

Rowan sat immediately on a folded blanket. Lily clung to Mara’s leg, exhausted, neb treatment done but eyes still wide with the aftertaste of fear. Mara’s cut hand had been cleaned and wrapped by the urgent care nurse under Tess’s watchful stare. Her shoulder was taped, imperfectly. Pain still lanced with every movement.

Tess set a bottled water in front of Mara and pushed a packet of crackers toward Rowan like it was an offering. “Eat,” she said. “Even if you don’t want to. Your body doesn’t care what you want.”

Rowan stared at the crackers as if they might be a trick. He took one and chewed slowly, eyes empty.

Mara leaned her head against the metal shelving, letting the coolness press into her skull. Her thoughts came in bursts now, not in a stream: *Rook’s gun. Cal falling. Harris smiling. The ring.* The ring was still on the table, wrapped in a paper towel.

Tess opened the notebook again under the harsh overhead light. The ink had run slightly on the first page from rain, but the names were clear enough. Too clear. Familiar names. People Mara had waved at in grocery stores without knowing anything about them beyond their choice of cereal.

Tess’s mouth tightened. “This is—” she started, then stopped. She didn’t have a word that covered this without making it smaller.

Rowan’s eyes flicked to the ring. “That’s… that’s from,” he whispered.

Mara looked at him. “You recognize it?”

Rowan swallowed. “Kyle Mercer. He—he was in high school. He died last year. Dad said it was… an accident.” His voice cracked on the last word like it didn’t fit anymore.

The ring lay there, dumb and damning.

Mara’s stomach turned. “That’s why,” she said softly, more to herself. “That’s why it’s in a towel.”

Lily lifted her head from Mara’s thigh. “Is Kyle a bad guy?” she asked, voice small.

“No,” Rowan said too quickly. Then, quieter: “No.”

Tess closed the notebook with care, as if it could spill if tipped. “Okay,” she said. “We need one thing we can use that doesn’t require anyone local to be honest.”

Mara rubbed her eyes with her good hand. “A state investigator?”

Tess snorted. “On a Saturday morning? Not unless the governor’s kid is involved.” She looked at Mara. “We need time. We need the kids somewhere safe. And we need to anticipate what Crowley does next.”

Mara’s throat tightened at the name. “He knows,” she said. “Rook said it like—like an order.”

Tess reached into her pocket and pulled out a cheap flip phone. “Burner,” she said. “Someone at the station owes me. Don’t ask.”

Mara took it. It felt wrong in her hand, too light for what it carried.

Tess continued, brisk. “You’re going to call one person who isn’t paid by Crowley. Not the cops. Not local reporters who go to his fundraisers. Someone outside the county. State attorney hotline, whatever. You keep it short.”

Mara stared at the phone. “And then what?”

Tess’s gaze went to Lily. “Then we move them. Now.”

Rowan’s head snapped up. “Move where?”

Tess’s face softened a fraction. “A friend of mine. She has a duplex. She won’t ask questions. And she’s not connected to anyone in this book.” Her eyes flicked to Mara. “We do this like an EMS call. One problem at a time.”

Mara nodded slowly. One problem at a time. That was a lie you told yourself when the problems were stacked to the ceiling. But it kept you moving.

She opened the flip phone and started dialing the state hotline number Tess had scribbled on a glove. The beep of each button sounded too loud in the supply room.

It rang.

Once. Twice.

A recorded voice: “You have reached the Office of—”

Mara hung up and tried again, choosing a different option. Her tongue felt thick. Her throat tasted like dried blood.

A human voice finally answered, bored. “Office of Inspector General.”

Mara forced her words into order. “My name is Mara Vance. I’m an EMT. I’m with two children. Their house was invaded tonight by men looking for evidence tied to Bennett Crowley. Local police are compromised. I have a ledger with names and a—” She glanced at the ring. “A class ring belonging to Kyle Mercer. I need—”

The voice sharpened slightly. “Ma’am, are you in immediate danger?”

Mara looked at Tess. Tess’s eyes were on the door, listening to nothing. “Yes,” Mara said.

“All right. Where are you?”

Mara’s gaze snapped to Tess again. Tess shook her head once, sharp. Don’t.

Mara chose a safer truth. “Not at the house. Somewhere public. I can’t—” Her voice wavered. She steadied it. “I can’t trust local dispatch.”

The voice sighed. “Ma’am, if you believe there’s immediate danger, you need to contact—”

“I did,” Mara cut in, and the anger in her voice surprised her. “And the officer knew my name before I told him.”

A pause. Then: “Okay. Okay. I’m going to take down your information. You said you have documentation?”

“Yes,” Mara said. “Yes. Names. Amounts. Dates.”

“All right. We’ll… we’ll open an intake.” The voice was cautious again, sliding back toward procedure. “If you can email—”

Mara almost laughed. Email. Like this was a complaint about potholes.

Tess leaned close and spoke quietly near Mara’s ear. “Get off. Don’t argue. We did what we can for now.”

Mara ended the call with a numb thumb.

“Not enough,” she whispered.

Tess’s voice was firm. “Enough to make you harder to disappear.”

They moved quickly after that. Tess drove with Rowan and Lily in the back seat. Mara sat up front, notebook pressed against her stomach like a wound. The sky was lightening from black to bruised gray. Morning was arriving as if nothing had happened.

Tess’s friend lived in a duplex behind a closed laundromat. The friend—Marcy—opened the door in sweatpants with her hair in a messy bun, took one look at Tess’s face, and stepped aside without questions.

Lily fell asleep on the couch almost immediately, Pickles clutched in her arms. Rowan stood in the corner like a guard dog, refusing to sit, refusing to look away from the windows.

Mara sank onto a kitchen chair. Her body shook now that it had permission. Small tremors in her hands, in her thighs. She hated it. She hated how her body betrayed fear after she’d forced it to be useful.

Tess crouched in front of her. “Hey,” she said, low. “Look at me.”

Mara met her eyes.

“You did good,” Tess said.

Mara’s throat tightened, and something hot stung behind her eyes. “I got someone killed,” she whispered.

Tess didn’t flinch. “You didn’t,” she said. “He chose the risk. He chose to be there.” Then, quieter: “And you kept those kids alive. That’s the math that matters.”

Mara’s breath hitched. She wiped her face with her sleeve, furious at herself for leaking.

Rowan’s voice came from the corner, thin. “My dad,” he said. “Is he—”

Mara looked at him. She didn’t have an answer. The parents had vanished into the night’s edges. Work dinner. Hotel. Rehearsed. Maybe already in Crowley’s hands.

Tess stood. “We’ll find out,” she said. It sounded like a promise she could be punished for.

A phone rang.

Not the burner. Tess’s actual phone, buzzing on the counter. An unknown number.

Tess stared at it like it was a snake. Then she answered, voice clipped. “Yeah?”

A man’s voice came through, audible enough that Mara could hear a soft, smooth cadence. “Ms. Donnelly,” the voice said. “Thank you for picking up.”

Tess went very still. “Who is this?”

A small laugh. “Bennett Crowley.”

Mara’s stomach dropped.

Crowley’s voice carried the warmth of a handshake. “I understand you’ve had a busy night. I don’t want misunderstandings. I want everyone safe.”

Tess’s knuckles whitened around the phone. “Safe means leaving children alone,” she snapped.

Crowley sighed gently, as if indulging a child. “Of course. Of course. That’s why I’m calling. Because a certain… babysitter has involved herself in matters she doesn’t understand.”

Mara’s skin prickled as if the voice had touched her.

Crowley continued, polite as church. “Mara Vance. EMT. Good at keeping people calm. Good at being the hero. Mara, if you’re listening—”

Mara leaned forward despite herself.

“I admire courage,” Crowley said. “Truly. But courage without context becomes recklessness. And recklessness gets children hurt.”

Mara’s jaw tightened so hard it ached.

Tess’s eyes flicked to Mara, warning. Don’t speak.

Crowley’s tone remained gentle. “I want what was taken from Mark Hale’s office. That notebook. That ring. That drive. Return them, and I will ensure you walk away from this with your life and your license intact.”

Mara’s hands curled into fists under the table. *Your license.* He knew what to threaten. He didn’t need to say “I’ll kill you.” He could ruin you and let you live in it.

Tess’s voice was rough. “You’re threatening kids.”

Crowley clucked softly. “No. I’m preventing harm. Threats are crude.” A pause. Then, almost conversational: “Your friend Mara is injured, yes? Shoulder. Hand. She should seek proper care. Stress can do terrible things to decision-making.”

Mara felt cold. He was painting her as unstable before anyone had even heard her version.

Crowley’s voice softened further. “Tell Mara she can bring the items to my office. Eight a.m. We’ll be reasonable. We’ll be adults.”

Tess’s laugh was short and ugly. “Go to hell.”

Crowley’s tone cooled, finally. “If she doesn’t come,” he said, and the warmth drained out like a bathtub, “then I will come to her. And I promise you—there are ways to make a babysitter look dangerous.”

The line went dead.

Silence filled the kitchen. The hum of the refrigerator. Lily’s small breathing from the couch. Rowan’s shaky exhale.

Mara stared at the phone as if it had burned a hole in the counter.

Rowan’s voice cracked. “He knows your name,” he whispered.

Mara nodded slowly. She could feel the shape of the threat like a hand closing around her throat: not just violence, but narrative. Paperwork. Charges. Headlines. *EMT attacks homeowner. Babysitter abducts children.*

Tess’s face was pale with fury. “We’re moving again,” she said. “Now.”

Mara’s voice came out low, steady in a way that frightened her. “No,” she said.

Tess froze. “Mara—”

Mara stood, shoulder protesting, and pulled the notebook out of her bag. Rain-warped paper, ink smudged, names still legible.

“He wants this back because it’s real,” Mara said.

Tess’s eyes sharpened. “What are you thinking?”

Mara looked at the sleeping child on the couch, at Rowan rigid by the window, at her own bandaged hand.

“I’m thinking,” Mara said, “that if I keep running, he keeps hunting. And next time, he doesn’t send idiots.”

Tess’s gaze held hers. “And if you go to his office, you walk into his house.”

Mara nodded once. “So I don’t go alone. And I don’t go quiet.”

Rowan swallowed. “What does that mean?”

Mara’s eyes flicked to the class ring on the table, wrapped in paper towel like evidence, like a body part.

“It means,” Mara said, voice flat and human, “we make him say it out loud.”
