- Spoken: “I’m not your confessional, Mara. Bring me proof I can print without getting someone killed.”
- Internal: “The city doesn’t fear truth. It fears coordination.”

---

# Calvin Rusk (developer / public patron; he/him)

### Background / personality / idiosyncrasies / flaws
- **Background**: 50s. Local real-estate developer with a curated image: funds murals, sponsors Pride-adjacent events, calls it “inclusive revitalization.” Runs pressure through zoning, licensing, selective policing, and private “consultants.” He doesn’t think of himself as evil; he thinks of himself as inevitable.
- **Personality**: Smooth, patient, paternal. Speaks like he’s offering opportunities even when he’s issuing threats. He genuinely believes queer spaces should exist—just not in ways that inconvenience investment.
- **Idiosyncrasies**:
  - Uses people’s first names too quickly; it’s a dominance play disguised as warmth.
  - Always has immaculate hands—no ink, no dirt—like the mess happens elsewhere.
  - Gives expensive pens as gifts.
- **Flaws**:
  - Underestimates people he sees as “nightlife” or “messy.”
  - Believes money is a solvent for all problems; when it fails, he escalates quietly.

### Physicality
- Impeccable suit, soft voice, steady eye contact that feels like being appraised. Rarely moves quickly; makes others rush around him.
- Smells faintly of clean soap and expensive wood—no smoke, no sweat.

### Motives / allegiances / relationships
- **Immediate motive**: remove Eclipse as an obstacle and keep his coercion network intact.
- **Deeper motive**: legacy—he wants the neighborhood to bear his name without acknowledging who he displaced.
- **Allegiances**: capital, reputation, control. He “supports” queer culture as branding and leverage.
- **Existing relationships**:
  - Has a city “community liaison” (can be unnamed in story) who interfaces with Ash and the club.
  - Compromised cops/inspectors who benefit from his influence.

### Flavour voice (quotes)
- Spoken: “Nobody is trying to erase you. We’re trying to improve the area. There’s a difference.”
- Internal (if glimpsed indirectly): “People like them mistake visibility for power. Visibility is just lighting.”

---

If you want, I can also add one more “anchor” character who appears briefly but repeatedly (e.g., **KJ**, the trans bouncer), because having one grounded supporting POV-adjacent presence often makes the world feel more inhabited and less plot-shaped.
step_outputs.6:
### Chapter 1 — The Doorway & the Dare

The city had a way of making everything look like it was bleeding.

Rain worried the edges of the curb, dragging cigarette wrappers and flyers into the gutter in slow, defeated spirals. The neon from Eclipse—violet tube letters fighting the drizzle—broke itself into pieces on the pavement. Every time a car passed, the reflections shivered like they were trying to get away.

I sat two doors down in my Corolla with the engine off and the windows cracked, letting the cold in so I wouldn’t fall asleep. The heater smelled like someone else’s cologne—old client, cheap, too much of it—and underneath that, the stale sweetness of smoke that never really left cloth once it had moved in. I could clean my apartment twice and wash my coat until the seams frayed, and still a stranger’s habits would cling to me like a second job.

On my dashboard, a stack of index cards slumped against a half-empty box of cinnamon toothpicks. Names, dates, addresses, license plates written in my own tight hand. When you spend too much time around people who can make a phone forget what it saw, you learn to trust paper. Paper and memory. Two flawed witnesses that, at least, you can look in the eye.

Across the street, Eclipse spat bodies into the night like it was doing them a favor.

They came out flushed and glittered and half-laughing, tugging coats closed against the weather. A drag queen in a wig the color of spilled wine stood under the awning smoking with the solemnity of a priest. Two men argued in low voices near the alley mouth, one of them wiping at his face like it had gotten wet somewhere other than the rain. A couple—women, hands linked—paused to look up at the sign as if making a wish.

Eclipse was the kind of place that survived by being useful. People talked about queer bars like they were a mood, a night out, a playlist. Eclipse was also a clinic waiting room, a job board, a reunion, a confession booth. It kept people alive in a city that preferred them quiet.

And it was about to have its permit reviewed.

I’d read the notice online two days ago, the kind of PDF that looked harmless until you understood what it could do. Licensing hearing in a week. “Nuisance complaints.” “Safety concerns.” Words that meant: We can close you without ever admitting we wanted you gone.

I wasn’t there for the hearing, not tonight. Tonight I was there because my voicemail had a message with the sound of someone trying not to cry.

“I don’t know who else—” the caller had started, breath catching on the edge of the sentence. “They said Nico just… left. But Nico doesn’t just leave. They wouldn’t.”

Nico Araya. Twenty-six. Sound tech, according to the caller. Last seen after a shift at Eclipse. The message didn’t say *missing person* in so many words, but it didn’t have to. You could taste panic through a phone line if you’d had enough of it in your mouth.

I waited until the line thinned. People leaving a club always looked like a miracle from a distance—alive, laughing, made luminous by someone else’s lights. Up close it was sweat and loneliness and the small violences you let happen to yourself when you wanted to be near other bodies.

The bouncer at Eclipse’s front door was a wall of a person in a fitted black tee, arms crossed, eyes scanning the sidewalk like it owed them money. Their head was shaved close, jaw square, and the kind of calm that didn’t mean peace so much as readiness.

When I got out of the car, the rain found me immediately. It slid down the back of my neck, cold fingers under my collar. I shut the door quietly and crossed the street, boots making that soft wet sound that always reminded me of old crime scene photos.

The bouncer watched me approach. Not hostile. Not friendly. Just assessing.

“Closed,” they said before I could open my mouth.

“I’m not trying to get a drink.”

They waited.

I pulled my PI license from my wallet. It was laminated, official-looking, and meant less than it used to. Still, it had weight in certain rooms.

Their eyes flicked down and back up. “We don’t talk to cops.”

“Not a cop.”

“You got the posture.”

“Occupational hazard.”

Their mouth tightened at one corner. If it was a smile, it had lost faith in the concept.

“Name?”

“Mara Vance.”

They looked at me like they were deciding whether I was a problem they could carry tonight. “What do you want, Mara Vance?”

“Nico Araya.”

Something shifted. Not surprise—recognition. A small tightening around the eyes, like a muscle bracing.

I kept my voice flat. “I’m looking into it. I was hired.”

“By who?”

“Roommate. Jay.”

That made the bouncer exhale through their nose. “Jay’s a mess.”

“People tend to be, when their people don’t come home.”

They didn’t argue with that. They glanced over my shoulder, at the street, at the cars. Checking for tails, probably. Checking for cops. Checking for anyone who’d use a missing person as an excuse to poke around a queer club.

“You can talk to Rory,” they said finally. “Manager. But if you bring trouble here—”

“I won’t.”

They stared at me until I understood the subtext. *You might not mean to.*

The door opened enough for me to slip inside. Warmth hit me first, heavy with citrus cleaner and old beer and the ghost of perfume. The bass was gone; without it, the club felt like a body after the heart stopped. Lights were up halfway, harsh on the scuffed floor, the glitter that had fallen like pollen.

In the back, someone was mopping with the aggression of a person trying to erase evidence. A bartender counted bills at the bar, lips moving as if reciting a prayer. The air held the scent of smoke, even though nobody was smoking inside. It clung to curtains and hair and the soft places between people.

Rory Quell stood near the bar with a clipboard and a lanyard heavy with keys. Broad-shouldered, tired-eyed, dressed in plain black like a uniform. They looked up when KJ—because I’d heard someone call them that by the door—said something under their breath.

Rory’s gaze found me and sharpened.

“No,” they said, like they’d seen my kind before and hadn’t liked the ending.

“I’m not here to shut you down,” I said.

“That’s what everyone says before they do.”

I set my license on the bar, let them see it without making them reach for it. People didn’t like reaching for things in front of strangers.

“I was hired to find Nico Araya,” I said. “They went missing after a shift. I’m talking to anyone who saw them last.”

Rory picked up the card with two fingers, glanced at it, and set it back down like it might stain. “We don’t have missing people. We have people who leave.”

“Nico doesn’t just leave,” I said, and watched their face for the flinch.

It came—small, involuntary.

Rory tapped the clipboard twice against their palm. “Nico clocked out at two. They were arguing with someone. Not staff.”

“Who?”

“If I knew that, do you think I’d be standing here talking to you?” Their voice wasn’t loud, but it had an edge. “Look, Mara—”

“Mara Vance.”

“Mara Vance,” they repeated, and it was too smooth for a first try. “We’re under review. Permit hearing next week. Inspectors already sniffing. If you’re here, it means someone outside this building cares about what happens inside it. That’s never good.”

“I can be discreet.”

Rory’s laugh was short and humorless. “Discreet is what people say when they mean secret.”

“Did Nico say anything to you before they left?”

Rory’s eyes flicked toward the back hallway—the staff corridor, a strip of shadow behind the lit room. “Nico was… on a tear lately. Taking pictures. Writing things down. Asking about paperwork. I told them to let me handle it.”

“And did they?”

Rory’s jaw worked. “They didn’t trust anyone who told them to be patient.”

“Can’t blame them,” I said, and meant it more than I wanted to.

From the back hallway came the squeal of a hinge, high and plaintive, like a complaint no one ever answered. The bathroom door, I guessed. Something about that sound dug under my ribs—too intimate, too familiar. Places like this had their own language of noises, and regulars learned it the way you learn the sounds of a house.

A figure appeared in the doorway of the staff corridor as if the shadows had decided to produce a person.

He leaned against the frame with a kind of laziness that had tension woven through it. Dark hair that needed a trim. Cheekbones sharp enough to cut glass. A cigarette between two fingers, unlit, like he was waiting for permission or daring someone to deny him.

He looked at Rory first, then at me. His gaze didn’t settle. It skimmed—my hands, my coat, the way I held my shoulders. When his eyes met mine, it felt like he’d already decided something about me and was waiting to see if I’d live up to it.

He had the scent of smoke on him already, even without the cigarette lit, like his skin had absorbed it over years and given up on being anything else.

Rory’s shoulders tightened. “Not now, Ash.”

Ash. The name landed like a joke with teeth.

He pushed off the doorframe and walked toward us, quiet as a thought you didn’t want. When he reached the bar, he set the cigarette down on the counter and fished in his pocket for a lighter.

It was the cheap kind, the kind you got free with a carton if you were lucky, scratched and stubborn. He flicked it once. Nothing. Twice. The wheel rasped.

I watched his hands. They didn’t quite stop moving.

Rory said, “We’re closed.”

Ash didn’t look at them. He looked at me. “You’re the investigator.”

It wasn’t a question.

I didn’t answer.

His mouth curved, almost. “That’s a yes.”

Rory’s voice cut in. “Ash—”

“I saw Nico,” Ash said, and the room shifted around the words. Even the bartender stopped counting.

My pulse did something unpleasant. “When?”

“After their shift,” he said, like he was describing the weather. “In the alley.”

Rory’s eyes went hard. “You didn’t tell me that.”

Ash shrugged, slow. “You were busy.”

“Busy trying to keep this place open.”

“Same thing,” he said, and there was something in it that wasn’t quite contempt and wasn’t quite tenderness.

I leaned forward a little, the way you do when you’re trying to keep a person from realizing they have power. “What did you see?”

Ash flicked the lighter again. A brief flare. He lit the cigarette and inhaled like it was a decision.

Smoke slid out of his mouth on the exhale, a pale ribbon that made the air between us look thicker. It carried menthol and something burnt.

“What I saw,” he said, “is that you’re gonna get yourself trespassed if you ask Rory for anything useful.”

Rory’s eyes flashed. “Get out of my bar.”

Ash didn’t move. He took another drag. His gaze stayed on me like I was the only person in the room. Like he’d chosen me as his problem.

“I can help,” he said.

“For what?” I asked.

He smiled, and it was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.

“For a favor,” he said. “And because you look like you’re trying very hard not to want anything.”

I hated that my face did something. A twitch, maybe. I kept my voice level. “I don’t do favors for information.”

“You do,” he said. “You just call them ‘choices’ so you can sleep.”

Rory slammed the clipboard down on the bar. “Ash, seriously.”

Ash finally turned his head. “Rory, I’m handling it.”

“You don’t get to handle anything,” Rory snapped. “You make things worse and then you disappear.”

Ash’s jaw tightened. For half a second the charm dropped away and something raw showed through—anger, hurt, or fear. Then it was gone, replaced by smoke and a half-smile.

He looked back at me. “Delivery entrance. After close. Fifteen minutes. No cameras.” He tipped his cigarette toward the staff corridor like it was a compass. “You want Nico? You don’t get them by being good.”

I should have said no. My license was already a threadbare thing. My name on the wrong incident report again and I’d be out of work. Out of this version of myself.

I should have turned around, walked back into the rain, called Jay, told them I’d do what I could within the law.

Instead, I reached into my pocket and felt my fingers curl around the cinnamon toothpicks, grounding myself with the small sting of spice.

“I’ll think about it,” I said, which is what people say when they’ve already decided.

Ash watched me like he could hear the decision solidify behind my teeth. He took the lighter—his lighter—and set it on the bar between us.

“Take it,” he said. “So you come back.”

I stared at the lighter. Cheap plastic. Scratched metal. The kind of object you could lose without noticing until you needed it. The kind of object that made you dependent without ever announcing itself as a trap.

Rory said, low, “Mara. Don’t.”

I picked up the lighter anyway. It was warm from his hand.

Ash exhaled smoke toward the ceiling. “See you,” he said, like it was inevitable.

Outside, the rain had turned colder. The street smelled like wet concrete and exhaust and the last stubborn traces of summer garbage. I walked toward my car with the lighter in my pocket and the uneasy sense that I’d already stepped somewhere I wouldn’t be able to step back from.

In the alley behind Eclipse, a cigarette butt lay mashed into the wet grit. The paper was torn at one end, ragged like someone had worried it with their teeth. There was a faint smear of something on it—lip balm, maybe, or blood.

I stared at it longer than I should have.

Then I got in the car, shut the door, and let the smell of smoke in my coat fill the small space until it felt like a second person riding with me.
step_outputs.7:
### Chapter 2 — Back Stairs, Wrong Places

At one forty-seven a.m., the city looked like it had given up on being seen.

The rain had thinned to a fine, needling mist that made streetlights smear. The neon from Eclipse was still on, but dimmer—like it knew it was being watched. I parked around the corner this time, out of habit and superstition, and walked the last half-block with my hands in my coat pockets and Ash’s lighter heavy as a coin against my thigh.

Fifteen minutes. No cameras.

I told myself it was just a conversation. I’d done worse for less. I’d sat in worse rooms with better reasons. I’d also promised myself, after the complaint that put my license under review, that I wouldn’t trespass again. Not into records rooms, not into offices, not into anyone’s life.

The back of Eclipse was quieter than the front, but not peaceful. The alley was a narrow throat between buildings, littered with flyers that had soaked through and turned to pulp. A dumpster idled in the shadows like a resting animal. The smell was wet cardboard, old beer, and something metallic that made the back of my tongue go sour.

He was there already, leaning against the delivery door, smoking like he had all the time in the world.

His cigarette ember made a small, stubborn star in the dark. When he saw me, his face didn’t change much, but his posture did—subtle shift of weight, a readiness that wasn’t quite welcome and wasn’t quite defense. He flicked ash with an impatient thumb.

“You came,” he said.

“I said I’d think about it.”

“That’s what that meant.” He exhaled, the smoke pale in the damp air. “You alone?”

“Do I look like I brought a marching band?”

His mouth twitched. “You look like you brought a warrant. Same energy.”

I stopped a few feet away, just outside the circle of his cigarette glow. Close enough to smell the menthol and the citrus cleaner that clung to him from inside. Close enough to see the fine tremor in his hand when he brought the cigarette to his mouth.

“You said you saw Nico,” I said.

He nodded once, like he’d been asked his name. “They were mad. Not the cute kind.”

“Who were they arguing with?”

He shrugged. “Someone in a nice coat who didn’t belong in this alley.”

“Man? Woman?”

“Man.” He dragged on the cigarette, eyes half-lidded, and for a second he looked tired in a way that had nothing to do with the hour. “You want details, you’ll have to come in.”

The delivery door was behind him, a steel slab painted black. No handle on the outside. No keypad, no visible lock. Rory didn’t run this door like a fortress; that would have made it a fortress in the wrong people’s minds. This door was run like an assumption. Most people didn’t try it.

I reached into my pocket and touched the lighter, a reminder of my own poor choices. “If you’re setting me up—”

“I don’t need to set you up,” he said. “You walked here on your own legs.”

“Why are you helping me?”

Ash smiled without humor. “Because I’m already involved. Because Nico’s a pain in the ass. Because Rory will die of stress before forty-five. Pick one.”

“You’re twenty-nine,” I said, watching him.

He blinked, almost startled. “What?”

“You look like you think Rory’s the old one.”

His laugh was quiet and short. Then he pressed his cigarette into the wet brick beside the door, smearing the ember out. The action was brisk, irritated, like he was punishing the cigarette for existing.

“Give me the lighter,” he said.

I didn’t hand it over. “It’s yours.”

“It’s a loan,” he corrected, and this time the smile was real enough to irritate me. “I want it back.”

“Why?”

He leaned in slightly, voice lowering, as if the alley had ears. “Because it’s mine. Because if you keep it, it means you’re coming back, and that makes it easier to find you when things go sideways.”

“Comforting,” I said.

He held out his hand anyway. I hesitated a beat too long, then placed the lighter in his palm. His fingers closed around it, warm and quick. He flicked it once—first try, flame blooming like it had been waiting for his touch. He didn’t light another cigarette. He just watched the flame for a second and then snapped it out.

“Stay close,” he said, and rapped twice on the door in a specific rhythm.

From inside came the scrape of metal, then the door cracked open. A sliver of light cut into the alley and painted Ash’s cheekbone sharp. He slipped through first, pulling me after him with a hand at my elbow—not quite a grab, not quite a touch. Practical. Intimate. Annoying.

Inside, Eclipse’s back corridor smelled more strongly of citrus cleaner and stale sweat. It was a narrow passage with pipes along the ceiling, posters half-peeled from the walls, and a faint vibration like the building remembered music even when it was quiet.

The person who let us in was not Rory. A younger staffer with a mop bucket and the exhausted eyes of someone who had already worked a double. They barely looked at me. Ash gave them a nod, and that was apparently enough.

He led me up a back stairwell. Concrete steps, paint chipped down to grey. The kind of stairs where secrets sat in the corners like dust. Halfway up, the bathroom door on the landing opened and that hinge squealed—high, plaintive. Someone came out, glanced at Ash, then at me, and went past without a word.

“Your place has a lot of hidden rooms,” I said, mostly to hear my own voice.

“Our place,” Ash corrected automatically, then looked away as if he hadn’t said it. “Don’t start romanticizing it. It’s a building with a liquor license and a target on its back.”

We reached a door marked STAFF ONLY in faded white letters. A simple knob. No keypad. I thought of how hard it would be to shut this place down if you really wanted to. You didn’t have to kick down doors. You just had to make sure the right ones stayed locked.

Ash produced a ring of keys from his pocket and found one without looking. The key slid in, turned. He pushed the door open.

The room beyond was a storage closet: cases of liquor, paper towels, spare lightbulbs, a folded ladder. Nothing cinematic. No wall of photos. No red strings. Just the infrastructure of a place that tried to keep running.

“This is your big secret?” I asked.

Ash stepped inside, shut the door behind us, and for a moment the sound of the club fell away completely. We were in a small pocket of silence that smelled like cardboard and bleach.

“You wanted Nico,” he said. “Nico kept their bag here when they didn’t want to leave it in the booth.”

“You have their bag?” My pulse quickened.

He crouched and dragged a duffel out from behind the cases. The bag was black, patched in two places, with a strip of gaffer tape labeling it NICO in block letters.

“They trusted you with it?” I asked.

Ash looked up at me, eyes sharp. “No.”

“Then why do you have it?”

He stood, slung the bag onto a crate. “Because I can open doors. Because people leave things behind. Because if I didn’t grab it, someone else would. Someone who doesn’t care.”

I watched his hands as he unzipped the duffel. He moved quickly but not sloppily. There was care in it, a kind of respect for Nico’s things even as he violated the boundary of them.

Inside was a mess of practical items: a multi-tool, two rolls of tape, a pair of work gloves, a bundle of cables. Ash dug past them and pulled out a small notebook—cheap paper, dog-eared. Then a disposable camera, the kind that felt obsolete until you remembered how hard it was to trace.

He set them on the crate between us.

“What is this?” I asked.

“A problem,” he said.

I picked up the notebook. Pages filled with dates and initials. Some were familiar—Rory’s, maybe, if you squinted. Some weren’t. There were numbers beside them, sometimes with a letter: V, I, P. Notes like “INSPECTOR—2nd warning,” “KJ—cop friend?,” “RUSK mtg???” scribbled in the margins.

“Rusk,” I said aloud.

Ash’s face went still. “You know that name.”

“Developer,” I said. “Calvin Rusk. Redevelopment project on the east side. He’s been buying buildings like he’s collecting teeth.”

Ash’s mouth tightened. “Yeah.”

The word had weight. Not confirmation exactly—something older, something loaded.

“You work for him,” I said.

“I work,” Ash said, too fast. He leaned back against the shelves, crossing his arms. “I work for whoever keeps the lights on. Sometimes that’s Rory. Sometimes that’s… other people.”

I turned the notebook page carefully, as if it might bite. There was a folded flyer stuffed between sheets. I pulled it free.

CITY OF —— LICENSING & SAFETY HEARING. Permit Renewal Review: Eclipse Entertainment LLC.

Date. Time. Location.

I felt my stomach drop. “Nico had this,” I said.

“Yeah,” Ash replied. “Nico was gearing up for war with a clipboard.”

I looked at the disposable camera. “What’s on it?”

Ash’s gaze flicked to it and away. “Pictures. Of people. Cars. Inspectors. I don’t know. Nico was… thorough.”

“I need to develop it.”

“You can’t just—” He stopped himself. His jaw worked. “You can. But… be careful.”

The closet felt smaller. The air had gone thin. Paper evidence was dangerous in a way digital wasn’t. Digital could be copied, sanitized, leaked. Paper had fingerprints. Cameras had film. Physical things required physical risk.

I pocketed the notebook, then hesitated. “I’m taking this.”

Ash nodded like he’d expected it. “That’s why you came.”

“And the camera,” I added.

His eyes sharpened. “That’s not yours.”

“It’s Nico’s.”

“They’re not here to argue with you,” he said, and there was something raw under it. “Don’t make their stuff your hero prop.”

The words hit harder than they should have. I hated him for being right, and hated myself for needing him to say it.

“Where is Nico?” I asked.

Ash’s expression closed. “If I knew for sure, I wouldn’t be doing this in a closet.”

“That’s an answer,” I said.

“It’s not a good one.”

We stood there for a moment in the bleach-scented dark, two people hovering over someone else’s life like vultures with different excuses.

A thump sounded outside the closet door. Then voices, muffled—staff, maybe, or security. The knob jiggled once.

Ash moved instantly. He grabbed the duffel, shoved the notebook’s remaining items back inside with quick hands, and slid it behind the liquor cases again. Then he stepped close to me—not touching, but near enough that the heat from his body warmed the cold space between us.

“Don’t talk,” he murmured.

The door opened.

Rory stood there, keys in hand, eyes sweeping the closet, landing on me like a verdict.

For a beat, nobody spoke. The club’s corridor noise poured in around Rory’s shoulders—footsteps, mop water sloshing, the distant metallic clank of a trash can.

Rory looked at Ash. “What the hell is this?”

Ash didn’t flinch. “I’m cleaning up.”

“With a PI?”

Ash’s smile returned, thin and bright. “You said you didn’t want to talk to cops. She’s not a cop.”

Rory’s gaze flicked to my face. “What did you take?”

I didn’t answer fast enough. A mistake. Silence was its own confession.

Ash cut in smoothly. “Nothing. She asked questions. I gave her nothing. Now she’s leaving.”

Rory stepped closer. Their voice lowered, dangerous. “Ash, you don’t get to decide who walks through my back doors.”

Ash’s eyes flashed. “Then decide. Kick her out. Kick me out. Kick Nico out while you’re at it. See how that goes.”

The words hung there, ugly and true. Rory’s throat bobbed as if swallowing something bitter.

I slipped past the edge of the conflict, into the corridor. KJ was at the stairwell, watching. Their gaze met mine—warning, not hostility.

I headed down the stairs toward the delivery door, my heart punching at my ribs. Behind me, I heard Rory say Ash’s name again, softer this time, like a plea disguised as anger.

Outside, the alley air hit me cold and wet. I walked fast, hands deep in my pockets, notebook pressing against my thigh like a bruise.

At the mouth of the alley, an unmarked sedan idled across the street with its lights off. Someone inside, silhouette barely visible. Watching. Waiting. Not cops, maybe. Or worse: cops in civilian clothes, the kind that didn’t have to play by the rules they pretended to enforce.

I kept my face neutral and didn’t look directly at the car as I passed. In the reflection of a puddle, I caught a flash of movement—Ash slipping out behind me, cigarette already in his mouth, as if smoke was the only thing that made him real.

He didn’t follow me to my car. He stopped at the edge of the alley and watched the street, watching the watcher.

When I started my Corolla, the heater coughed warm air and the cologne smell rose again. I pulled away slowly, and in my rearview mirror I saw Ash lift a hand—not a wave, not a goodbye. Something in between. A gesture like: *Remember you owe me.*

The notebook on my passenger seat looked harmless. Cheap paper. Shitty pen ink.

Bad ideas always did, at first.
step_outputs.8:
### Chapter 3 — Daylight Makes It Worse

By nine in the morning, Eclipse looked like any other building pretending it wasn’t tired.

In daylight the violet neon was off, the windows were just dark glass, and the mural next door—two women dancing under an impossible moon—had been half-swallowed by beige primer. The city called it “prepping.” The rest of us called it what it was: erasure with a work order.

I hadn’t slept. I’d tried, stretched out on top of my bed with my boots kicked off and my coat still on, like I could keep myself ready by refusing comfort. My phone buzzed twice. A spam call. A reminder email from the state licensing board about “continuing education requirements,” which felt personal.

I showered anyway. Hot water, cheap soap. I stood under the stream until the heat turned to sting, then shut it off and listened to my apartment settle. Somewhere, a neighbor’s smoke alarm chirped once—low battery—then went quiet. The building was full of small warnings nobody answered.

In my kitchen I laid Nico’s notebook open beside my own scratch pad and the disposable camera. The camera was light in my hand, ridiculous and dangerous at the same time. Digital pictures were a convenience. Film was a confession.

The notebook pages were a jumble of dates and initials. Some patterns emerged when I stopped trying to force them.

Tuesdays and Thursdays had more entries. Permit inspections? Trash pickups? Names repeated beside “INS” and “LIC.” There were notes like “R—said keep mouth shut,” “KJ saw car again,” “ASH???” circled twice, then crossed out so hard the page tore.

And always that name: RUSK.

I’d seen Calvin Rusk’s face on billboards and local news, smiling beside renderings of “mixed-use revitalization” that looked like every other sterile development trying to cosplay community. He sponsored murals. He sponsored Pride-adjacent events that were mostly corporate booths and cops in rainbow patches. He did not sponsor places like Eclipse unless he was also holding a knife behind his back.

At ten I drove to the address Ash had given me last night as the price of his help.

He hadn’t said “clinic.” He’d said, Deliver this sealed envelope to a woman named Lorna at a day place on Seventh. Say it’s from me. Don’t ask questions.

It wasn’t the worst favor I’d ever been offered, which is how they got you.

The clinic sat in a converted storefront between a payday loan place and a shuttered bakery. The sign was hand-painted, the kind you could tell had been updated over the years with careful lettering to cover old words. A rainbow sticker in the window was sun-faded to pale stripes.

Inside smelled like disinfectant and old coffee. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead. A television in the corner played daytime court TV with the volume low, captions rolling like confessions nobody listened to.

People sat spaced out in plastic chairs. A man with track marks on his forearms tapped a rhythm on his knee. A woman in a hoodie stared at the floor like it owed her something. A kid with purple hair chewed on the sleeve of their sweatshirt and scrolled on their phone with shaking hands.

No one looked surprised to see me. Strangers came in and out of places like this all day. The city spat people out, and some places tried to catch them.

At the front desk, a receptionist with reading glasses peered up. “Can I help you?”

“I’m looking for Lorna,” I said.

The receptionist’s gaze sharpened just slightly. “Who’s asking?”

“Mara.”

“Last name?”

I hesitated. A small pause can be a mistake. “Vance.”

She considered me, then jerked her chin toward a hallway. “Second door. Knock.”

The hallway smelled like latex gloves and lavender spray that wasn’t fooling anybody. I knocked on the second door.

“Yeah,” a voice called, tired.

I stepped in.

Lorna was in her fifties, maybe, with grey hair pulled back in a tight knot and a face that looked like it had learned not to waste expression. She sat behind a metal desk piled with forms. A jar of pens. A bowl of hard candies. Her eyes flicked to my hands, then to my face.

“You’re not one of mine,” she said.

“No,” I replied. “I have something for you.” I pulled the envelope from my coat pocket. It was plain, no address, sealed with tape.

Her gaze dropped to it. “From who?”

“Ash Serrano.”

Something in her face softened, then hardened again, as if tenderness was an old reflex she didn’t trust. “Where is he?”

“I don’t know,” I said, because it was the truth and also because it was safer. “He asked me to deliver it.”

Lorna took the envelope without touching my fingers. She turned it over, checking the seal, then tucked it into a drawer and locked it.

“Is it money?” I asked before I could stop myself.

Lorna’s eyes lifted. “That your business?”

“It becomes my business when I’m carrying it,” I said.

She leaned back slightly, chair creaking. “It’s rent assistance,” she said, like she was giving in to a harmless question. “For one of our folks. He thinks he’s slick.”

“And is he?”

Her mouth twitched. “He’s alive. That’s slick enough.”

I watched her for a beat. “Does he owe you?”

“Everybody owes everybody,” Lorna said. “That’s how we survive.”

A line from Ash last night came back to me—*You do favors. You just call them choices so you can sleep.* I didn’t like how well he’d read me.

I nodded toward the waiting room beyond the hallway. “Do you know Nico Araya?”

Lorna’s eyes narrowed. “Why?”

“I’m looking for them.”

“No,” she said immediately, and it wasn’t denial. It was caution. “You don’t bring that in here.”

“Lorna—”

She stood. Not tall, but the room shifted around her like she knew how to claim space. “Listen. People come here because we keep things separate. Street from clinic. Trouble from treatment. You want to find your missing person, go do it out there. Don’t make this place one more door the city can kick in.”

I held up my hands, palms out. “I’m not police.”

“Everyone says that,” she replied, echoing Rory, echoing the city. “And then the police show up anyway.”

I swallowed. “Did you see Nico? Before they disappeared?”

Lorna’s face closed, but not fully. “They came in once,” she admitted. “Looking for Narcan. Looking for… information.” She frowned. “They asked about inspections. About who had been seen around Eclipse. Like they were building a case.”

“Did they say they were in danger?”

“They said they were tired of being told to be patient.” Lorna’s gaze pinned me. “Sound familiar?”

I didn’t answer.

She sat back down, like the moment of vulnerability had cost her something. “Leave me out of it,” she said. “And tell Ash—” She stopped, jaw tightening. “Tell him he can’t buy forgiveness with envelopes.”

“I’ll pass it on,” I said, though I didn’t know if I would. Ash didn’t seem like a man who accepted forgiveness in any form, bought or earned. He seemed like someone who stayed just out of reach of it.

On my way out, a kid in the waiting room glanced up. Their eyes were bloodshot, their eyeliner smudged. They looked like they’d been to Eclipse once and decided it was holy.

They watched me with a wary familiarity. “You with the club?” they asked quietly.

“I’m with a missing person,” I said.

Their mouth tightened. “They’re gonna shut it down,” they whispered, and the fear in it made my chest hurt. Not the abstract fear of losing a bar. The concrete fear of losing the one place that didn’t ask for an explanation every time you breathed.

I left with Lorna’s words stuck under my tongue like a splinter.

Outside, the sky was a low lid of cloud. I sat in my car and opened Nico’s notebook again, letting the daylight flatten the ink so I could see what I’d missed.

There—on a page I’d skimmed too fast—was a list of addresses. One circled: a municipal building downtown. Licensing and Safety. Another circled: “WELLNESS SUITES?” with a question mark and a street name.

And beneath that, in smaller writing: *he knows. ash knows. ask him right.*

I shut the notebook and stared at my steering wheel until my hands stopped shaking.

At noon I made a call I didn’t want to make. I found the number in my phone under a name I’d never deleted because deletion was a lie you told yourself about closure.

“Records,” a woman answered, bored.

“This is Mara Vance,” I said. “I need to verify a permit inspection schedule for Eclipse Entertainment LLC.”

There was a pause. Typing. Then, “Who are you with?”

“I’m a licensed private investigator. I’m working on—”

“Not our job,” she said, and I could hear her smile through the line.

“It is your job to tell me if an inspection notice was issued,” I said, keeping my voice even. Even was safer than pleading.

“Even if it was,” she replied, “we don’t give that information out to—” She stopped, then continued, “—to you.”

“Let me talk to a supervisor,” I said.

Another pause. A different voice came on, deeper, impatient. “Vance.”

My stomach tightened. I recognized him before he said his name. “Sergeant Halden,” I said, and hated that my voice did something—tightened, like a dog showing its teeth.

“We heard you’re sniffing around Eclipse,” Halden said. “That true?”

“I’m investigating a missing person.”

“Missing people are our jurisdiction.”

“I’m aware.”

He chuckled. “Then why aren’t you bringing it to us?”

Because you’d bury it. Because you’d use it. Because you’d make it worse. I didn’t say any of that.

“I have a client,” I said.

“You have a license under review,” he replied, too smoothly. “You keep acting like you’re still one of us, you won’t have either.”

My fingers dug into the steering wheel. “Is that a threat?”

“It’s advice,” he said. “Drop it. Eclipse is already on the board’s radar. You want to be the reason they get shut down? You want to be the reason your name comes up again in front of the committee?”

He knew exactly where to push. The city had its hands on all of us. Some people just pretended they didn’t.

“I’m looking for someone,” I said. “That’s all.”

Halden’s tone cooled. “People like Nico Araya don’t disappear into thin air. They disappear into choices. You wanna help? Stop making it worse.”

The line went dead.

I sat there for a long minute, listening to the hum of my phone, the faint hiss of city traffic. Daylight made everything clearer, and that was the problem. In daylight you could see the mechanisms. You could see the way the city didn’t need alleyways and guns to hurt you. It had permits. Hearings. Sergeants with phones.

I drove back toward Eclipse in the late afternoon, not to go in, just to circle. The beige primer on the mural had grown. Two dancers were now one torso and a smear. A work crew had left their ladders stacked against the wall like abandoned bones.

I parked across the street and watched the building like it might blink.

Ash came out of a side door around six, not the main entrance, not the alley. He wore a hoodie, hands shoved into the pocket, head down against the mist. He wasn’t smoking.

That was how I knew he was worse than he looked.

I got out of my car and crossed the street. He saw me halfway and stopped, eyes scanning the sidewalk before settling on me with a kind of resigned irritation.

“You’re early,” he said.

“I went to the clinic,” I replied.

His face tightened. “Lorna.”

“She thinks you’re trying to buy forgiveness.”

He snorted. “She thinks correctly.”

I held up the notebook. “Nico wrote that you know. That I should ask you right.”

Ash’s gaze flicked to the notebook and away, like it burned. “Nico wrote a lot of things.”

“Who were they arguing with in the alley?” I pressed. “Last night you gave me atmosphere. I need detail.”

He looked past me at the mural being erased. The primer looked like a bandage slapped over a wound that wouldn’t heal.

“You don’t want the detail,” he said quietly.

“I do,” I said. “I want to find them.”

His jaw worked, as if he was chewing a lie into something smaller. “The guy in the nice coat,” he said finally. “He had a city badge on his belt. Not police. Inspector. Nico called him out by name.”

“What name?”

Ash’s eyes met mine. For a moment, something like fear flickered there—fear not for himself, but for the consequences of letting me have information. Then his mouth hardened.

“Rusk,” he said.

I felt the word settle into my bones.

Ash took a step closer. He smelled like clean hoodie fabric and the stubborn ghost of smoke. His voice dropped, intimate without being gentle.

“You keep digging like this,” he said, “and you’re gonna light the whole box. Not just the people you hate.”

“I don’t hate anyone,” I said automatically.

He gave me a look like he didn’t believe in that kind of purity. “Sure,” he said. “And I smoke for my health.”

I stared at him, and the desire that rose in me was as ugly as it was real: the desire to grab his wrist and demand truth; the desire to press my mouth to his and shut him up; the desire to run.

Instead, I did something small and stupid and honest.

I pulled a bottle of water from my coat pocket—one I’d grabbed at the clinic vending machine, out of habit. I twisted the cap, took a sip first, and held it out.

Ash stared at it like it was a trick. Then he took it, fingers brushing mine, and drank like he’d forgotten what it felt like to accept something without paying.

For one second, his eyes closed.

Then he handed it back. “You’re gonna do a bad thing,” he said, not quite a question.

“Probably,” I admitted.

His mouth curved. Smoke would come later, I could tell. First came that look, that dare.

“Meet me tonight,” he said. “Not at Eclipse. Somewhere else. I’ll show you what Nico found.”

“And what do you get?” I asked, because I had learned the hard way that nothing was free.

Ash’s smile was thin. “I get to not be alone in it,” he said, and then he turned and walked away before I could decide whether that was a confession or another lie.

### Chapter 4 — The Archive

He chose a place that didn’t look like noir.

No rain-slick rooftop. No abandoned warehouse with swinging chains. No jazz seeping through a cracked window. Just a self-storage facility off the freeway with a bright orange sign and a row of fluorescent lights that made everyone’s skin look sick.

I arrived ten minutes early and parked between a moving truck and a sedan with a child seat in the back. Somewhere nearby a dog barked in the muffled way dogs did when they were trapped inside metal. The air smelled like hot asphalt and stale cardboard.

The security gate was open—daytime hours. That, too, made me uneasy. Darkness felt like an excuse. Daylight felt like accountability.

Ash was already inside the facility when I found him. He stood in the corridor between units, hands in his hoodie pocket, head tilted as if listening to the building breathe. No cigarette. A small mercy or a warning.

“You’re late,” he said as I approached.

“I’m early.”

He glanced at my face, then away. “Same thing.”

He led me down the corridor without explaining. The concrete floor was painted grey and scuffed by dollies. Each unit door had a lock. Each lock was a promise that what was behind it belonged to someone.

Halfway down the hallway, he stopped at unit 218.

He didn’t pull out a key right away. He just stared at the lock as if waiting for it to speak first.

“You’re nervous,” I said.

He laughed softly. “No.”

It was a lie so clean it almost convinced me. Then his hand came out of his pocket holding a key ring. He chose a key, slid it into the padlock, and turned.

The lock clicked open with a sound like a throat giving up.

Ash swung the unit door up. The metal rattled. Light spilled into the small space.

Inside: not furniture, not holiday decorations. A folding chair. A cheap table. Plastic bins stacked like a sloppy altar. A shoebox on the table. A paper bag with handles, the kind you got from a pharmacy.

This wasn’t storage. It was a staging area.

He stepped aside to let me see.

“Welcome,” he said. “To the part of the city that doesn’t make the news.”

I walked in slowly, hands empty and visible. Every instinct I had told me to treat the bins like a bomb.

Ash reached past me and pulled the shoebox closer. His fingertips lingered on the cardboard as if he was touching something fragile.

“What is this?” I asked.

He looked up at me. Under the fluorescent lights, his face was too honest. Shadows were a mercy; this lighting offered none.

“This,” he said, “is why Nico didn’t come home.”

He opened the shoebox.

The first thing I saw was a Polaroid—glossy, curled at the corners. A man in a suit, older, smiling too wide, arm around someone whose face was turned away. The background was unmistakable: Eclipse’s back room, the one with the velvet curtains and the broken chandelier. The photo had a date scrawled on the bottom.

Ash flipped to the next. A woman in a city uniform leaning against the bar, eyes unfocused, a hand on the thigh of someone off-frame. Date. Initials. Next photo: a judge I recognized from the news—public smile, private hands on someone’s waist in a bathroom mirror. Next: a cop, one I’d seen outside Eclipse before, exchanging cash with a kid who couldn’t have been more than nineteen.

My throat tightened.

“These aren’t crimes,” I said, though my voice didn’t believe it. “Some of them.”

“Some are,” Ash replied. He sifted through the photos with a kind of practiced contempt, like he’d been forced to handle this evidence too many times. “Some are just… leverage.”

He pulled out a notebook—different from Nico’s. This one was thicker, lined paper, handwriting neat and consistent. Names, dates, places. Amounts. Notes: “inspection delayed,” “complaint withdrawn,” “permit issue expedited.” Every line connected intimacy to bureaucracy like it was the same currency.

I took the notebook from him without asking. The paper was cold.

“You said you didn’t know what Nico found,” I said.

He shrugged, and it was almost careless, except his eyes didn’t match. “I knew the shape of it.”

I flipped a page. There were initials that matched Nico’s notebook, but this handwriting wasn’t Nico’s. This was someone who did this for a living. Someone who liked organization.

“This is a blackmail ledger,” I said.

Ash’s mouth twisted. “Call it whatever makes you feel smart.”

I looked up sharply. “Don’t do that.”

His gaze held mine. For a second I saw something like shame there, quickly buried under irritation.

“Sorry,” he said, and the word sounded unused.

On the table beside the shoebox sat three cheap audio recorders, the kind you could buy at a drugstore. Ash picked one up and held it out.

“Listen,” he said.

I took it, thumbed the play button.

Static, then a voice, low and controlled: a man speaking like he was explaining policy. “We’re not shutting you down. We’re offering you a path forward. Compliance. Cooperation.”

Another voice, younger, rough with anger. Nico.

“I’m not cooperating with extortion,” they said. “You don’t get to hold our home hostage because you want a parking garage.”

A small sound—someone laughing quietly.

Then the man again, indulgent. “This isn’t about you. This is about the neighborhood. About safety. About—”

“About money,” Nico snapped. “About you and Rusk and—”

A sharp noise. Someone grabbing something. The recorder jostled, the audio spiking into distortion.

Nico’s voice returned, breathless. “Don’t touch me.”

The recording ended abruptly.

My hands had tightened around the recorder so hard my knuckles ached. I let it go quiet in my palm. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead like an indifferent god.

“You have Nico,” I said, too fast. Too direct.

Ash’s jaw clenched. “No.”

“Then who recorded that?”

His gaze dropped to the floor. “Nico did. Or someone close. They left it here.”

“In a storage unit,” I said. “With blackmail photos.”

“Yeah.” Ash looked up, eyes sharp again. “Because Nico was building insurance. Because they thought evidence was a shield.”

I thought of what Lorna had said. *They asked about inspections. Like they were building a case.* I thought of the kid in the waiting room whispering *They’re gonna shut it down* like it was a death sentence.

“If this gets out,” I said slowly, flipping through the photos again, “it burns everyone. Not just Rusk.”

Ash’s mouth went tight. “Now you’re getting it.”

I set the photos down carefully. In one of them, a closeted city councilman—married, three kids, brand-new teeth—kissed a man in the shadow of Eclipse’s stage. If this went public, the councilman would suffer, sure. But so would the man he kissed. Whoever he was. Some people had lawyers and PR firms. Some people had just a job they couldn’t afford to lose and a landlord they couldn’t afford to anger.

“This is poison,” I said.

“It’s leverage,” Ash corrected, and the way he said it made me understand he’d had to learn the difference to survive.

I heard footsteps in the corridor outside the unit. Slow, casual. Then a cart rolling past, squeak-squeak-squeak. A woman’s voice on a phone, complaining about boxes.

My body didn’t care that it was probably nothing. My body remembered what it felt like when the wrong footsteps meant violence.

Ash’s head snapped toward the corridor. His shoulders went rigid. His eyes widened slightly, the charm dropping away completely.

“Someone’s here,” he said.

“It’s daytime,” I whispered back. “There are people everywhere.”

He shook his head once, small, emphatic. “Not like this.”

He moved quickly, scooping the ledger and a fistful of Polaroids into the pharmacy bag. The shoebox went back into a bin. He shoved the bins deeper into the unit, stacking them like a barrier.

“Help,” he hissed.

I grabbed the folding chair and table, folded them, leaned them against the back wall. It was useless, almost absurd—like tidying could hide a crime. But hands needed something to do. Mine were already shaking.

The footsteps stopped outside our unit.

A shadow fell across the threshold.

Ash killed the overhead light, plunging us into dimness. The corridor light still spilled in, making the edges of the unit glow.

He caught my wrist and pulled me back, deeper into the unit behind the bins. His hand was warm and firm, thumb pressing against the pulse point like he could quiet it by force.

We crouched. Dust tickled my nose.

In the silence, I could smell him—citrus cleaner, smoke embedded in fabric, the faint medicinal sweetness of cough drops. I could also smell my own fear, metallic and sour.

The shadow shifted.

A man’s voice drifted down the corridor. “Yeah. Two eighteen. That’s what she said.” Then, closer, “No, I don’t know. Just check.”

My stomach dropped. Two eighteen.

Ash’s grip tightened on my wrist. His eyes met mine in the dim. They were wide and furious and—underneath—panicked.

He mouthed, *Don’t move.*

The unit door next to ours rattled as someone tested a lock. Not ours—218’s neighbor. Then a curse. “Locked.”

Footsteps moved closer. A pause at our threshold.

I held my breath until my lungs burned.

Then the voice again, bored now. “It’s nothing. Probably moved it.” A second voice, quieter, irritated: “Rusk wants it found.”

Rusk wants it found.

The words hit like a fist to the chest. Not because they were surprising, but because they made everything suddenly real: this wasn’t paranoia. This was a search.

Ash’s eyes flashed at the mention of the name. He looked like he might lunge out of the unit and do something stupid just to prove he wasn’t afraid.

Instead, he did something worse.

He leaned in close to my ear, his lips almost brushing my skin, and whispered, “If they open this door, you run.”

“Like hell,” I whispered back.

His breath hitched. “Mara.”

Hearing my name in his voice did something to me. It made me want to be brave and also made me aware of the exact ways I wasn’t.

The footsteps shifted again, then began moving away. The cart squeaked. The voices receded.

Ash stayed still for a beat longer than necessary, like he didn’t trust the world. Then he let go of my wrist.

The place where his thumb had pressed against my pulse felt exposed.

We stood in the dim unit, both breathing too hard, like we’d done something physical. Like we’d been running.

Ash flicked his lighter once, not to light a cigarette—just to see the flame. It flared, reflected in his eyes.

He snapped it shut again, and the darkness returned.

“You see why I didn’t want you in it?” he said quietly.

I swallowed. My mouth was dry. “You brought me in.”

He smiled, thin. “Yeah,” he said. “Bad idea.”

I looked at the pharmacy bag in his hand. The ledger. The Polaroids. The recorder with Nico’s voice trapped inside like an insect in amber.

“This isn’t something we can just dump online,” I said.

“I know,” he replied, and there was a bitterness in it like he’d watched other people make that mistake and call it justice.

I stepped closer without meaning to. The distance between us in the unit felt suddenly charged, intimate in a way I hadn’t consented to. My body kept trying to turn fear into something it could use.

Ash’s gaze dropped to my mouth, then back to my eyes, like last night. Like it was a habit. Like he was bracing.

“Do you smoke?” he asked abruptly.

“No,” I said.

He nodded, as if confirming a theory. “Good.”

“What?”

He shrugged, not looking away. “Smoke sticks. It tells on you.”

I stared at him, at the smudged shadows under his eyes, the tension in his jaw, the way his hands never stopped moving. He looked like a person who had learned to live with tells because hiding them took too much energy.

“You’re shaking,” I said.

He scoffed. “You’re shaking.”

We were both shaking. We were both pretending not to notice.

Outside, daylight continued. Somewhere, a dog barked again, muffled. A car alarm chirped and cut off. The world kept moving like nothing was happening in unit 218.

I reached out and took the pharmacy bag from his hand. Not snatching. Not demanding. Just taking weight so he didn’t have to hold it alone.

For a second, his fingers stayed on the handle with mine, reluctant to let go. Then he did, and his hand hovered in the air like it didn’t know what to do without an object.

“You’re sure you want it?” he asked, voice quieter.

“No,” I said honestly. “But I’m taking it anyway.”

His mouth curved, and this time there was something almost tender in it. Almost.

“Then we’re both screwed,” he said.

I should have laughed. I should have stepped back. I should have told him to leave, to go smoke somewhere far away from my life.

Instead, in the dim fluorescent aftermath of fear, I leaned in and kissed him.

It wasn’t smooth. My teeth bumped his, and he made a startled sound against my mouth—half laugh, half breath. He tasted like menthol and stress and something bitter. He hesitated, then kissed me back like he’d decided to be reckless.

When we pulled apart, my heart was hammering harder than it had when Rusk’s people were outside the unit.

Ash stared at me like I’d just handed him a loaded weapon.

“Bad idea,” he whispered.

“Yeah,” I said, my voice rough. “I know.”

And outside, the city kept working on its beige primer, painting over the parts of itself that didn’t fit the renderings.

### Chapter 5 — The Worst Kind of Protection

I woke up with the taste of menthol in my mouth and panic on my tongue.

For a few seconds I didn’t know where I was. My own bedroom looked unfamiliar in the thin morning light—unmade bed, blinds half-bent, a stack of mail on the dresser like a threat. My phone lay face-down on the nightstand. When I reached for it, my hand came up empty and I felt that brief, stupid vertigo that comes when your brain expects an object and the world refuses.

Then I saw it. The phone was on the floor beside the bed, screen cracked a little more than it had been yesterday.

A memory surfaced: Ash leaving the storage facility with his hood up, no cigarette, jaw tight. Me getting back in my car with the pharmacy bag on the passenger seat like a live animal. The kiss in the dark unit—too human, too wrong, too electric. The way he’d looked at me afterward, like he didn’t know if he wanted to run or stay.

I sat up fast. The room swam. I swung my legs over the edge of the bed, feet finding the cold floor.

The pharmacy bag was gone.

My breath stopped. I scanned the room like the bag might be hiding under the bed. The table by the window. The coat hook. My kitchen counter, visible through the doorway. Nothing. My throat tightened so hard it hurt.

I got up and tore through my apartment with the kind of frantic efficiency you only get when you’re terrified and furious. I checked the closet. The bathroom. Under the couch cushions. Behind the cereal boxes. The bag hadn’t grown legs. Someone had taken it.

There were no signs of forced entry. No splintered lock, no pry marks. My door was old and temperamental; it whined when you opened it wrong. If someone had kicked it, the whole building would’ve heard.

Which meant the possibilities narrowed quickly to the kind that made me feel sick.

I’d brought Ash into my car. I’d let him see where I lived. I’d kissed him like it didn’t matter that his whole life was built on disappearing.

I stood in my kitchen in bare feet, staring at the empty counter where I’d set the bag down last night. The air smelled like my cheap dish soap and the lingering smoke in my coat. In the sink, a single mug sat with a ring of coffee dried at the bottom. Evidence of a routine I didn’t deserve.

My phone buzzed in my hand.

A calendar alert: CONTINUING ED DEADLINE. Another reminder dressed up as bureaucracy.

Then, almost immediately, another buzz. A text from Jay.

**any news? police came by. asked about nico. i told them i hired you. sorry.**

My stomach dropped again. Police coming by Jay’s place meant someone wanted to control the narrative. It meant Halden’s “advice” had teeth.

I typed back: **don’t apologize. stay home. don’t talk to anyone else.**

I didn’t add *including me* because it felt cruel and also because I couldn’t afford to cut myself off from the client. Not yet.

I threw on jeans and a clean shirt and my coat—the one that still smelled faintly like cigarettes no matter what I did—and went out into the morning.

Eclipse was quiet at eleven. The front windows were dark. A man in a city vest stood on the sidewalk across from it, clipboard in hand, staring at the building like he was measuring where to insert the knife. He didn’t look up as I passed.

Inside, the club smelled like it always did in daylight: citrus cleaner, stale beer, the ghost of perfume. Rory was behind the bar counting receipts with the grim devotion of someone practicing for grief.

KJ stood by the door, arms crossed, gaze sharp.

Rory looked up when I approached. “You again.”

“I need to talk to Ash,” I said.

Rory’s expression tightened. “Why?”

“Because he took something from me.”

KJ’s eyebrows lifted slightly. Rory’s gaze flicked to them, then back to me.

“You let him near your place?” Rory asked, tone flat.

I didn’t answer. Answering would make it real in a way I couldn’t afford yet.

Rory slapped the receipts down. “Ash hasn’t been here.”

“Don’t lie to me,” I said, too sharp, and immediately regretted it. Rory didn’t owe me honesty. I was the outsider with the badge-adjacent posture.

Rory’s eyes went cold. “I’m not lying. I haven’t seen him since last night.”

KJ spoke for the first time, voice low. “We had a patrol car circle twice this morning. Not our usual. Someone’s leaning on the block.”

“Rusk,” I said.

Rory’s jaw worked. “We don’t say that name in here like it’s a curse that can’t hear you.”

I leaned closer, keeping my voice down. “Do you know where Ash goes when he’s not here?”

Rory stared at me for a moment, then shook their head once. “Ash goes nowhere. That’s the problem. He disappears inside the city like smoke.”

My hands clenched around the bar edge. “He has the ledger.”

Rory’s face went very still. “What ledger?”

I hesitated. I could see the math running behind Rory’s eyes—hearing the word, calculating risk, imagining headlines, imagining a shut-down notice served with a smile.

I didn’t give them the details. I couldn’t. Not without endangering every person in those Polaroids whose faces I hadn’t even learned yet.

“It’s evidence,” I said, and heard how thin it sounded.

Rory’s gaze sharpened. “Evidence of what?”

“Of why the permit hearing matters,” I said. “Of leverage. Of coercion.”

KJ’s expression hardened. “That kind of thing gets people hurt.”

“Yes,” I said. “That’s why I need it back.”

Rory’s voice was quiet, dangerous. “If you bring that kind of heat here—”

“I know,” I cut in, harsher than I meant. “I’m not trying to burn your club down. I’m trying to find Nico.”

At Nico’s name, Rory’s eyes flickered. KJ looked away, as if watching the door.

Rory sighed, a sound like a tired hinge. “Go,” they said. “Before you make it worse.”

“I already did,” I said, and walked out before my voice could crack.

Outside, my phone buzzed again. This time it was a number I didn’t recognize. I didn’t answer. It called twice more, then stopped.

My hands were shaking as I drove. Not from fear of being followed—though that was there too—but from rage at the way my own choices kept tightening the net around me. The city didn’t need to chase you. It just waited for you to trap yourself.

I went back to my apartment building, checked my door. No scratches. No signs. Nothing.

Inside, the absence of the pharmacy bag was a physical wound. I kept turning my head toward the counter, like my eyes could make it reappear through stubbornness.

On the kitchen floor, near the trash can, something caught my attention: a smear of ash. Grey dust. Tiny, easily overlooked.

I crouched, rubbed my thumb over it. It stained my skin.

Ash.

He’d been here. He’d stood in my kitchen and smoked or shaken ash from his sleeve or from the ghost of a cigarette he wasn’t supposed to be having. He’d taken the bag and left me only residue.

My phone buzzed again—this time a text, unknown number.

**stop looking. you’re not built for what happens next.**

My throat went tight. It wasn’t Halden’s style. Halden would’ve put his name on it, would’ve enjoyed it. This was vaguer, meaner in a different way.

I typed back: **who is this?**

No response.

A minute later: another text.

**if you go to the hearing with this, eclipse closes. people get hurt. nico stays gone.**

My stomach rolled. Threat, prediction, or both.

I paced my apartment, chewing the inside of my cheek until I tasted blood. The tell I hated. My body giving away what my face refused.

I thought of Nico’s voice on the recorder—*Don’t touch me.* I thought of Lorna’s waiting room. Of the kid whispering *They’re gonna shut it down* like it was apocalypse. I thought of Ash in the storage unit whispering *If they open this door, you run.*

And then I thought of Ash’s lighter, the flame he’d watched without smoking, like the idea of combustion had comforted him.

He wasn’t built for safety. He was built for escape routes.

My phone buzzed again, this time from Tessa Kline.

**heard a rumor about Eclipse. you poking the bear again?**

Of course she had. The city was a mouth; it chewed everything and spit it back out into gossip.

I stared at her text until the screen dimmed.

Then I called her.

She answered on the second ring. “Mara.”

“I need you,” I said, and hated how it sounded.

Tessa exhaled. “That’s not how you usually start.”

“I’m serious.”

“I know,” she said. “That’s why I’m already tired.”

I closed my eyes. “Nico Araya is missing. There’s evidence tied to Rusk. Real evidence. Not just vibes.”

A pause. “Don’t say his name like that on a phone,” she snapped. Then, softer, “What kind of evidence?”

“The kind that could get people outed,” I said. “The kind that could ruin the wrong lives.”

Silence. I could hear traffic behind her, a horn, footsteps. She was walking, always walking.

“You don’t have it,” she said finally, not a question.

“No,” I admitted. “It was taken.”

“Taken by who?”

I swallowed. Saying his name out loud felt like admitting I’d made a mistake I couldn’t undo. “Ash Serrano.”

Tessa made a sound. “Jesus. Your taste hasn’t improved.”

“Don’t,” I said, too sharp. “He’s—”

“He’s trouble,” she finished. “And he’s not stupid. If he took it, he had a reason.”

“His reasons don’t help me,” I said.

“They might,” Tessa replied. “If his reason is ‘not getting everyone killed.’”

I stared at my kitchen counter, empty. “What do I do?”

Tessa was quiet for a beat, and when she spoke her voice was careful, the tone she used when she didn’t want her words to be used against her later.

“You don’t dump it,” she said. “Not if it’s what you’re implying. You don’t become the weapon you’re trying to disarm.”

“I’m not trying to dump it,” I said. “I’m trying to find Nico.”

“And you think the evidence finds Nico,” she said. “Maybe. Or maybe it just finds a grave.”

The phone line hummed between us like an argument neither of us wanted to win.

“Call me when you have something I can verify,” Tessa said. “And Mara?”

“Yeah.”

Her voice softened, just slightly. “Don’t do this alone.”

I almost laughed. The irony sat heavy in my chest.

When I hung up, my apartment felt smaller. The air felt used up.

I sat on the edge of my bed and stared at the smear of ash on my thumb until it blurred.

Then I did the thing I’d been avoiding because it made me feel too much: I went looking for Ash in the places he would be if he didn’t want to be found.

Not in the club. Not in the alley. Not in any place with a door someone else could control.

I drove to the discount grocery store on Sixth, the one open twenty-four hours with the harsh lights and the cheap cough medicine locked behind glass. Because Ash had told me once, without telling me, that he bought his vices and his remedies in the same aisle.

He was there, under fluorescent lights that made him look too young and too tired, holding a box of nicotine patches in one hand and a bag of cough drops in the other.

He saw me at the same moment I saw him.

And the way his face went carefully blank told me everything before he opened his mouth.

“You’re going to make a scene?” he asked.

“Where is it?” I said.

His gaze flicked to the security camera above aisle six, then back to me. “Not here.”

“You took it from my apartment,” I hissed.

“I borrowed it,” he corrected, and his calm was the most frightening thing about him. “And if you’re smart, you’ll let me keep it.”

My hands clenched at my sides. “Smart for who?”

He looked at the nicotine patches like they might answer.

“Smart for Eclipse,” he said finally. “Smart for Nico. Smart for you.”

“And what about smart for you?” I demanded.

His mouth tightened. “That too.”

I took a step closer. The air smelled like detergent and plastic and his smoke trapped in fabric. “You don’t get to decide what’s smart for me.”

His eyes flashed, sudden anger. “And you don’t get to decide you can fix this by being righteous.”

“I’m not righteous,” I snapped. “I’m just—” I stopped. Because I didn’t know what I was, in this moment, except furious and scared and wanting.

Ash’s voice dropped, hard. “You want Nico back? You want Eclipse open? Then you do what works. Not what’s clean.”

He shoved the nicotine patches into his basket. “There’s a hearing,” he said. “Paperwork. A room full of people pretending they don’t hate you. You want to win? You use leverage.”

“You mean blackmail,” I said.

He stared at me. Under the fluorescent lights, his eyes looked almost grey. “I mean survival,” he said.

And that was the worst kind of protection: the kind that asked you to become what you were fighting.

I opened my mouth to say something sharp, something that would hurt him the way he’d hurt me.

Instead, because I am not as in control as I like to think, I said the thing that fell out of me like truth.

“You stole from me,” I whispered.

Ash flinched—just barely. His jaw tightened. “I warned you,” he said. “Smoke sticks. It tells on you.”

Then he turned his cart and walked away, leaving me standing in the aisle with my hands shaking and the knowledge that I would follow him anyway.
